source.title: Introduce telemetry-driven synthetic load generator and hyperops for batch profile autotuning
source.author: Sofia Morales
source.description: Problem: Production telemetry indicates wide variance in per-customer request shape (seq lengths, prompt/templates, temperature) which makes static batching defaults suboptimal. We need a way to synthesize representative load from existing traces, run lightweight microbenchmark passes, and generate conservative batch/cache/quantization profiles that are safe for rollout. This PR implements a telemetry-driven 'synthetic-econ-proxy' tool plus runtime hyperops that drive an autotuner. Goals: 1) generate reproducible synthetic streams from sampled traces, 2) run a fast hyperparameter sweep for batch sizes, microbatch windows, and cache TTLs in a sandbox mode, 3) attach a compact 'handoff' metadata blob to candidate profiles for safe staged rollout and automatic rollback. Key features: - synthetic-econ-proxy CLI: consumes sampled telemetry (p50/p95 seq distribution, client headers, model family) and emits a deterministic request stream for local or staged perf runs. - hyperops runner: lightweight orchestrator that runs short trials across candidate batch/microbatch/kv-cache policies and collects cost/latency metrics. - profile handoff format: single-line compact metadata (versioned tag, checksum, compatibility hints, suggested fallback) used by the runtime to decide compatible rollouts. - safety: candidate profiles are generated with conservative SLO buffers and include automatic fallback to prior profile on SLA regressions. - developer UX: preview command (redwoodctl perf preview --profile <file>) that replays the synthetic stream against a chosen model variant and prints unit economics estimates. Motivation and context: This is an engineering-first approach to reduce human iteration when choosing defaults for new customers or workloads. Rather than relying on a single scoring heuristic, we synthesize a workload closest to the customer's telemetry and run a controlled sweep to find economically sensible defaults. See linked Linear items and test harness in the commits. Checklist: - [x] synthetic stream generator - [x] hyperops runner and metric collectors - [x] compact profile handoff format and compatibility checks - [x] redwoodctl preview command + CLI docs - [x] unit and integration tests - [x] e2e smoke runs on staging - [x] changelog/release notes Comments: This PR purposefully keeps quantization changes limited to attaching quant 'hints' to profiles rather than forcing conversion; quantization conversions remain an explicit operator action. The approach avoids large runtime changes and hooks into existing rollout machinery.
commits: 0a8f3d8  add synthetic-econ-proxy CLI and parsing for sampled telemetry
3b2c4e1  implement hyperops runner and short-sweep strategy
7f4b9aa  add profile handoff schema + checksum validation
b9c2a6f  redwoodctl perf preview integration and CLI docs
d4a71c2  add unit tests for generators and safety rollback path
f1e9bde  adjust CI workflow to include perf-sim smoke in staging
source.review_thread:
Reviewer: Asha Patel — Nice work sketching the generator. Can we ensure deterministic seeding across environments?
Author: Sofia Morales — Yes, synthetic-econ-proxy accepts an explicit --seed flag; CI uses a fixed seed for staging smoke runs.
Reviewer: Liam Nguyen — The sweep looks heavy; can we cap the default to 3 short runs per candidate? Also needs an estimate of wall-clock time.
Author: Sofia Morales — Done: hyperops default sweep depth is 3 (configurable), typical run < 4 minutes per candidate on staging GPUs; added docs.
Reviewer: Marina Soto — Please ensure profile handoffs are backward compatible with existing rollout API.
Author: Sofia Morales — Added version tag and compatibility hints; runtime will ignore unknown fields and validate checksum.
Reviewer: Ethan Park — CI failure on first run (lint) — fixed formatting in hyperops/runner.py.
Approvals: Asha Patel approved, Liam Nguyen requested a small follow-up which was addressed. Final: LGTM from reviewers, merging with squash.
source.ci_checks: staging-lint: pass; unit-tests: pass; integration-smoke: pass; perf-smoke (staging, non-blocking): pass; security-scan: pass
source.release_notes: Adds a telemetry-driven synthetic workload generator and an autotuner that produces conservative batching/cache profiles with preview tooling. No forced quant conversions; quant hints attached to profiles.

source.title: Self-healing traffic gate with prognostic rollback scheduler
source.author: Priya Suresh
source.description: Motivation: We observed incident patterns where a small regression in an A/B arm degraded user experience slowly and only surfaced after sustained traffic. This PR adds a prognostic rollback scheduler and a self-healing traffic gate that combines short-horizon telemetry forecasting, a conservative anomaly scorer, and lightweight replay/bisect workflows so the system can proactively hold or revert risky promotes before widespread impact.  Summary of changes: implemented a prognostic score pipeline that consumes recent SLO/time-series windows and outputs a short-term risk forecast; added TrafficGate controller that enforces holdpoints when forecasted risk crosses policy thresholds; built a rescue-oracle that can run fast replay of recent 1% samples in a sandbox to validate rollback decisions; added operator-visible "rescue" and "replay" actions to Redwood Console so on-call can review diagnostics and trigger manual rescue if needed; introduced feature-flagging and gradual enablement config under rollout.policy.prognostic.enabled=false by default.  Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-4421) [x] unit + integration tests for forecast scorer [x] smoke tests for traffic gate hold/open actions [x] e2e replay against local-capture harness [x] console UI surfaced and smoke validated  Implementation notes: forecasting uses a lightweight ARIMA-like exponential smoother tuned for short windows to avoid model drift; anomaly scorer penalizes latency regressions higher than minor fidelity deltas; the rescue-oracle runs isolated synchronous replay (max 30s) and returns a scalar pass/fail + diff snapshot used to justify automated rollback.  Rollout plan: behind feature flag in main, enable in staging for 48h with canary percent=2% then roll to 10% with manual checks; enable prognostic auto-hold only after 7d stable metrics.  Related: supports both canary and A/B lanes and hooks into existing rollout audit logs and snapshotting.  Why different: unlike prior reactive rollback controllers, this one uses short-term prognostic signals and a bounded replay verification step to reduce false-positive auto-rollbacks.  Links: Linear ENG-4421 (design), SUP-1723 (support ticket that motivated fast replay).
commits: a1f3b9f  Implement prognostic scorer and unit tests
b7d2e41  Add TrafficGate controller and policy plumbing
c4e9f12  Integrate rescue-oracle sandbox replay harness
d5a0ab3  Console: surface rescue/replay buttons and diagnostics panel
e2d7c99  Add feature flag and rollout docs
review_comments: Diego Morales: Nice approach on the short-horizon forecasting. Concern: false positives may still trigger a hold. Can we add an entropy-based cooldown so multiple non-overlapping anomalies are required before auto-hold?
Priya Suresh: Added entropy cooldown in the policy (policy_schema.yaml) with conservative defaults and tests showing reduced false positives. See commit b7d2e41.

Ava Chen: Please add a simulation that replays historical incidents to prove the prognostic thresholds reduce incident blast radius. Also request: metric tags should be consistent with observability conventions.
Priya Suresh: Added historical incident simulation harness and adjusted tags to use service.namespace and rollout.id. See test/integration/replay_harness_test.go and telemetry changes.
Liam O'Neill: Minor nit: console button copy should say 'Request rescue replay' not 'Rescue'. Fixed. Also add a guard so replay cannot run for >30s to protect hosts.
Priya Suresh: Addressed both nits; replay timeout enforced in replay_oracle.go and UI copy updated.
source.release_notes: Added prognostic rollback scheduler and self-healing traffic gate with conservative defaults; feature-flagged under rollout.policy.prognostic.enabled; Console now shows 'Request rescue replay' action for operator verification.
source.post_merge_tasks: - Monitor ENG-4421 canary dashboard for false positives for 14 days. - Add policy presets for enterprise customers (follow-up ENG-4488). - Announce feature-flag rollout and ops runbook.
source.notes_for_release: This is opt-in via rollout.policy.prognostic.enabled=false. Recommend staged enablement per PR rollout plan. No breaking changes.
source.incident_reference: SUP-1723: customer reported slow-degrading A/B arm that wasn't caught by threshold-based alerts; motivated replay harness development.

source.title: per-request graceful-degradation: kernel compatibility shim + precision-hint API
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description:
Motivation: We continue to see tail latency regressions in mixed-precision kernels when models with large kv-cache footprints are routed through the 'fast' execution path. This PR introduces a lightweight compatibility shim and a per-request 'precision hint' header that lets the router/scheduler choose a conservative execution mode for requests that prioritize correctness over absolute token throughput.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a kernel-compat shim layer in the runtime that detects binary incompatibility or quantization sensitivity for a model+request combination and exposes a low-overhead boolean that the scheduler uses to avoid aggressive fusion/quantization fast-paths; implements an opt-in HTTP header (X-Redwood-Precision-Hint: prefer-correct | prefer-fast | neutral) and SDK flags that map to the same hint; extends continuous-batching logic to respect per-request hints by routing hinted requests to a separate lightweight queue that favors conservative kernels and smaller batch coalescing; introduces a KV-cache safety check that prevents prefix-optimized eviction heuristics from running for hinted requests; updates the metrics to surface how many requests used the compatibility shim and the delta in p50/p95 latency and token cost; includes tests and performance microbenchmarks.

Design notes and trade-offs: The shim is intentionally minimal: it does not change model weights or quantize on the fly. Instead it flips kernel-selection heuristics (no aggressive fused attention kernels, prefer fp16-safe paths, disable some prefetch transforms) and reduces batch coalescing windows to reduce peak memory pressure on hinted requests. This increases per-token cost but avoids correctness edge-cases introduced by certain fused kernels (observed in edge-case tests with malformed KV cache shapes). We chose a header + SDK-first API because it is the least invasive while enabling customers and platform teams to opt-in per-call.

Migration and rollout: The feature is behind a server-side flag (runtime.enable_precision_hint_shim). Default is off for all clusters; enabled in staging during canary; default for some internal teams with model reg tests. Rollout plan attached in the checklist.

Checklist: - [x] shim implementation and unit tests - [x] SDK header plumbing (python + ts) - [x] scheduler queue change and e2e tests - [x] add metrics and dashboard panels - [x] perf microbenchmarks (attached) - [x] rollout gating flag and docs

Related: Linear: ENG-4280, ENG-4297. Incident: SUP-1223 (customer reported hallucination/regression after aggressive kernel upgrade). More context: see internal doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4280/precision-hint-design (internal link).

Notes for reviewers: focus on runtime/shim correctness (no changed behavior for default-off), scheduler behavior (deadlocks or starvation between two queues), and SDK ergonomics. The PR intentionally avoids changing model catalog metadata until we validate metrics from canary.

Release notes: Adds an opt-in per-request precision hint header and a runtime compatibility shim to favor correctness over fused-kernel throughput.
commits: a3f9d2c: add kernel compatibility shim and detection hooks
b7e44f1: expose X-Redwood-Precision-Hint header and SDK plumbing (py/ts)
c1d34b7: scheduler: separate hinted-request queue and adjust coalescing windows
d9f2e6a: kv-cache: add safety check to skip eviction heuristics for hinted requests
f4b8a12: metrics: shim usage, hinted-queue latency, token_cost deltas
e0c3a9b: tests: unit + e2e + perf microbench harness and fixtures
ff12cde: docs: runtime flag, header docs, rollout checklist
changed_files: runtime/compat/shim.go - core detection and shim boolean export
runtime/scheduler/queue_manager.go - hinted queue implementation and coalescing windows
runtime/kvcache/eviction.go - safety gating for hinted requests
sdks/python/redwood/request.py - header plumbing and high-level API
sdks/typescript/src/request.ts - header plumbing and helper enum
metrics/dashboards/hinted_requests.pan - new panels for shim usage and cost deltas
review_thread: Lina Gomez: Left a comment on runtime/compat/shim.go - can we make the detection deterministic across restarts? Reply: Ethan Park: shim maintains a strict serialization of detection state; added a small on-disk marker and unit test.

Marcus Patel: Scheduler change looks OK but I'm worried about hinted-queue starvation under heavy hinted load. Reply: Ethan Park: added metrics and a low-priority backpressure channel which will allow hinted queue to borrow capacity from default when overall latency SLOs are in danger.
Ava Chen: SDK surface is slightly wordy; can we provide helper enum docs? Reply: Ethan Park: simplified the TS/PY helpers and updated api/headers.md with examples.
QA Bot: e2e hinted_queue_test failing intermittently on CI. Reply: Ethan Park: fixed a race in the test harness; re-ran CI and tests passed.
source.release_notes: Opt-in per-request precision hint header (X-Redwood-Precision-Hint) and runtime compatibility shim for correctness-oriented execution. Disabled by default; enable via runtime.enable_precision_hint_shim for canary.

source.title: Probabilistic length cohorter with pad-aware scheduling and KV-prefill heuristics
source.author: Samir Rao
source.description:
Motivation: long-tail sequence length variance continues to drive excessive padding and suboptimal kernel utilization in our continuous batching pipeline. Simple bucketing reduces padding but creates brittle boundaries and poor utilization when per-slot latency SLOs are tight. This PR introduces a probabilistic length cohorter that (1) forms soft cohorts by sampling length-affinity probabilities instead of hard buckets, (2) attaches a pad-cost estimate to each pending request using a small learned linear cost model, and (3) integrates KV-prefill priority to bias cohorts toward requests that benefit from warm KV caches. The goal is to reduce aggregate padding waste while keeping P95 latency within SLO and increasing kernel lane alignment for improved throughput.

Approach: the cohorter computes a length-affinity vector for each request based on requested max_tokens, recent sequence length distribution from the request's token histogram, and a lightweight token-density predictor. Requests are then assigned to cohorts probabilistically (tempered softmax) rather than deterministically; the cohort scheduler runs an acceptance policy that weighs cohort fill-level against estimated pad cost and KV-prefill benefit. The pad-cost estimator is a tiny ridge-regression model trained on our production trace snapshots (serialized model included under benchmarks/). KV-prefill heuristics mark requests with hot-key overlap to get a small scheduling boost so that cohorts end up with multiple warm-KV requests more often, reducing expensive cold-KV misses during generation. Integration points: runtime/batching/cohorter, runtime/scheduler/policy, runtime/kv/prefill, and a new bench harness under benchmarks/batch-sim.

Results: microbenchmarks on a 4xA100 pool and production-like trace show median pad tokens per request down 28% and average per-token GPU time improvement of 6% for multi-tenant traces. End-to-end P95 latency increased by 2% in worst-case stress tests but stayed within our target SLO for dedicated capacity. Cost-per-output-token decreased ~8% in the synthetic workload and ~3-5% on several customer traces. See benchmarks/summary.csv for full breakdown. We also measured improved kernel lane alignment (measured as contiguous lane occupancy metric) up 9% which maps to fewer kernel launches in multi-lane kernels.

Checklist: 1) unit tests for cohorter assignment and pad-cost estimator included, 2) integration test for scheduler with KV-prefill, 3) bench harness and dataset snapshot included, 4) flag guarded rollout (feature flag: probabilistic_cohorter) and runtime config knobs (temperature, pad_cost_weight, kv_prefill_boost), 5) docs RFC added. Backwards compatibility: default feature flag is OFF; old deterministic bucketer remains unchanged unless flag enabled. No API changes.

How to test locally: run 'make bench-cohorter' to reproduce microbenchmarks; run integration tests 'pytest tests/integration/test_cohorter.py::TestPrefillBias'. Deployment plan: staged rollout behind feature flag; enable for 5% of traffic on dedicated clusters for 48 hours, validate P95 and pad metrics in console dashboards; increase to 100% if stable.

Related: follows from ENG-7123 (experimental pad-cost model) and complements PRs for kv-prefill routing. Linear: ENG-8852. Notes: tuned default temperature=0.4 and pad_cost_weight=0.7 after ablations; conservative defaults chosen to avoid regressing latency-sensitive customers.
commits: c1a4d3e Add probabilistic cohorter core logic and tests
b2f7b9a Introduce pad-cost estimator, training snapshot and inference wrapper
d3e8f1c Integrate cohorter into continuous batching path and add feature flag
e7a2c45 Add KV-prefill bias hooks and scheduler acceptance policy
f9b1ae0 Bench harness and summary CSV, docs RFC and rollout notes
review_comments: Ava Chen: Overall this looks promising. Two immediate concerns: a) we need stronger guardrails for latency regression if temperature is misconfigured, b) please add a litmus test that simulates KV cold misses to ensure prefill bias doesn't starve other cohorts.

Samir Rao (author): Added an acceptance gate keyed on observed queue latency and a configurable emergency fallback that disables probabilistic placement when P95 grows > 1.15x baseline. Added an integration litmus in tests/integration/test_cohorter.py that injects artificial KV cold-miss penalties.
Miguel Ortega: Can we avoid heap allocations on the hot path in cohorter.assign()? The current sample path uses make([]float32) every assignment.
Samir Rao: Reworked sampling to use a pooled buffer from runtime/batching/pool.go and eliminated per-request allocations; bench results for tail latency improved slightly after this change.
Priya Patel: Please ensure the pad-cost model's training data is versioned and does not contain customer PII. Also add a short paragraph in the RFC about retraining cadence.
Samir Rao: Snapshot included under benchmarks/batch-sim/ with README explaining anonymization and retention; added RFC paragraph about quarterly retraining and alerts for drift.
source.release_notes: Add an opt-in probabilistic length cohorter that reduces padding waste and improves kernel lane alignment; gated by feature flag 'probabilistic_cohorter'. Includes KV-prefill heuristics and bench harness. Rolling rollout recommended.

title: Probabilistic length cohorter with pad-aware scheduling and KV-prefill heuristics
preview:
Motivation: long-tail sequence length variance continues to drive excessive padding and suboptimal kernel utilization in our continuous batching pipeline. Simple bucketing reduces padding but creates brittle boundaries and poor utilization when per-slot latency SLOs are tight. This PR introduces a probabilistic length cohorter that (1) forms soft cohorts by sampling length-affinity probabilities instead of hard buckets, (2) attaches a pad-cost estimate to each pending request using a small learned linear cost model, and (3) integrates KV-prefill priority to bias cohorts toward requests that benefit from warm KV caches. The goal is to reduce aggregate padding waste while keeping P95 latency within SLO and increasing kernel lane alignment for improved throughput.

Approach: the cohorter computes a length-affinity vector for each request based on requested max_tokens, recent sequence length distribution from the request's token histogram, and a lightweight token-density predictor. Requests are then assigned to cohorts probabilistically (tempered softmax) rather than deterministically; the cohort scheduler runs an acceptance policy that weighs cohort fill-level against estimated pad cost and KV-prefill benefit. The pad-cost estimator is a tiny ridge-regression model trained on our production trace snapshots (serialized model included under benchmarks/). KV-prefill heuristics mark requests with hot-key overlap to get a small scheduling boost so that cohorts end up with multiple warm-KV requests more often, reducing expensive cold-KV misses during generation. Integration points: runtime/batching/cohorter, runtime/scheduler/policy, runtime/kv/prefill, and a new bench harness under benchmarks/batch-sim.

Results: microbenchmarks on a 4xA100 pool and production-like trace show median pad tokens per request down 28% and average per-token GPU time improvement of 6% for multi-tenant traces. End-to-end P95 latency increased by 2% in worst-case stress tests but stayed within our target SLO for dedicated capacity. Cost-per-output-token decreased ~8% in the synthetic workload and ~3-5% on several customer traces. See benchmarks/summary.csv for full breakdown. We also measured improved kernel lane alignment (measured as contiguous lane occupancy metric) up 9% which maps to fewer kernel launches in multi-lane kernels.

Checklist: 1) unit tests for cohorter assignment and pad-cost estimator included, 2) integration test for scheduler with KV-prefill, 3) bench harness and dataset snapshot included, 4) flag guarded rollout (feature flag: probabilistic_cohorter) and runtime config knobs (temperature, pad_cost_weight, kv_prefill_boost), 5) docs RFC added. Backwards compatibility: default feature flag is OFF; old deterministic bucketer remains unchanged unless flag enabled. No API changes.

How to test locally: run 'make bench-cohorter' to reproduce microbenchmarks; run integration tests 'pytest tests/integration/test_cohorter.py::TestPrefillBias'. Deployment plan: staged rollout behind feature flag; enable for 5% of traffic on dedicated clusters for 48 hours, validate P95 and pad metrics in console dashboards; increase to 100% if stable.

Related: follows from ENG-7123 (experimental pad-cost model) and complements PRs for kv-prefill routing. Linear: ENG-8852. Notes: tuned default temperature=0.4 and pad_cost_weight=0.7 after ablations; conservative defaults chosen to avoid regressing latency-sensitive customers.

source.title: silence-probe-reconnect-backfill-and-cancel-deadline-enforcement
source.author: Ava Patel
source.description: Motivation: Customers observed client disconnects that left server-side streaming state in a limbo where tool-calls were retried and KV-cache backfills were skipped, causing higher token costs and functional duplication. This PR introduces a 'silence probe' reconciliation path, a reconnect probe that attempts lightweight session health checks on client reconnects, an explicit cancel-deadline enforcement for in-flight tool/function calls, and safer backfill semantics when reconnect resumes. Summary of changes: 1) server: add silence-probe detection that marks streams as 'candidate-orphan' after configurable idle windows and enqueues a lightweight snapshot probe before evicting; 2) reconnect: on new client handshake include reconnect-probe phase which validates KV-cache continuity and replays missing prefix tokens if safe; 3) cancel-deadline: tool invocation subsystem now tracks cancel deadlines separately from client websocket state and enforces idempotent abort semantics with explicit response code for clients; 4) backfill behavior: when resuming, we prefer prefix-reconciliation over blind backfill and record a short reconciliation trace to observability; 5) OpenAI-compat: adapter layer now maps the reconnect-probe handshake into a 409/202-style flow when applicable and preserves function-call idempotency tokens. Implementation notes: code changes touch the session manager, rpc/stream controller, toolcall executor, and the OpenAI compatibility adapter. Default timeouts are conservative (idle->candidate-orphan = 45s, probe timeout = 5s, cancel-deadline grace = 2s) with knobs exposed in config. Tests: added unit tests for probe state transitions, e2e integration test that simulates abrupt client disconnect and reconnect with toolcalls, and property tests for idempotent abort on repeated cancel signals. Safety: probe performs non-destructive validation only and will not execute user-visible toolcalls during reconciliation. Rollout plan: feature gated via runtime flag 'streaming.silence_probe_v1' with spreadsheet of customers to flip; metrics added: stream.probe.started, stream.probe.success, stream.resume.reconciled, toolcall.abort.forced; alerting suggested on probe.failures > 1% over 10m. Backwards compatibility: existing client behavior unchanged unless server flag enabled. Related: follows ENG-4821 and references earlier streaming incident INT-998. Breaking change: none. See release_notes for end-user impact.
source.commits: 3 commits: 1) add silence probe state machine and basic probes (session manager + controller) 2) implement reconnect-probe handshake and prefix reconciliation; update OpenAI adapter mapping 3) enforce cancel-deadline in toolcall executor, add tests and docs
source.changed_files: server/session/manager.go, server/stream/controller.go, server/toolcall/executor.go, adapters/openai/handshake.go, runtime/config/defaults.yaml, tests/integration/stream_reconnect_test.go, docs/streaming-silence-probe.md, telemetry/metrics.yaml, Makefile
source.review_comments: Marcus: requested tighter metrics granularity around probe latency and suggested a separate probe_failure tag for tenant-scoped indexing. Elena: found a race in session eviction path when probe returns while eviction is already scheduled; asked for a test and small lock refactor. Samir: asked for clearer mapping of reconnect flow in OpenAI-compat adapter and an explicit 409 vs 202 decision table. Author replies: added metric 'stream.probe.latency_ms' and split probe_failure tenant tag, refactored eviction locking to use session-level RW mutex and added unit test, documented decision table in adapters/openai/handshake.go and docs/streaming-silence-probe.md. Approval: Marcus approved after metrics change. Elena approved after lock fix. Samir approved after docs update.
source.release_notes: Feature gated streaming silence-probe and reconnect probe which improves resilience after abrupt client disconnects. Adds cancel-deadline enforcement for toolcalls to avoid duplicated side effects. No behavioral changes until the runtime flag is enabled.

source.title: ack-window-coalescing-and-reconnect-probe-for-resumable-streams
source.author: Maya Singh
source.description:
Context: We have had intermittent production incidents where client disconnect followed by rapid reconnects created duplicate tool/function calls or left server-side inflight work stranded. This PR introduces a lightweight client ACK window and a reconnect probe handshake to make streaming sessions more resilient to flapping connections while preserving OpenAI compatibility for client-driven cancels.

Goals:
- Allow the client to acknowledge receipt of terminal tokens (finalized structured output / function call invocation) so the server can safely drop inflight state sooner and avoid double-execution on reconnects.
- Coalesce rapid client cancel/soft-abort signals within a short ACK window to reduce noisy cancellation churn during flaky networks.
- Add a reconnect probe (small idempotency-aware handshake) that validates resume tokens and server lease state before resuming expensive work (tool calls/KV-cache continuations).
- Maintain compatibility with current OpenAI-style cancel/close semantics — no change required for most clients.

What changed (high-level):
- Protocol: add optional "ack_window_ms" header on initial stream request (default 3000ms). During the ack window the server accepts a single cancel token and will delay aggressive resource reclamation for that window; repeated cancels within the window are treated as coalesced (no new cancel events emitted to tool call layer).
- Protocol: introduce a short probe exchange during reconnect: client sends a lightweight probe with last-seen sequence id and resume_token; server responds with probe_ack and explicit lease_state (active/draining/expired). Resume proceeds only on probe_ack==ok and lease_state==active (or draining with explicit allow_resume flag).
- Server: tracking of client-ack receipts for final structured outputs. When a client ACK for a terminal event is received, the server will mark the session checkpointed and allow immediate resource reclamation without waiting for full TTL. This avoids duplicate tool invocations on quick reconnects.
- Tool/fn calling: calls are now annotated with an idempotency fingerprint (derived from call_id + resume_seq) and the runtime consults a short-lived dedupe cache keyed by that fingerprint. The cache lifetime is aligned to the ack_window + server_drain_timeout to balance dedup protection with memory usage.
- SDKs/compat: no breaking API change for typical streaming use. New optional headers are supported by Redwood's OpenAI-compat adapter; we added transparent fallbacks for clients that don't send the new fields.

Implementation notes:
- Added stream_ack.go in internal/runtime to centralize ack/coalescing logic and metrics.
- Modified openai/compat adapter to parse ack_window_ms and to run the reconnect probe flow on connection resumptions.
- Added a short-lived in-memory dedupe cache with metrics and an adaptive eviction timer. For Dedicated/Private, cache can be backed by local ephemeral Redis if configured (opt-in).

Testing and validation:
- Unit tests for ack coalescing, probe handshake, and dedupe cache eviction.
- Integration test that simulates client disconnect/reconnect flapping and asserts no duplicate tool calls and that structured output is only emitted once.
- Load test scenario: 10k streams with randomized disconnect windows to measure overhead; results show <=0.6% p99 token latency increase and 18% reduction in duplicate tool calls under flapping scenarios.

Checklist:
- [x] Design doc linked (ENG-912)
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Backwards compatible adapter changes
- [x] Metrics/instrumentation (stream.ack.* and stream.probe.*)
- [x] E2E flapping load test results attached

Rollout plan:
- Merge behind feature flag stream_ack_coalesce (off by default).
- Enable for 10% of hosted traffic for 24h; monitor stream.probe.failures and tool.duplicate.detected.
- Enable SDK opt-in examples in quickstarts after 10% ramp is healthy.

Notes/Tradeoffs:
- Acknowledgement window delays aggressive server TTL reclamation; chosen default (3s) is conservative — short enough to limit resource ties, long enough to absorb network blips.
- Dedup cache increases memory pressure slightly; adaptive eviction mitigates long tails. For Dedicated customers we provide config knobs to tune ext cache.

Related/precedent: several internal incident postmortems (SUP-428) drove this work. This complements prior resumable-handshake PRs but focuses on coalescing/cancellation semantics and an explicit probe phase to gate resume onto server lease health.

c9d2a7b - openai/compat: parse ack_window_ms and implement reconnect probe
f5d48e1 - runtime: idempotency fingerprint + dedupe cache for tool calls
9b7e2c4 - tests: unit + integration for ack/probe/dedupe
0f3a5b6 - docs: add protocol notes and rollout guidance

source.title: model-catalog: add filters, compatibility flags, and pricing/limits fields to /models API
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Summary: Adds richer filtering and capability metadata to the public model catalog endpoints. This PR introduces query filters for capability-based discovery and model attributes, plus explicit compatibility flags and first-class pricing/limits fields on model descriptors. Motivation: customers have asked for a machine-readable way to pick models by capability (e.g., tool-calling, structured output, streaming-stable) and to programmatically surface cost/limit information for routing and billing. The current /models response is sparse and requires ad-hoc parsing of descriptions in downstream services. What this changes: 1) API surface: GET /v1/models now accepts new query params: 'capability' (comma-separated values: chat, completion, embedding, tool_calling, structured_output, streaming), 'openai_compat' (true|false), 'pricing_tier' (free|standard|premium), and 'min_max_tokens' (min,max). Example: GET /v1/models?capability=tool_calling,structured_output&openai_compat=true 2) Response schema additions: each model object gains optional fields: 'capabilities' (string[]), 'compatibility_flags' (string[]; e.g. "openai_chat", "openai_completion", "openai_embeddings"), 'pricing' (object with flat_rate, per_token_input_usd, per_token_output_usd, currency), and 'limits' (object: max_tokens, max_requests_per_minute, concurrent_requests). These are optional to preserve backwards compatibility; existing clients will continue to work if they ignore new fields. 3) OpenAI compatibility adapter: adapter maps compatibility_flags to legacy OpenAI-style fields for the OpenAI compatibility layer. Added translation layer so openai_compat=true returns aliases expected by older clients. 4) Server-side indexing: index added for capability tags to keep catalog query latency within SLOs (see perf notes below). 5) Docs & SDK: OpenAPI spec updated, python/ts SDKs surface the new fields and query helpers. Compatibility/migration notes: fields are additive and optional; no required fields were changed. The only risk is downstream code that relies on the previous string parsing of model descriptions — we've added an integration test and a migration note in docs. feat(api): accept capability/openai_compat query params and wire to controller
chore(sdk): expose model.capabilities helpers in python and ts SDKs

review_comments: Priya: Please enumerate allowed capability values in the OpenAPI spec and add a small enum for compatibility_flags — this will make SDK generation more reliable.
Maya: Added enum list and OpenAPI examples in models.yaml, and added enum validation in controller.go.
Diego: For pricing fields, prefer per_token_input_usd/per_token_output_usd over input/output_rate to avoid unit confusion. Also add currency field.
Maya: Switched naming per Diego's suggestion and added currency. Also added a note to release_notes about units.
Ethan: One of the integration tests failed on first run due to a race in cache warmup; can we add a short backoff / retry in test harness?
Maya: Fixed test to wait on cache warmup signal; re-ran CI, all green.
source.release_notes: Added capability-based filtering and machine-readable model metadata: capabilities, compatibility_flags, pricing (per-token input/output), and runtime limits. These fields are optional and additive; SDKs updated to expose helpers. Operators can enable experimental rollout via feature flag 'model_catalog_caps_v1'.
files_to_review_snippets: server/catalog/controller.go: parse capability/openai_compat query params, call store.FilterByCapabilities, enrich results with pricing/limits from model registry
server/catalog/translator_openai.go: map compatibility_flags -> openai.compatibility and provide legacy aliases for openai_compat=true
source.notes_on_performance: Added an index on model_capabilities (store_sql.go + index_migration.sql) and a small in-memory prefix cache for frequent queries. Benchmark: 99th percentile query latency for capability queries across a 10k model catalog dropped from 180ms to 42ms locally. Metrics added to monitor cache hit rates and filter latency.
source.migration_instructions: Operators: ensure DB migration index_migration.sql is applied during maintenance window. If you rely on text parsing of model descriptions, switch to the structured 'capabilities' field when ready. Feature flag: model_catalog_caps_v1 toggles population of pricing/limits for existing models; new models will include fields by default.
source.user_visible_changes: Developers can now call GET /v1/models?capability=tool_calling to discover models that advertise tool calling. Responses will include pricing and limit hints to enable cost-aware routing.

title: model-catalog: add filters, compatibility flags, and pricing/limits fields to /models API
preview: Summary: Adds richer filtering and capability metadata to the public model catalog endpoints. This PR introduces query filters for capability-based discovery and model attributes, plus explicit compatibility flags and first-class pricing/limits fields on model descriptors. Motivation: customers have asked for a machine-readable way to pick models by capability (e.g., tool-calling, structured output, streaming-stable) and to programmatically surface cost/limit information for routing and billing. The current /models response is sparse and requires ad-hoc parsing of descriptions in downstream services. What this changes: 1) API surface: GET /v1/models now accepts new query params: 'capability' (comma-separated values: chat, completion, embedding, tool_calling, structured_output, streaming), 'openai_compat' (true|false), 'pricing_tier' (free|standard|premium), and 'min_max_tokens' (min,max). Example: GET /v1/models?capability=tool_calling,structured_output&openai_compat=true 2) Response schema additions: each model object gains optional fields: 'capabilities' (string[]), 'compatibility_flags' (string[]; e.g. "openai_chat", "openai_completion", "openai_embeddings"), 'pricing' (object with flat_rate, per_token_input_usd, per_token_output_usd, currency), and 'limits' (object: max_tokens, max_requests_per_minute, concurrent_requests). These are optional to preserve backwards compatibility; existing clients will continue to work if they ignore new fields. 3) OpenAI compatibility adapter: adapter maps compatibility_flags to legacy OpenAI-style fields for the OpenAI compatibility layer. Added translation layer so openai_compat=true returns aliases expected by older clients. 4) Server-side indexing: index added for capability tags to keep catalog query latency within SLOs (see perf notes below). 5) Docs & SDK: OpenAPI spec updated, python/ts SDKs surface the new fields and query helpers. Compatibility/migration notes: fields are additive and optional; no required fields were changed. The only risk is downstream code that relies on the previous string parsing of model descriptions — we've added an integration test and a migration note in docs. Rollout plan: staged rollout with feature flag 'model_catalog_caps_v1'; default enabled for beta accounts and disabled for strict compat customers. Monitoring: new metrics added: model_catalog.filter_latency_ms, model_catalog.cache_hit_rate, and pricing_field_populated_ratio. Related: Linear ENG-1729 (catalog filter work), PM-404 (pricing requirements). Checklist: - [x] API schema and OpenAPI docs updated - [x] Server implementation of filters and response enrichment - [x] OpenAI compatibility adapter translation layer - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] SDK updates (python + ts) - [x] Perf benchmark added to benchmark-lab - [x] Docs + release notes Notes: see inline snippets below for example response and query. Example response snippet (illustrative): {"id":"rw-mini-1","name":"rw-mini","capabilities":["chat","embedding"],"compatibility_flags":["openai_chat","openai_embeddings"],"pricing":{"per_token_input_usd":"0.00001","per_token_output_usd":"0.00002","currency":"USD"},"limits":{"max_tokens":"4096","max_requests_per_minute":"600","concurrent_requests":"10"}}

source.title: Introduce kernel-level heatmap and cost-flow annotator for invocation traces
source.author: Arianna Chen
source.description: Motivation: We need a low-overhead way to surface GPU/kernel-level queuing and contention hotspots correlated to token-level cost and per-stage latency. This PR adds a sampled, lightweight kernel-heatmap export and a cost-flow annotator that stitches stage timing with kernel occupancy to help SREs and runtime engineers diagnose expensive invocation patterns. Design summary: 1) A new kernel heatmap sampler emits compact histograms of kernel queue wait and execution time per-device and per-invocation-phase; sampling is prioritized for requests that exceed a configurable latency or cost threshold. 2) A cost-flow annotator postprocesses traces to attach a per-invocation-stage cost attribution vector (model load, key-value cache miss penalties, kernel stalls, token-generation time) and synthesizes rollup markers used by dashboards. 3) Minimal on-path overhead: sampler uses atomic ring-buffers and amortized aggregation at a local telemetry worker; annotation is done asynchronously by the trace aggregator to avoid adding tail latency. Implementation details: Added a new runtime probe (runtime/probes/kernel_sampler.cc) with export hooks to the observability pipeline, a proto schema (telemetry/protos/kernel_heatmap.proto) and a canonical generator for the annotator (observability/annotators/cost_flow_annotator.go). Also updated the console dashboards (dashboards/infra/kernel-heatmap-panel.json and dashboards/infra/cost-flow-explorer.json) and the SLO playbook (runbooks/kernel-contention.md). Rollout plan: feature gated behind observability.kernel_heatmap flag, disabled by default for existing customers. Canary enabled for internal clusters and a stage behind the rollout gate; follow-on step will enable 0.1% sampling for hosted tier and 1% for dedicated, then iterate. Backfill: included a small backfill job (observability/scripts/backfill_heatmap.py) to index recent traces for correlation testing. Testing: unit tests for sampler ring-buffer and proto serialization, integration test that validates annotations appear on traces end-to-end, and a perf microbenchmark showing <1.3% median p99 increase when sampling at 0.1% on benchmark lab. Migration/rollback: feature flag removal and a short migration script to drop the temporary heatmap index if disabled. Related: ENG-5421 (investigation into GPU contention during long prompts), ties to runbook playbooks introduced in previous telemetry work.
commit_summary: Add kernel sampler probe and ring-buffer aggregation (runtime/probes/kernel_sampler.cc)
Define kernel_heatmap proto and serialization helpers (telemetry/protos/kernel_heatmap.proto)
Add cost-flow annotator and glue to trace aggregator (observability/annotators/cost_flow_annotator.go)
Dashboard panels and explorer for kernel heatmap and cost flow (dashboards/infra/*)
Backfill and test harness for validating annotation correctness (observability/scripts/backfill_heatmap.py)
Unit and integration tests, CI fixtures, and small perf harness
files_changed: runtime/probes/kernel_sampler.cc — new probe that samples kernel queue/execution histograms (compact packed format)
telemetry/protos/kernel_heatmap.proto — proto and versioning notes for heatmap export
observability/annotators/cost_flow_annotator.go — attaches per-stage cost vectors and rollup markers to traces
observability/scripts/backfill_heatmap.py — limited backfill for recent traces for correlation testing
dashboards/infra/kernel-heatmap-panel.json — new panel with per-device occupancy heatmap and tail markers
dashboards/infra/cost-flow-explorer.json — interactive explorer to visualize stage-level cost attribution and token slices
runbooks/kernel-contention.md — operator runbook and mitigation steps
integration-tests/telemetry/kernel_sampler_test.go — end-to-end test that validates trace enrichment
docs/observability/kernel-heatmap.md — user doc for the new gate and how to interpret the panel
review_comments: Priya Natarajan: Overall this looks helpful. Two asks: (1) add guardrails for memory used by the ring buffer on small hosts, (2) include an example of how cost-flow attribution maps to tokens in the dashboard docs.
Arianna Chen (author): Implemented ring-buffer soft-limit and metric when limit is reached; expanded docs with a token-annotated example screenshot (docs/observability/kernel-heatmap.md).
Ethan Park: Can we avoid shipping the proto change as breaking for older telemetry consumers? Suggest versioning and a compat shim in the trace aggregator.
Arianna Chen: Added proto v1/v2 handling in trace aggregator and a compatibility converter in observability/annotators/compat_converter.go, added tests for the converter.
Lena Voss: CI flaked on integration test due to timing; please add a retry wrapper to the test harness so flakes are minimized.
Arianna Chen: Added retry/backoff wrapper and bumped the test timeout; reran integration suite.
ci_checks: ci/bench — pass (microbench shows <1.3% p99 regression at 0.1% sampling)
source.release_notes: Add kernel-level sampled heatmaps and a cost-flow annotator for invocation traces. Feature is behind feature flag observability.kernel_heatmap and disabled by default; initial rollout will be canary then small percentage sampling. Provides new dashboard panels and operator runbook for diagnosing kernel contention and per-stage cost attribution.
source.notes_for_ops: If enabling at scale, monitor telemetry/sampler_memory and telemetry/heatmap_queue_drops. Backfill job should be run during low traffic. Rolling back requires disabling feature flag and running drop_heatmap_index migration script.
source.risk_profile: medium: introduces new telemetry exports and storage, but guarded by feature flag. Compatibility shim added to avoid breaking older collectors.
post_merge_tasks: Enable feature flag for internal canary cluster
Run backfill_heatmap.py against last 7 days in staging
Observe telemetry/sampler_memory and adjust sampling thresholds
source.human_readable_summary: This PR instruments kernel queue/execution hotspots and stitches them into token-stage cost attributions. It adds lightweight sampling in the runtime and asynchronous annotation in the trace pipeline, plus dashboards and runbook. The feature is gated and rolled out gradually to limit overhead and storage growth.

source.title: per-tenant throughput SLA enforcer + shared burst token refactor
source.author: Priya Nair
source.description:
Motivation: customers on Dedicated ask for predictable per-tenant throughput guarantees while still allowing short-term bursts across a shared fleet. This PR implements an in-runtime per-tenant throughput SLA enforcer plus a refactor of the fleet-wide shared burst token pool to support weighted allocation and backpressure coordination with the autoscaler.

Summary of changes: added a light-weight controller (runtime/fleet/sla_enforcer.go) that tracks per-tenant token budgets (tokens/sec) and allocates from the local host bucket first, then from the shared burst pool when available. The shared burst pool has been moved out of the legacy global dispatcher and into a refactored service (runtime/fleet/burst_pool.go) that exposes an async lease API and utilization metrics. The autoscaler now consumes burst-pool pressure signals to pre-warm capacity when pool exhaustion is sustained. Console and metrics surfaces updated to show per-tenant SLA usage and pool utilization. Documentation and runbook include guidance for setting tenant SLAs and fallback behavior.

Behavior and guarantees: tenants configured with a throughput_sla (rps/tokens-per-sec equivalent) will be rate-limited at the runtime layer; transient bursts up to configured burst_quota are served from the shared pool subject to per-tenant weight and global fairness. When the pool is exhausted for >2s for a tenant, the SLA enforcer starts returning a structured quota_exceeded error with an RFC-structured JSON body that downstream services can use for graceful degradation. Autoscaler integration: a new metric (burst_pool_pressure_ratio) is emitted and used by the Dedicated autoscaler to trigger proactive pre-warm scaling when sustained >75% for >30s.

Backward compatibility: all behaviors default to existing admission rules when no per-tenant SLA is configured. The shared pool refactor maintains previous heuristics but exposes knobs for weight and max_shares per tenant. Migration is guarded behind runtime feature flag "enable_sla_enforcer" (off by default in existing installs).

Testing: unit tests for lease accounting and weight arithmetic added; integration tests simulate multi-tenant burst scenarios and assert observability metrics and autoscaler hooks; e2e Canary harness included under perf-canary to validate latency/p99 under mixed workloads.

Related items: linked Linear tasks and runbook updates are referenced below. The PR is intentionally scoped to runtime and orchestration layers; model-serving kernels are unchanged.

Notes: this PR contains a couple of nontrivial refactors; reviewers, please pay attention to concurrency in burst_pool.go and to the metric tagging in runtime/telemetry.
commits: a1f4d12: add SLA enforcer controller and basic leases
b7c9e30: refactor burst pool into standalone service + async lease API
c2d4f90: autoscaler: react to burst_pool_pressure_ratio for pre-warm logic
d99f3ab: console: show tenant SLA usage and pool utilization dashboards
e11b6c4: tests: add integration scenarios and canary harness config
f3a7b2e: docs: runbook + ops guide for configuring tenant SLAs
review_comments: Daniel Kim: please double-check lease expiry race — I saw a potential double-lease return in high-concurrency paths.
Priya Nair: addressed in a1f4d12 -> added an atomic state machine and testcase; also added comment explaining invariant.
Sofia Martinez: console panels look good; can we add a compact table of 'per-tenant effective_rate' for top 10 tenants?
Priya Nair: added panel and a telemetry tag to export top tenants as a metric (see commit d99f3ab).
Ethan Park: nit: prefer context-aware logger fields instead of fmt in burst_pool.go; otherwise LGTM.
Priya Nair: switched to structured logging and re-ran CI.
source.conversation_summary: Start: feature proposal + design doc. Mid-review: concurrency fixes and telemetry additions. Final: approvals after autoscaler integration test and canary pass. Approved by Daniel, Sofia, Ethan.
source.rollout_plan: Feature gated by runtime flag enable_sla_enforcer. Canary rollout: 5% of Dedicated clusters for 24h in us-west-2, then 25% in us-east-1 with autoscaler prewarm thresholds enabled. Observability guardrails: alert if sla_violation_count rises >5x baseline or burst_pool_pressure_ratio >0.85 for 5m.
source.merge_details: merged via squash (squash-merge), commit message consolidated authors' commits, merged by Daniel Kim after successful canary and autoscaler integration tests.
source.release_notes: Added per-tenant throughput SLA enforcement and a refactored shared burst token pool. Allows Dedicated customers to specify throughput_sla for tenants; supports weighted shared bursts and autoscaler pre-warm integration. Backwards compatible when feature flag is disabled.

source.title: add-invoke-slice-sampler-and-token-cascade-explainer
source.author: Samir Patel
source.description: Motivation: long-tail token cost and latency noise make it hard to correlate bursts to kernel warmup, prefix vs completion cost, and scheduling contention. This PR introduces two complementary observability primitives: (1) an Invoke Slice Sampler that slices invocations into time-aligned 'slices' to preserve bursty token-attribution while dramatically reducing cardinality and storage cost; and (2) a Token Cascade Explainer that attributes per-token cost/latency to a small set of causal contributors (prefix KV hits/misses, kernel warmup penalty, batching wait, and I/O stalls) and exposes an explainer panel in the console. Summary of changes: - New sampler: telemetry/ingest/slice_sampler.go adds a deterministic slice keyed by (tenant, route, invocation_bucket) and emits sampled span anchors plus compact slice digests. Sampler is opt-in via telemetry.config.slice_sampling.enabled=false|true. - Token cascade: pkg/observability/token_cascade.go implements cascade attribution heuristics that correlate per-token timing with kernel occupancy metadata and KV cache events; exposes cascade tags on spans (cascade.stage and cascade.score). - Dashboards: dashboards/invocation_cascade_explainer.json adds a new panel with per-slice token waterfall, kernel warmup heatmap, and slice-level cost breakdown (prefix vs completion vs kernel). - Ingest/transform: telemetry/transform/slice_hydrator.go and telemetry/transform/cascade_enricher.go perform lightweight join of sampled anchors to full spans to produce rollups. - Docs & examples: docs/observability/token-cascade.md and examples/observability/quickstart-slice.sh. - Tests: tests/e2e/observability/synthetic_slice_test.py adds deterministic workload that reproduces warmup-burst patterns and validates attribution. Design notes and rollout: the slice sampler reduces telemetry volume by ~12x on our internal bursty trace workloads; we configured a conservative default sampling rate of 1/100 slices per tenant but added adaptive rate increases for tenants with high SLO sensitivity. The cascade explainer is purely additive instrumentation and will appear as optional panels; it does not change any runtime decision logic. Backwards compatibility: existing span shapes and tags remain unchanged unless telemetry.config.cascade_enrichment.enabled=true. Rollout plan: feature flag gated, staged rollout over 2 weeks: opt-in in staging, 10% of production tenants, 50% after stability checks, full rollout. Testing: unit tests for sampler determinism, integration tests for hydration path, and e2e synthetic replay. Checklist: - [x] implement sampler and hydrator - [x] add cascade attribution heuristics - [x] new dashboard panels and docs - [x] synthetic e2e tests - [x] feature-flag gating and config defaults Performance notes: measured ~0.8ms median overhead added to ingest path when enrichment enabled (95th percentile spikes at 5ms during backfill). Storage cost impact: with default sampling we saw ~12x reduction in sampled-span volume on internal traces, with cascade rollups adding ~3% storage. Related: ENG-6591 (observability long-tail), SUP-4121 (customer-reported burst attribution request).
commits: Initial slice sampler and tests (sampler + deterministic hash)
Cascade attribution MVP and unit tests
Hydrator + transform pipeline integration
Dashboard panels and layout tweaks
Documentation and quickstart script
Add e2e synthetic replay test and CI jobs
discussion: Ava Chen (reviewer): Nice separation between sampling and enrichment — can we make the slice key stable across language SDKs? Suggest exporting the hash function spec in telemetry/hash_spec.md.
Samir (author): Ack — added telemetry/hash_spec.md and updated quickstart to reference it. The hash uses FNV-1a 64 then mod slice_size — deterministic across runtimes.
Diego Marquez (reviewer): Found a race in hydrate pipeline when backfilling; please ensure we use idempotent joins and avoid OOM on large backfills.
Samir (author): Fixed by switching the hydrator to streaming join with a bounded buffer and added a backfill safety config (telemetry.transform.hydrator.backfill_max_bytes).
Priya Nair (approver): Verified dashboard panels in staging and confirmed the explainer aligns with traced kernel occupancy. Approving with one nit on labeling in README.
CI bot: All observable tests passed on rerun (first run had transient lint failure, rerun fixed).
ci: lint: failed (transient newline in docs) -> fixed -> passed

source.title: Orchestra pausepoint harness with transparent rewind and cohort quarantine
source.author: Aisha Noor
source.ci_summary: Initial pipeline failed on integration test (shadow-replay#timeout) due to an upstream fixture; flaky test isolated and test rerun passed. Lint and unit tests green. E2E smoke passed in canary environment.
commits: b2c3f4a: add orchestration harness and pausepoint state machine
d9a8ee1: implement cohort-quarantine controller and quarantine policies
e1f2c33: telemetry: add pausepoint traces and decision reasons to span tags
a7b6c55: docs: runbook and playbook for manual holdpoint intervention
c4d5b66: tests: integration scenarios for transparent rewind and replay
source.description:
Motivation:
We need a deterministic way to hold, inspect, and safely rewind model rollout traffic for live canaries and A/B experiments without losing session-affinity or producing silent regressions. Current pause mechanics are ad-hoc (manual scaling, pod cordon) and opaque in telemetry which makes post-hoc root cause and safe rewind labor-intensive.

What this PR does (summary):
- Introduces a Pausepoint state machine inside the orchestrator that exposes explicit holdpoints for staged rollouts. Holdpoints can be triggered by policy (telemetry gates) or manually from the Console.
- Implements a Cohort Quarantine controller that isolates affected user slices when a holdpoint is entered; quarantined cohorts are routed to a shadow or golden variant, and traffic is stamped for replay.
- Adds Transparent Rewind: deterministic session-preserving replay of quarantined traffic against previous model snapshots with side-by-side scoring so operators can compare deltas before committing an automatic revert.
- Expands telemetry: every pausepoint decision now emits a decision_reason tag, cohort_id, and a deterministic trace id that ties replay sessions to original requests.
- Includes operator runbook and console UI for inspecting pausepoint state, kicking off a rewind, or escalating to manual rollback.

Design notes:
- Pausepoint lifecycle: OPEN -> HOLDING -> QUARANTINED -> REPLAYING -> RESOLVED (COMMIT/REVERT). Transitions are guarded by timeouts and safety tokens so long-running holds can be auto-escalated.
- Quarantine semantics: quarantined cohorts are isolated at routing layer; their requests are mirrored to both candidate and golden models. Mirrors are rate-limited and tagged for replay capture.
- Rewind integrity: replay is performed preserving session cookies and KV cache pointers when available; where exact replay is impossible (external side-effects) we mark the trace as non-deterministic and exclude it from automatic revert decisions.

Checklist:
- [x] Pausepoint state machine + controller implementation
- [x] Cohort quarantine routing + mirror capture
- [x] Deterministic replay harness for transparent rewind
- [x] Telemetry tags and Console UI for pausepoint inspection
- [x] Integration tests covering holdpoint flow and replay verification
- [x] Runbook and operator playbook for incident handling

Backward compatibility:
- Pausepoints are opt-in via a new CRD (Pausepoint) and a feature flag. Default behavior for existing rollouts is unchanged.

Risks and mitigations:
- Increased memory footprint for mirrored requests: mitigated with streaming mirrors and configurable retention TTLs.
- Replay non-determinism could produce spurious signals: we surface non-deterministic flags and exclude affected traces from automated scoring.

How to test locally:
1) bootstrap local orchestrator with feature flag enabled (scripts/local_replay_fixture.sh)
2) create a Pausepoint CR using examples/rollouts/cohort_quarantine.yml
3) trigger a canary and send test traffic; use Console PausepointPanel to enter HOLD state
4) initiate Replay and watch telemetry spans emitted to local Jaeger

Related work and tickets: ENG-3245 (pausepoint design), ENG-3310 (transparent rewind prototype), SUP-4421 (customer incident: noisy canary requiring manual quarantine)

Notes on scope: This change focuses on rollout control-plane and routing glue; it does not attempt to modify model runtime kernels or KV cache serialization across versions — those are future follow-ups (linked in design doc).
review_comments: Jordan Blake: Nice decomposition; two questions: how do we guarantee session-affinity during replay when candidates change network endpoints? Suggest adding a sticky session token in the routing layer.
Author (Aisha Noor): Agree — added sticky session token logic and tests (see pausepoint_controller.go commit).
Ravi Kapoor: CI showed a flaky test on first run. I retriggered and it passed. Also: please add a TTL knob to the cohort quarantine list to prevent indefinite quarantine.
Author (Aisha Noor): Added quarantine_ttl to CRD and defaulted to 30m in helm chart. Updated runbook to warn operators about long quarantines.
Priya Singh: Telemetry looks good, but for privacy we should redact PII before storing replay payloads. Add a pipeline step to scrub headers.
Author (Aisha Noor): Added header-scrub hook in scripts/local_replay_fixture.sh and marked non-deterministic traces when scrub removes key fields.
source.release_notes: Add pausepoint orchestrator: safe holdpoints for staged rollouts, cohort quarantine with mirrored replay, and transparent rewind for deterministic rollback decisions. Feature is behind a feature-flag and requires Pausepoint CRDs.

source.title: shortburst-attention-fusion-and-slab-packing-for-short-sequence-latency
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: Many customer workloads at Redwood are dominated by short sequences (<64 tokens) where per-request overhead (kernel launch, KV cache management, micro-batch alignment) dominates token cost. This PR introduces a runtime path optimized for short-burst workloads: 1) an attention-fusion kernel variant that fuses small-sequence attention + rotary/ALiBi precompute to reduce kernel launches for sequences <= 64, 2) a token-slab packing allocator that packs multiple short requests into a single device slab with per-request offsets to avoid per-token KV allocations, and 3) a micro-continuous batching tweak that lowers the batching tick for short sequences while preserving overall throughput for long sequences. The goal is to reduce median latency for short requests without regressions for long-tail workloads. Changes: implemented fused_attention_short.cu and refactored attention dispatch to pick the fused kernel based on sequence_length and head_count thresholds; added slab_packer.{cc,h} which provides a fast arena allocator for short KV segments and a lightweight header describing offsets; introduced a new shortburst batching policy that lowers the flush timeout to 2ms for short requests and adds an adaptive cooldown to avoid microbursting on sustained high QPS; added smoke and microbench tests simulating tens of thousands of small requests. Compatibility: default behavior is conservative—the fused kernel engages only when model geometry matches supported head/hidden layouts and when quantization mode is FP16 or 4-bit fused path; slab packing is opt-in via runtime cfg short_slab_enabled=true to allow staged rollouts. Rollout plan: canary on dedicated-pool-3 for 48 hours, metric watch on median_p95_latency_shortseq, kv_allocator_misses, gpu_util; automatic rollback on error spikes. This PR also includes a short note for SDK teams to add a small-client-side retry jitter to avoid synchronized flapping. Checklist: [x] unit tests added for slab allocator and fused kernel dispatch [x] microbench harness for short-seq latency [x] docs: runtime tuning note in perf/short_seq.md [x] Linear ticket: ENG-1842.
source.commits: 4 commits: (1) shortburst: add fused attention kernel variant and doc comments; (2) slab: implement token slab allocator with header+offsets and wrap in RAII; (3) batching: add shortburst policy and adaptive cooldown; (4) tests/docs: microbench, unit tests, and perf note.
review_comments: Diego Martinez: Nice approach; can we add a guard for head_dim % 8 != 0 to avoid warp divergence?
Author (Ava Chen): added head_dim modulo guard and fallback to standard kernel when misaligned; microbench shows <0.5% extra overhead for those cases
Priya Kapoor: please ensure slab_packer zeroes headers for safety across reused slabs
Author (Ava Chen): headers zeroed and test added to cover reuse case
source.release_notes: Add short-sequence optimized attention fusion and token slab packing to reduce median latency for workloads with sequences <=64 tokens; opt-in via runtime config 'short_slab_enabled' and controlled rollout.
source.benchmark_summary: Canary microbench (RTX A100, FP16 weights) shows median latency for 32-token requests reduced from 8.9ms to 5.1ms (43% improvement); 95th percentile reduced from 18.3ms to 10.7ms; throughput effect on long sequences (>512) is within +-1.5%.
source.regression_guard: Added unit tests for allocator reuse, attention dispatch fallbacks, and integration-shortseq harness; telemetry asserts on kv_allocator_misses and kernel_selection_ratio added to prevent silent regressions.

source.title: Add hardware-timestamp tracepoints and synthetic-replay microbench for kernel latency and tile occupancy
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Added optional hardware timestamp tracepoints and a synthetic replay microbenchmark to capture kernel-level latency distributions and tile occupancy. Exposed new dashboard metrics for per-kernel P50/P99 and per-tile live occupancy counters. No runtime behavior changes when tracing disabled.
source.description: Motivation: pinpoint sub-kernel latency variance and per-tile occupancy skew seen during continuous batching on long sequences. This PR adds lightweight hardware-timestamp tracepoints around kernel launches, a small lock-free per-tile occupancy counter, and a deterministic synthetic-replay microbenchmark that replays recorded request traces at controlled concurrency for stable profiling. Changes are gated behind runtime flags so there is zero overhead when disabled. High-level summary: 1) runtime/tracing/hw_timestamp_trace.h/.cc implements RAII-style trace scopes that capture wallclock and GPU hw timestamps when available and annotate the internal timeline with span ids, 2) runtime/scheduler/tile_counter.h/.cc introduces a cache-friendly per-tile occupancy counter sampled by the scheduler to record occupancy distributions, 3) tools/replay_bench parses a compressed request trace and replays with configurable arrival patterns and concurrency to exercise the batching scheduler reproducibly, 4) observability/exporters/perf_dashboard_exporter.py converts trace output into a bundled JSON for the perf dashboard including per-kernel latency histograms and per-tile occupancy heatmaps, 5) added unit and microbench tests and CI job to validate reproducible runs. Checklist: - [x] add hw timestamp trace scopes - [x] implement per-tile occupancy counters - [x] implement synthetic replay bench - [x] export summarized metrics for dashboard - [x] add CI microbench job - [x] update docs and example traces. Usage notes: enable tracing with REDWOOD_TRACING=hw_timestamp or use the scheduler flag --enable-tile-counters. Replay tool accepts --trace-file and --concurrency flags. Recorded traces are compressed with a simple run-length encoding to keep artifacts small. Backwards compatibility: tracing is opt-in and defaults to off so production behavior is unchanged. Performance: measured overhead with tracing on is ~2-3% for short sequences and up to 7% during heavy kernel churn; with tracing off overhead is nil. See release notes for more details.

title: Add hardware-timestamp tracepoints and synthetic-replay microbench for kernel latency and tile occupancy
preview: Motivation: pinpoint sub-kernel latency variance and per-tile occupancy skew seen during continuous batching on long sequences. This PR adds lightweight hardware-timestamp tracepoints around kernel launches, a small lock-free per-tile occupancy counter, and a deterministic synthetic-replay microbenchmark that replays recorded request traces at controlled concurrency for stable profiling. Changes are gated behind runtime flags so there is zero overhead when disabled. High-level summary: 1) runtime/tracing/hw_timestamp_trace.h/.cc implements RAII-style trace scopes that capture wallclock and GPU hw timestamps when available and annotate the internal timeline with span ids, 2) runtime/scheduler/tile_counter.h/.cc introduces a cache-friendly per-tile occupancy counter sampled by the scheduler to record occupancy distributions, 3) tools/replay_bench parses a compressed request trace and replays with configurable arrival patterns and concurrency to exercise the batching scheduler reproducibly, 4) observability/exporters/perf_dashboard_exporter.py converts trace output into a bundled JSON for the perf dashboard including per-kernel latency histograms and per-tile occupancy heatmaps, 5) added unit and microbench tests and CI job to validate reproducible runs. Checklist: - [x] add hw timestamp trace scopes - [x] implement per-tile occupancy counters - [x] implement synthetic replay bench - [x] export summarized metrics for dashboard - [x] add CI microbench job - [x] update docs and example traces. Usage notes: enable tracing with REDWOOD_TRACING=hw_timestamp or use the scheduler flag --enable-tile-counters. Replay tool accepts --trace-file and --concurrency flags. Recorded traces are compressed with a simple run-length encoding to keep artifacts small. Backwards compatibility: tracing is opt-in and defaults to off so production behavior is unchanged. Performance: measured overhead with tracing on is ~2-3% for short sequences and up to 7% during heavy kernel churn; with tracing off overhead is nil. See release notes for more details.

source.title: Queue-horizon throttling and KV probe backoff for continuous batcher
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Motivation: recent production incidents showed pathological tail latency when short-lived high-priority traffic interleaved with KV-heavy long-context requests. The continuous batcher could become congested by probe/burst requests that pin KV slots and prevent fairness tokens from progressing, causing latency spikes. This PR introduces a queue-horizon throttling policy and an exponential backoff for KV probe requests to reduce transient pin contention while preserving throughput.
commits: 9bd7f2c - batcher: introduce QueueHorizon windowing and accounting
b3a1e77 - kv_cache: probe backoff controller with exponential + jitter
d2f6c18 - scheduler: integrate horizon checks into admit path and fairness tokens
7e4b2a0 - kernels: add lightweight hint for pinned-kv friendly kernel selection
c5f0a9b - tests: new e2e and microbench for horizon throttling and probe backoff
f8a3e44 - docs: config reference and rollout notes for queue-horizon and kv-probe-backoff
review_comments: Marco: Can you explain how QueueHorizon differs from the aging-token approach? I want to ensure we don't duplicate accounting.

Priya: QueueHorizon is a windowed admission frontier that prevents admitting requests whose expected service window exceeds a configurable horizon. It complements aging tokens by preventing the queue head from being dominated during short spikes; both mechanisms run together.
Yuna: We saw a small throughput regression in stress when backoff enabled at 5ms base. Can we expose a knob to tune aggressive vs conservative backoff?
Priya: Added config batcher.kv_probe.backoff_profile with presets (conservative, default, aggressive). Updated docs with recommended profiles for Dedicated vs Hosted.
Liam: The kernel selector hint looks good but needs a comment explaining safety for quantized kernels.
Priya: Added clarifying comment and unit tests for selector fallback to ensure quantized path unchanged if hint unsupported.
benchmark_summary: Tail P99 latency (mixed KV-heavy + short requests) reduced from 482ms -> 168ms in 95th-percentile scenarios (20k RPS mix), measured on c6g-large GPU cluster
Overall throughput impact: -2.3% at peak when using conservative backoff; aggressive profile recovers throughput with modest P99 tradeoff
KV-probe failures (timeouts) decreased by 68% due to staged backoff avoiding repeated immediate probes
End-to-end latency for short inference requests improved median by 12ms under bursty traffic due to horizon gating
source.conversation_summary: Reviewers requested explicit tuning knobs and conservative defaults; we added three profiles, updated docs, and added e2e tests. CI flaked once on benchmark infra; rerun passed. After approvals from Marco and Yuna and a final security scan, we squashed and merged.
release_notes: Added queue-horizon throttling to the continuous batcher to reduce tail latency under bursty mixed workloads
Introduced KV probe exponential backoff to reduce repeated KV pin contention and transient timeouts
New config knobs: batcher.queue_horizon.window_ms and batcher.kv_probe.backoff_profile (conservative|default|aggressive)
source.rollout_plan: Canary rollout: 5% traffic for 24h, then 25% for 12h, 100% after 48h if no regressions. Monitoring: p99 latency, kv_probe.backoff_count, kernel-fallback-rate; automatic rollback on sustained p99 increase >20% or error spike.
source.notes_for_ops: Operators should monitor 'batcher.queue_horizon_rejects' and may select 'conservative' backoff for latency-sensitive Dedicated clusters. For priority-queue tenants, consider raising horizon_ms to prevent admission of long-context requests during bursts.
source.testing: Unit coverage added for queue horizon math and backoff jitter. E2E YAML reproduces the mixed-workload scenario; benchmarks validated on staging GPU pool. Load test scripts included in tools/benchmarks.
source.acknowledgements: Thanks to infra team for benchmark infra support and to SRE for incident context that motivated this work.
