source.title: Proportional backfill allocator and soft-reservation smoothing for Dedicated fleets
source.author: Maya Chen
commits_summary: implement proportional backfill allocator and smoothing state machine
add unit tests for proportional logic and edge cases
integration test that simulates staggered reservation churn
add feature flag and rollout script + docs
source.description:
Motivation: Dedicated fleets often have fragmented reserved capacity where tenants underutilize their reservations for short windows. Current behavior returns unused tokens aggressively which leads to oscillation when reservations are re-acquired and contributes to noisy autoscaler decisions. This PR implements a proportional backfill allocator plus soft-reservation smoothing to allow unused reserved capacity to be temporarily shared across tenants in a controlled, decaying manner.

What this changes (single string): 1) Proportional backfill allocator: when there is spare reserved headroom, we compute per-tenant proportional backfill based on their active demand and reservation weight. Backfill amounts are distributed as fractional credits with deterministic rounding to avoid starvation. Backfill is tagged with ttl and a reclaimable flag so original owners can reclaim capacity within the reclaim window. 2) Soft-reservation smoothing: when reservations change (scale down/release), we do not return all freed capacity immediately. Instead we put freed tokens into a smoothing pool that decays with configurable half-life (default: 60s). This reduces allocation volatility when tenants frequently adjust reservations or experience microbursts. 3) Autoscaler policy hook: autoscaler now includes a backfill-aware decision step. Scale-down decisions subtract smoothing pool before computing headroom so we avoid premature scale-ups triggered by transient backfill. 4) Telemetry: new metrics added - fleet.backfill.utilization_pct, fleet.backfill.reclaimed_events_total, fleet.backfill.decay_pool_size, tenant.backfill.received_tokens_total. Dashboards: new console dashboard for backfill behavior and a backfill heatmap for the last 24h. 5) Feature flag: opt-in behind config proportional_backfill_v1; default disabled for existing fleets. 6) Tests: unit + integration tests that simulate reservation churn, reclaim races, and fairness checks.

Checklist: - [x] Core allocator + smoothing implementation - [x] Unit tests (edge rounding/TTL) - [x] Integration test covering reclaim races - [x] Metrics emitted and dashboard wiring - [x] Docs for ops and rollout - [x] Feature flag gating and safe defaults - [x] Manual canary run in staging (see rollout notes)

Files changed (high level): allocator core, smoothing state machine, autoscaler hook, metrics registry, console dashboard, docs, feature flag config, tests and CI pipeline additions for integration tests. No public API changes to the Redwood API surface.

Review conversation (summary):
- Liam: raised concern about fairness under extreme skew (90/10) and suggested a cap per-tenant to avoid monopolization. PR added a per-tenant max_backfill_pct and tests.
- Priya: asked for explicit reclaim semantics when original owner scales back up rapidly; I added a reclaim window (default 15s) and deterministic priority for reclaiming older backfill credits first.
- Evan: requested telemetry labels for fleet_id and reservation_type; added those labels and lightweight cardinality guards.
- QA: flagged a timing flake in integration test; fixed by moving from wall-clock sleeps to a simulated tickable clock in tests.
- Security: validated no elevation of privilege since backfill is an allocator layer only and does not change auth.

CI notes: new integration tests increase pipeline time by ~2m. First run had a flaky that was fixed; final CI status is pass.

Rollout plan: gate behind proportional_backfill_v1 feature flag. Start with canary fleets (staging and two low-traffic prod fleets) for 48h, observe fleet.backfill.* metrics and autoscaler scale-up rates. If stable, expand to 20% of dedicated fleets for 7 days, then full rollout. Emergency rollback: flip feature flag off.

Backwards compatibility / risk: opt-in behavior by default. The allocator adds an internal pool but does not change reservation accounting semantics. Risk mode: incorrect smoothing params could increase tail latency for under-reserved tenants; mitigated by conservative default params and telemetry.

Release notes: Introduces a controlled proportional backfill allocator and soft-reservation smoothing for Dedicated fleets (feature-flagged). Adds telemetry and a console dashboard. Opt-in default.
source.test_plan: Unit tests + integration harness added. Integration simulates 50 tenants over 30 minutes with reservation churn patterns (sinusoidal + random spikes). Tests assert no tenant gains > max_backfill_pct and reclaim races resolve within reclaim_window. CI includes the integration harness as a gated job. Manual canary run executed in staging on 2026-02-14 with no regressions observed.
source.rollout_plan: Step 1: Enable proportional_backfill_v1 on staging and one low-traffic prod fleet for 48h. Step 2: If metrics stable (backfill.utilization_pct < 20% and reclaimed_events rate < baseline * 2), enable for 20% of fleets for 7 days. Step 3: Gradual 100% enable over 72h. Rollback: disable flag. Post-deploy: monitor tenant latency p50/p95, scale-up rate, and backfill reclaim alerts.
source.release_notes: Add proportional backfill allocator + soft-reservation smoothing (opt-in), new metrics and console dashboard, small config flags for smoothing half-life and per-tenant caps.
source.notes_for_release_engineering: Feature flag file added at deploy/featureflags/proportional_backfill_v1.yaml; coordinate with SRE to ensure canary fleets are labeled. Dashboard JSON included for console import. Integration job added to CI; may require extra runner quota during rollout.

source.title: introduce-cost-rules-engine-and-autotune-batching-defaults
source.author: Priya Kapoor
source.description: Summary: Add a Cost Rules Engine that automatically recommends and applies batching and KV-cache defaults per model and workload profile, exposes quantization profile hints for safe auto-application, and introduces a unit-economics estimator in the console and CLI. Motivation: customers routinely ask for hands-off cost tuning; operational teams want reproducible, audited defaults that reduce per-token costs without surprising latency regressions. This PR bundles runtime heuristics, a small rules DSL, console UI plumbing, and an opt-in rollout for auto-quantization. What changed (high level): - new runtime module redwood/serving/cost_rules.py with rules evaluation, telemetry hooks, and safe-apply gates - batcher tuning extension that selects default batch sizes based on latency SLOs and sequence-length histograms - KV cache policy defaults and prefix cache eligibility heuristics - quantization profiling pipeline (offline profiler + runtime hinting) and a safe auto-apply policy with rollback - console/CLI cost estimator showing projected per-token unit economics with suggested knobs - integration tests, docs, and an opt-in feature flag + audit logs Rollout plan: default rules are disabled by default for existing tenants. We enable the rules engine for internal canaries and add a gradual opt-in toggle in the console. Quantization auto-apply is gated behind a policy that requires: a) offline eval against golden prompt set, b) latency/regression guardrails, and c) explicit tenant opt-in. Checklist: - [x] implement rule evaluator and runtime hooks - [x] expose console estimator and API endpoints - [x] add offline quant profile pipeline and safe-apply path - [x] add unit + integration tests - [x] add docs and release notes - [x] add audit events and rollout flag Notes on safety: the rules engine never mutates a serving model binary directly; it returns a recommended configuration which a controlled subsystem applies with canarying and audits. The PR includes a conservative default profile that favors latency SLOs for small-sequence chat workloads and cost for high-throughput embedding workloads. Related: ENG-3412 (cost-automation epic), ENG-3470 (quantization-safety), SUP-1298 (customer support case motivating default cache rules).
commits: feat(cost-rules): add rules engine and evaluator (+tests)
feat(batching): extend batcher to accept runtime defaults and histogram buckets
feat(quant): add offline profiler and hint schema
feat(console): add unit-economics estimator panel and opt-in toggle
chore(tests): integration tests for rule application and rollback
fix(ci): flake in integration test, increase timeout
docs: add cost-rules design doc and user guide
squash: address review comments and remove debug logs
review_conversation: Ava Chen: High level LGTM. Please ensure telemetry includes pre/post token-cost breakdown and include schema for audit events.
Priya Kapoor: Added observability proto and wired pre/post token metrics. See redwood/observability/events/cost_rules_audit.proto.

Miguel Santos: Concern: automatic quantization risks subtle quality regressions for instruction-following models. Suggest conservative rollout and a required eval pass. Also call out per-tenant opt-in in docs.
Priya Kapoor: Agree. Implemented safe-apply policy (requires offline eval pass and rollback hook). Added docs and feature flag page. Quant auto-apply disabled by default.
Liam O'Connor: Noted a potential race in batcher config writes during scale events. Suggested a lock around update path. I added a mutex in tuner.go and a unit test replicating scale churn.
Ava Chen: CI failure was a real flake in integration test; reran and now all checks passed. Approving to merge once CI green.
Miguel Santos: Approve. Leaving a suggestion for a follow-up to tune embedding-heavy workloads after 2-week telemetry window.
source.release_notes: Add a Cost Rules Engine that recommends batching and cache defaults, an opt-in quantization safe-apply pipeline, and a unit-economics estimator in the console/CLI. Disabled by default for existing tenants; expected to reduce per-token cost by up to ~20% for high-throughput embedding workloads when enabled. See redwood/docs/cost-rules.md for configuration and rollout guidance.
source.notes_for_ops: Enable feature flag in helm: costRules.enabled=true for internal canaries. Monitor metrics: cost_rules.recommendation.applied_count, cost_rules.rollback_count, tokens.per_second and request.latency.p50/p95. Keep quant auto-apply disabled until 1-week canary telemetry looks stable.
follow_up_tasks: ENG-3499: telemetry-driven tuning for embedding workloads
ENG-3500: UI polish for estimator (comparison mode)
ENG-3501: auto-schedule periodic quant profiles for long-tail models

Motivation: customers routinely ask for hands-off cost tuning; operational teams want reproducible, audited defaults that reduce per-token costs without surprising latency regressions. This PR bundles runtime heuristics, a small rules DSL, console UI plumbing, and an opt-in rollout for auto-quantization. What changed (high level): - new runtime module redwood/serving/cost_rules.py with rules evaluation, telemetry hooks, and safe-apply gates - batcher tuning extension that selects default batch sizes based on latency SLOs and sequence-length histograms - KV cache policy defaults and prefix cache eligibility heuristics - quantization profiling pipeline (offline profiler + runtime hinting) and a safe auto-apply policy with rollback - console/CLI cost estimator showing projected per-token unit economics with suggested knobs - integration tests, docs, and an opt-in feature flag + audit logs Rollout plan: default rules are disabled by default for existing tenants. We enable the rules engine for internal canaries and add a gradual opt-in toggle in the console. Quantization auto-apply is gated behind a policy that requires: a) offline eval against golden prompt set, b) latency/regression guardrails, and c) explicit tenant opt-in. Checklist: - [x] implement rule evaluator and runtime hooks - [x] expose console estimator and API endpoints - [x] add offline quant profile pipeline and safe-apply path - [x] add unit + integration tests - [x] add docs and release notes - [x] add audit events and rollout flag Notes on safety: the rules engine never mutates a serving model binary directly; it returns a recommended configuration which a controlled subsystem applies with canarying and audits. The PR includes a conservative default profile that favors latency SLOs for small-sequence chat workloads and cost for high-throughput embedding workloads. Related: ENG-3412 (cost-automation epic), ENG-3470 (quantization-safety), SUP-1298 (customer support case motivating default cache rules).

source.title: Add Security FAQ / RFP snippets to Internal Admin (approved-language library + API)
source.author: Hanae Suzuki
source.release_notes: Internal-only: adds a centrally managed, versioned snippet library for approved Security FAQ + RFP response language, with RBAC gating, audit logging, and an API for GTM tooling to consume canonical text blocks.
source.body: Summary: This PR adds first-class “Security Snippets” support to Internal Admin so we can centrally manage approved FAQ/RFP text blocks (and their versions) that Sales/SE tooling consumes. This is part of the Enterprise Security Enablement Kit refresh: we’ve had repeated drift between Drive/Confluence snippets, HubSpot templates, and ad-hoc copy/paste. The goal is a single source of truth with clear ownership, review metadata, and safe distribution semantics (no restricted evidence in snippets). Motivation / context: Enterprise security reviews are gating Dedicated/Private deals; field teams need fast, consistent answers without over-claiming. Today, approved language lives across Confluence + Drive and gets copied into various tools. This introduces: (1) a canonical snippet store; (2) an API for downstream consumption; (3) RBAC + audit log events for changes; (4) explicit versioning + “last reviewed” metadata to align with enablement kit governance. Scope (what’s included): - New snippet domain model + persistence: `security_snippets` table (Postgres) with: `key` (stable identifier), `title`, `category`, `deployment_modes` (Hosted/Dedicated/Private flags stored as text), `content_markdown`, `status` (draft|in_review|approved|deprecated), `version`, `last_reviewed_at`, `expires_at` (optional), `owner` (team/alias), `approver` (name), and `notes_internal` (e.g., references to evidence request playbook). - Versioning semantics: update creates a new version row (immutable content by version). “Approved” is per-version; deprecating a version does not delete it. - Internal Admin UI: new “Security & Compliance → Snippets” page with: list + filters (category, status, deployment mode), diff viewer between versions, and an “export” view (raw markdown or plaintext) for downstream tools. - Public/consumption API (internal): `GET /internal/snippets/security?status=approved` and `GET /internal/snippets/security/{key}`. The API returns only non-restricted fields; explicitly excludes `notes_internal` and any future evidence references. - Permissions: gated behind Internal Admin RBAC permission `admin.security_snippets.write` (write) and `admin.security_snippets.read` (read). Default: read for Sales Ops + Solutions Eng; write limited to Security/GRC + Product Security PM. (See reviewers’ notes below re: default grants.) - Audit logging: emits `security.snippet.created`, `security.snippet.updated`, `security.snippet.status_changed` with actor, snippet key, version, and timestamp. These show up in existing audit log pipelines for internal systems. - Seeded initial snippet keys: a small curated set to validate workflow (SSO/SAML, SCIM, RBAC granularity, audit log retention/export, data use/no-training language, encryption in transit/at rest, SOC 2 report request steps). The exact text was copied from the working GTM draft library and then reviewed in this PR; it is not intended to be a complete library in this change. Out of scope / explicit non-goals: - No new product features or promises; no roadmap language. - No restricted compliance evidence (SOC 2 report, pen test reports, vuln scan artifacts) is stored here. Snippets may include the approved process for requesting evidence under NDA, but not the evidence itself. - No direct HubSpot/CRM integration in this PR; the first consumer is the generic response tool (tracked separately) and manual use via export. Implementation details: - Backend: `internal-admin-api` adds `SecuritySnippetService` + repository; migrations in `migrations/2026_02_07_security_snippets.sql`. - Frontend: `internal-admin-ui` adds `SecuritySnippetsPage`, `SnippetEditor` (markdown), and `VersionDiffModal`. - API response includes `content_markdown` and `content_text` (server-side stripped markdown) to reduce divergence between tools that support rich text vs plain text. - All endpoints require admin auth; responses include `etag` and support `If-None-Match` to reduce polling load for downstream tooling. Changed areas (high level): - `services/internal-admin-api/src/security_snippets/*` - `services/internal-admin-api/src/routes/internal/snippets_security.ts` - `services/internal-admin-ui/src/pages/security/SecuritySnippetsPage.tsx` - `packages/rbac/policies/internal-admin.yaml` - `packages/audit-events/src/events/security_snippets.ts` - DB migration and seeds under `services/internal-admin-api/db/*` Example snippet (illustrative, shortened): Key: `sso_saml_scim_overview` Title: “SSO (SAML) + SCIM support” Category: “Access Controls” Content (excerpt): “Redwood supports SAML 2.0 SSO for the Redwood Console and supports SCIM 2.0 for user provisioning with supported IdPs. Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced for console actions and API keys. For details on supported IdPs and RBAC roles, see the internal capability reference. If a requirement is not currently supported, follow the Security Questionnaire Exceptions Policy.” Test plan: - Unit tests: service + repository (create, new version on update, status transitions) - API tests: auth required; status filter; ETag behavior - UI smoke test: create draft (GRC/Sec role), request review, approve, view diff - Verified audit events appear in internal audit log stream for a status change - Manual: export approved snippets as plaintext and paste into a sample RFP questionnaire section (formatting preserved reasonably) Security / compliance considerations: - RBAC gating prevents broad write access; all mutations are audited. - “No evidence stored” is enforced by process + UI copy: editor includes a warning banner and blocks uploads/attachments. - PII: none expected; we added a lint check in editor that warns (non-blocking) if content appears to include email addresses or phone numbers. Review conversation (highlights): - Marcus Lin (Security Eng): requested audit event fields include `snippet_key` + `version` and that the update path is versioned (not in-place). Addressed in commit `a8b3f2c` (adds `version` and immutability). Approved after changes. - Olga Petrov (GRC): requested stronger do-not-say guardrails and explicit “evidence request under NDA” language in SOC 2 snippet; also requested that ISO language avoids implying certification. Updated seeded snippets and added an `approved_claims_tags` field (string list stored as comma-separated text) to support future reporting; tags are not yet exposed to consumers. Approved. - Naomi Feldman (Product Security PM): requested that deployment mode filters be explicit (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) and that any roadmap mention be removed from seeded audit logging snippet. Updated copy and added deployment mode UI chips. Approved. - Camila Reyes (Product Marketing): requested consistency with security messaging pillars (terminology: “Hosted API / Dedicated / Private”) and removal of ambiguous “industry standard” claims in the generic encryption snippet. Updated snippet text accordingly. Approved. - Markus Klein (Solutions Eng): requested plaintext export option and that snippets be searchable by common questionnaire phrasing (“Do you train on customer data?”, “SIEM export”, “SCIM”). Added full-text search across title/content and `keywords` field (stored as text). Approved. CI status: - Build: pass - Unit tests: pass - Lint: pass - Migration check: pass - UI build: pass Rollout / ops notes: - Feature is behind existing Internal Admin access; new page visible only with `admin.security_snippets.read`. - Seed data runs in staging only; production will start empty and be populated by Security/GRC after the enablement kit review cycle. - Follow-up: wire the generic response tool to this API and enforce “approved only” in consumers (tracked in INT-1234 + PM-4829 follow-up tasks).
commits: db: add security_snippets table + migration
api: CRUD + versioning semantics for security snippets
rbac: add read/write permissions for snippets
audit: emit snippet create/update/status events
ui: add snippets list, editor, version diff + export
api: add ETag + plaintext rendering
seed: add initial approved-language snippet set (staging only)

source.title: SDKS: auth mosaic + streaming consolidation and ergonomic API polish
source.author: Maya Patel
source.commits: 4 - py: add SessionSurface and retry presets; ts: backpressure hints + token helper; go: session factory + iterator safety; docs: consolidate examples and changelog
source.description: Context: We observed subtle divergence in how Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs handled token refresh during long-lived streams and how retry presets influence stream resume characteristics. This caused two classes of production issues: (1) opaque reauth races that produced 401s during resume, and (2) inconsistent backpressure hints leading to latency spikes when batching heuristics changed across SDKs. This PR introduces a small cross-SDK surface (SessionSurface) and a compact "mosaic" of auth utilities to align behavior. Key goals: harmonize reauth handshake, provide ergonomic retry presets (latency-first and throughput-first), and expose minimal stream backpressure hints to SDK consumers. Changes: - Introduce SessionSurface (py) and SessionFactory (go) as a lightweight facade for long-lived sessions; exported helpers in ts for parity. - Add two retry preset bundles: latency_aware and throughput_aware (shared canonical definitions in pkg/common/retry). - Token choreography: tokenHelper/ensureToken (ts) and a small auth-mosaic helper (py/go) to centralize refresh windows and jitter. - Streaming ergonomics: backpressure hints API (ts) + iterator safety guard (go) + python convenience wrapper for async generators. - Docs & examples: migration guide with concrete recipes for upgrading long-lived streams; example showing how to pick retry presets and combine with SessionSurface. Testing: unit coverage across SDKs; manual smoke runs against a dedicated canary stack. CI: one flaky legacy test in sdks/python was rerun and passed on the second run. Checklist: - [x] API design reviews with SDK leads - [x] Cross-language parity tests - [x] Changelog entries and migration guide - [x] Version bumps for SDK packages (py 1.13.0, ts 1.29.0, go 1.12.2) Rollout plan: merge to main, cut coordinated SDK releases; library consumers that rely on automatic token refresh should switch to the new SessionSurface/Factory or call the token helper from their stream bootstrap code. Related: ENG-2345 (stream reliability), PM-7890 (developer ergonomics).
review_comments: Ava Chen: Left a request to clarify the token jitter math in tokenHelper.ts — can we make the 10% jitter configurable?
Maya Patel (author): Updated tokenHelper to expose jitterPct with a sensible default; added unit test for edge window lengths.
Luis Moreno: Question about deprecation strategy for old streaming helper; suggested a deprecation period and compatibility shim.
Maya Patel (author): Added a one-release compatibility shim and a migration note in the docs.
Priya Shah: Approve after minor nits addressed (docs clarifications and an extra TS example).
Tomás García: Verified go iterator safety changes on staging canary; approves.
source.release_notes: Introduce lightweight SessionSurface/SessionFactory to unify long-lived session behavior across SDKs; add latency_aware and throughput_aware retry presets; expose tokenHelper/ensureToken and minimal backpressure hints for streaming ergonomics. Includes SDK version bumps: python 1.13.0, typescript 1.29.0, go 1.12.2.

source.title: Console: Shadow experiment results page (quality deltas + example drilldowns) behind feature flag
source.author: Dylan Brooks
source.release_notes: Console: Adds Shadow Experiment Results page with quality delta charts (incl. confidence bands), segment filters, and scored example drilldowns. Gated behind feature flag `shadow_experiments_results_ui`.
source.body:
## Summary
Adds the Console UI for viewing Shadow Experiment results: quality deltas vs control, confidence intervals, segment breakdowns, and a drilldown drawer for scored examples.

This is explicitly *read-only* UI for the beta. Creation flow remains in the existing experiment wizard (DES-611 work), and the results page is linked from Rollouts timeline + the Shadow Experiments list.

Everything in this PR is gated behind `shadow_experiments_results_ui` (per tenant). When disabled, routes fall back to the existing placeholder/"Coming soon" page.

## Why
We have shadow traffic shipping in dogfood and early Dedicated beta, but teams can’t validate quality changes without an at-a-glance view of deltas and a way to inspect examples. This PR wires up the results UX to the new aggregates + examples endpoints from the Console backend (PR #24933) and renders:
- Quality delta charts (candidate vs control), with 95% CI bands and significance callout when applicable
- Segment filters (safe dimensions only) + time range picker
- "Top regressions" table and per-metric deep links
- Example drilldown drawer (prompt/response *redacted*) with rubric breakdowns and scorer metadata

## Feature flag
- Flag: `shadow_experiments_results_ui`
- Default: off
- Enabled for: internal dogfood tenant only (handled via INT-912 / config rollout)

## UX highlights
- Results header: Experiment name, route, control target, shadow target(s), sampling %, rubric version, data retention indicator
- Metric cards: e.g. `format_adherence_rate`, `refusal_rate`, `instruction_following_score_mean`, `safety_policy_violation_rate`
- Chart behavior:
  - Shows control and candidate series + delta band view toggle
  - Confidence band computed server-side; UI simply renders (no client stats)
  - Tooltips show sample size (n), window, and whether samples are shadow-sampled vs prompt-set
- Segment filters (cardinality-safe): `region`, `request_type`, `model_variant`, `status_class` (2xx/4xx/5xx), `token_bucket` (bucketed)
  - Explicitly *not* supported in UI: `prompt_id`, `customer_id`, raw `route_id` free text (route is chosen from context)

## API wiring
- GET `/console/v1/shadow-experiments/:id/aggregates?window=1h&range=24h&segment=region`
- GET `/console/v1/shadow-experiments/:id/examples?metric=format_adherence_rate&outcome=regression&limit=50&cursor=...`
- Error/empty states:
  - If scoring lag or no data: show "Waiting for scoring" with link to troubleshooting docs (internal)
  - If RBAC denies `shadow:read_examples`: hide examples tab and show callout

## Implementation details
- Adds `ShadowExperimentResultsPage` route + navigation entry under Observability → Shadow Experiments
- New components:
  - `QualityDeltaChart` (line + band)
  - `MetricDeltaCard` (delta, CI, sample size)
  - `SegmentFilterBar` (safe enum dims only)
  - `ScoredExampleDrawer` (prompt/response + rubric breakdown)
- Uses existing design system primitives (Panels, Callouts, Tables, Skeleton loaders)
- Maintains strict PII posture:
  - UI never renders raw prompt/response unless backend returns redacted text (expected)
  - Displays explicit "Redacted" markers when fields omitted

## Tests
- Unit tests:
  - chart toggle behavior (series vs delta)
  - segment filter param mapping + resets
  - flag gating renders placeholder when off
- Playwright:
  - loads results page, applies time range + segment, opens example drawer

## Screenshots (internal)
- Results overview: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-overview
- Segment breakdown: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-segments
- Example drawer: https://console-staging.redwood.internal/screens/24947-example

## Rollout plan
1) Merge behind flag (this PR)
2) Enable for internal tenant only (ENG-5416)
3) Enable for 2 Dedicated beta customers after SRE signoff on scoring lag dashboards (ENG-5409)

## Checklist
- [x] Feature flag gating added
- [x] RBAC checked before showing example drilldowns
- [x] Empty/scoring-lag states
- [x] Segment dimensions constrained to allowlist
- [x] Basic Playwright coverage

## Related
- Backend APIs: PR #24933
- Dashboards: observability-pack PR #391
- ADRs: adr-shadow-metrics-data-model-and-cardinality, quality-metrics-observability-standard

---

### Commit summary
1. console: add shadow experiment results route + nav entry
2. console: implement quality delta chart + metric cards
3. console: add segment filter bar with dimension allowlist
4. console: add scored examples table + drawer (RBAC gated)
5. console: wire up API client + pagination + retries
6. console: add tests (unit + Playwright)

### Changed areas (high level)
- `console/apps/web/src/routes/shadowExperiments/ShadowExperimentResultsPage.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/components/quality/QualityDeltaChart.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/components/quality/ScoredExampleDrawer.tsx`
- `console/apps/web/src/api/shadowExperiments.ts`
- `console/apps/web/src/flags/featureFlags.ts`
- `console/apps/web/tests/playwright/shadowExperimentsResults.spec.ts`

Small illustrative snippet (flag gating):

if (!flags.shadow_experiments_results_ui) {
  return <ShadowExperimentsResultsPlaceholder />;
}

### CI
- web:lint ✅
- web:typecheck ✅
- web:test ✅
- web:playwright ✅ (reran once due to flake; see conversation)

### Review conversation (highlights)
Kenji Nakamura (requested changes): "Chart density is a bit high; can we default to 24h and keep 7d behind a secondary selector? Also the CI band needs a lighter tint to match the rest of the Console charts."

Dylan Brooks (author): "Updated defaults to 24h and moved 7d/30d into the time-range dropdown. Adjusted band opacity to 12% and used the `--chart-band` token. Pushed in 5f7c3b1."

Allison Grant: "Segment filter list looks safe but please hard-block arbitrary querystring dims; we’ve been burned by people copy/pasting links with `segment=prompt_id`."

Dylan Brooks: "Added allowlist validation + fallback to `none` with a toast. Also added a small unit test for invalid segment values."

Sean Gallagher: "Make sure example drawer doesn’t fetch examples automatically on page load; this can spike load when people share links."

Dylan Brooks: "Good call — examples now fetch only when the Examples tab is opened, and we cap initial load at 25 with explicit pagination."

Chloe Martin: "API client should pass `rubric_version` through for display, but don’t use it to branch UI logic yet."

Dylan Brooks: "Wired `rubric_version` into header display only. No conditional rendering."

Marissa Cole: "Can we add a callout making it clear this doesn’t affect user traffic and is asynchronous?" 

Dylan Brooks: "Added info callout under header: 'Shadow results are computed asynchronously and may lag by up to N minutes.' (N pulled from backend config)."

CI note: Playwright failed once with a timeout on opening the example drawer in staging; reran and passed. Added an extra wait on the skeleton -> content transition.

### Merge
Merged via squash into `main`.

source.title: Audit logs for shadow experiment + shadow policy changes
source.author: Kevin Osei
source.description:
Summary: Adds audit log entries for shadow experiment lifecycle actions (create/update/archive) and for route-attached shadow policy updates. This is part of Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics beta readiness: we need a clear who/what/when trail for experiment changes and policy toggles, especially for Dedicated/Private tenants with compliance requirements.

Context / Motivation: Shadow traffic is explicitly “fail-open” and non-user-impacting, but misconfiguration can still create meaningful cost/retention risk (sampling rate, targets, redaction flags). During dogfood and early customer discussions, we’ve been asked to provide auditable records for: (1) who created/changed an experiment, (2) who attached/detached shadow policies to routes/rollouts, and (3) what fields changed. This PR extends the existing AuditLog service to capture the relevant actions and adds minimal schema support for structured diffs.

What’s included: (1) New audit event types: SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_CREATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_ARCHIVED, SHADOW_POLICY_ATTACHED, SHADOW_POLICY_UPDATED, SHADOW_POLICY_DETACHED. (2) Console backend wiring: emits audit events from ShadowExperiments CRUD endpoints and from policy mutation endpoints used by rollouts. (3) Schema updates: adds resource_type = "shadow_experiment" | "shadow_policy" and resource_id fields, plus optional change_summary (field-level diff) with allowlisted keys to avoid leaking prompt/content. (4) RBAC + attribution: ensures actor is the authenticated user/service principal; includes org_id + project_id + environment in the audit payload for correlation in Private deployments. (5) Tests: unit tests for event emission and redaction/allowlist behavior; integration test verifies a mutation request produces a single audit row with stable event_type.

Not included / Follow-ups: No UI changes in Console for viewing these events (tracked separately). No backfill for historic changes prior to this PR. We intentionally do not log prompt content, model outputs, or header values; only configuration metadata and allowlisted field diffs.

Implementation notes: Audit payload fields are explicitly allowlisted. For SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, we capture changes for: name, description, sampling_rate, target_variants, rubric_bundle_id, and retention_mode. For shadow policy events, we capture: enabled, sampling_rate, shadow_targets (IDs only), fail_open, and timeout_ms. Anything else is recorded as "[redacted]" in change_summary.

Risk: Low. Audit emission is best-effort and does not block the write path (fail-open). If AuditLog is down/slow, we enqueue to the existing async audit buffer with bounded retries.

Validation: Ran console-backend unit tests + integration suite, and verified in staging that create/update/archive actions produce expected event types with correct resource_id. Confirmed no request body or prompt fields end up in the audit record.

Screenshots/Logs: Included in internal staging run (see test output in CI artifacts).

Release note (internal): Shadow experiments and shadow policies now generate audit log events on create/update/archive/attach/detach.
commits: auditlog: add shadow_* event types + resource typing
console: emit audit logs for shadow experiments CRUD
policy: emit audit logs for shadow policy attach/update/detach
auditlog: allowlisted change_summary + redaction guards
tests: add coverage for event payload + async failure handling
changed_areas: security/auditlog: event type registry + payload schema
source.conversation:
Marcus Lin (Security) - Requested changes: Can we guarantee we never log request bodies or prompt text? Also please ensure actor attribution is always the end-user when invoked via Console (not the internal service account), and include org_id/project_id in the audit payload.

Kevin Osei (author): Updated to compute diffs on the decoded config object rather than raw JSON; added allowlist and hard redaction for unknown keys. Actor now comes from the auth context (user_id/service_principal_id) with explicit precedence rules. Added org_id/project_id/environment to the base payload.

Chloe Martin (Console backend) - Requested changes: Please add an event for archive/disable (not just delete) since we’re using soft-delete for experiments in v1. Also, ensure we don’t double-log on idempotent updates.

Kevin Osei (author): Added SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_ARCHIVED event; wired to the archive endpoint. Added idempotency guard: only emits UPDATED when computed diff is non-empty.

Naomi Feldman (Compliance) - Comment: For Dedicated/Private customers we need to be able to export audit logs to SIEM; do we have stable resource_type/resource_id and a consistent event naming scheme?

Kevin Osei (author): Resource typing added and event types are in the shared registry; names are stable and documented inline. SIEM export uses the same audit_log table; this PR does not change export.

Jada Williams (Rollouts) - Comment: Policy events should include rollout_id when the change originates from a rollout config push.

Kevin Osei (author): Added optional rollout_id in the audit payload when present in request context; omitted otherwise.

CI notes: First run failed on migration lints (missing down migration). Fixed by adding down migration and re-running. Subsequent CI green.

Approvals: Marcus Lin approved after redaction/attribution updates. Chloe Martin approved after archive event + idempotency guard. Naomi Feldman approved with note to document event types in the security controls appendix (follow-up).

Risk: Low. Audit emission is best-effort and does not block the write path (fail-open). If AuditLog is down/slow, we enqueue to the existing async audit buffer with bounded retries.

Validation: Ran console-backend unit tests + integration suite, and verified in staging that create/update/archive actions produce expected event types with correct resource_id. Confirmed no request body or prompt fields end up in the audit record.

Screenshots/Logs: Included in internal staging run (see test output in CI artifacts).

Release note (internal): Shadow experiments and shadow policies now generate audit log events on create/update/archive/attach/detach.
text:
Summary: Adds audit log entries for shadow experiment lifecycle actions (create/update/archive) and for route-attached shadow policy updates. This is part of Shadow Traffic Quality Metrics beta readiness: we need a clear who/what/when trail for experiment changes and policy toggles, especially for Dedicated/Private tenants with compliance requirements.

Context / Motivation: Shadow traffic is explicitly “fail-open” and non-user-impacting, but misconfiguration can still create meaningful cost/retention risk (sampling rate, targets, redaction flags). During dogfood and early customer discussions, we’ve been asked to provide auditable records for: (1) who created/changed an experiment, (2) who attached/detached shadow policies to routes/rollouts, and (3) what fields changed. This PR extends the existing AuditLog service to capture the relevant actions and adds minimal schema support for structured diffs.

What’s included: (1) New audit event types: SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_CREATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_ARCHIVED, SHADOW_POLICY_ATTACHED, SHADOW_POLICY_UPDATED, SHADOW_POLICY_DETACHED. (2) Console backend wiring: emits audit events from ShadowExperiments CRUD endpoints and from policy mutation endpoints used by rollouts. (3) Schema updates: adds resource_type = "shadow_experiment" | "shadow_policy" and resource_id fields, plus optional change_summary (field-level diff) with allowlisted keys to avoid leaking prompt/content. (4) RBAC + attribution: ensures actor is the authenticated user/service principal; includes org_id + project_id + environment in the audit payload for correlation in Private deployments. (5) Tests: unit tests for event emission and redaction/allowlist behavior; integration test verifies a mutation request produces a single audit row with stable event_type.

Not included / Follow-ups: No UI changes in Console for viewing these events (tracked separately). No backfill for historic changes prior to this PR. We intentionally do not log prompt content, model outputs, or header values; only configuration metadata and allowlisted field diffs.

Implementation notes: Audit payload fields are explicitly allowlisted. For SHADOW_EXPERIMENT_UPDATED, we capture changes for: name, description, sampling_rate, target_variants, rubric_bundle_id, and retention_mode. For shadow policy events, we capture: enabled, sampling_rate, shadow_targets (IDs only), fail_open, and timeout_ms. Anything else is recorded as "[redacted]" in change_summary.

Risk: Low. Audit emission is best-effort and does not block the write path (fail-open). If AuditLog is down/slow, we enqueue to the existing async audit buffer with bounded retries.

Validation: Ran console-backend unit tests + integration suite, and verified in staging that create/update/archive actions produce expected event types with correct resource_id. Confirmed no request body or prompt fields end up in the audit record.

Screenshots/Logs: Included in internal staging run (see test output in CI artifacts).

Release note (internal): Shadow experiments and shadow policies now generate audit log events on create/update/archive/attach/detach.

source.title: Shadow traffic: telemetry events + ingestion hooks (v1)
source.author: Caleb Johnson
source.release_notes: Adds v1 shadow traffic telemetry event schemas (pair + scoring status), ingestion mapping into analytics tables, and stub dashboards/alerts for dogfood/beta validation.
source.description:
Context: Shadow traffic routing (ENG-5402) mirrors a sampled subset of prod requests to one or more shadow targets. To compute quality deltas (and to debug routing/scoring lag) we need a low-cardinality, privacy-aware set of telemetry events that (a) links the control and shadow inference outcomes, (b) can be ingested reliably in Hosted/Dedicated/Private, and (c) provides enough dimensions to slice by route/model/region without exploding metrics cardinality.

This PR introduces shadow traffic telemetry events and wires them into the ingestion pipeline (Kafka -> ingest -> warehouse tables) with initial dashboard stubs for internal dogfood.

What’s included: (1) New event schemas: shadow_pair_created (emitted when the mirror is scheduled), shadow_inference_completed (shadow target returned or timed out), and shadow_scoring_status (async scoring queued/completed/failed). Each event includes a stable shadow_pair_id and request_fingerprint (HMAC), plus required dimensions: tenant_id (hashed), route_id (low-card), control_model_id, shadow_target_id, deployment_mode, region, outcome, and timing fields. (2) Cardinality controls: disallow freeform tags in shadow telemetry, bucket user_agent, truncate prompt_length/response_length into fixed buckets, and explicitly drop prompt_id / conversation_id at emit-time. Route-level and model-level dimensions are included; anything higher-card must be attached via example sampling endpoints later (out of scope). (3) Ingestion pipeline hooks: adds validators/normalizers for the new events, forwards them through the existing ingest service, and creates the initial storage tables shadow_pairs_v1 and shadow_scoring_v1 (partitioned by day, clustered by tenant_hash + route_id). (4) Initial dashboards stubs: adds basic Grafana JSON stubs for shadow throughput, shadow target error rate, and scoring lag; these are intentionally minimal and will be expanded in observability-pack PR-391.

PII / data handling: This PR does not store raw prompts/responses. request_fingerprint is computed using an HMAC keyed per environment (see config), and tenant_id is hashed before leaving the data plane. Any headers included are allowlisted (content-type, accept, x-redwood-route) and explicitly exclude auth/cookie-like fields.

Testing: Unit tests for schema validation and normalization, golden tests for event-to-table mapping, and an end-to-end ingest test that emits a synthetic shadow_pair_created + shadow_inference_completed and asserts the warehouse row shape. Load testing / overhead benchmarking is tracked in ENG-5412.

Rollout plan: gated behind feature flag shadow_telemetry_v1 (default off). Dogfood tenants can enable via internal config. Fail-open: telemetry emission failures do not impact request path; ingestion lag only affects dashboards/metrics.

Follow-ups: (a) scoring payload references once redaction pipeline lands (ENG-5411), (b) additional aggregation views for confidence intervals, (c) tighten SLO alert thresholds once we have baseline dogfood data.

Notes for reviewers: please focus on (1) schema field choices and defaults, (2) cardinality guardrails, and (3) whether the ingestion mapping matches the query patterns we expect for Console drilldowns.
source.commits_summary: 1) telemetry: add shadow traffic event schemas + validators 2) ingest: add normalizers + routing to shadow_* tables 3) analytics: create table definitions + partitions/clustering 4) dashboards: add stub panels for throughput, error rate, scoring lag 5) tests: golden mapping + e2e ingest coverage
source.changed_areas: Telemetry event schemas, ingest service validators/normalizers, analytics table mappings, Grafana dashboard stubs, test fixtures
source.illustrative_snippet: Event dimensions intentionally limited to reduce cardinality. Example (normalized row): tenant_hash=sha256(tenant_id), route_id, control_model_id, shadow_target_id, region, deployment_mode, outcome in {ok, timeout, error}, latency_ms_bucket in {<100,100-250,250-500,500-1000,>1000}, prompt_tokens_bucket in {<128,128-512,512-2048,>2048}. request_fingerprint is HMAC-SHA256 over (route_id, canonicalized_messages, tool_schema_hash) with env key; raw text never leaves the data plane.
review_conversation: Nadia Rahman: Can we confirm we’re not emitting route_name anywhere? We’ve had issues when names include customer-provided slugs.
Caleb Johnson: Correct—route_name is dropped at emit-time; only route_id (UUID) is included. I also added a validator to hard-fail if route_name shows up in the event payload.
Allison Grant: The dashboard stubs look fine, but the scoring lag panel should use shadow_scoring_status rather than inference_completed. Otherwise we’ll misinterpret queue backlog as compute time.
Caleb Johnson: Good catch. Updated the stub query to compute lag from queued_at -> completed_at in shadow_scoring_v1. Left inference_completed for shadow target health panels.
Sean Gallagher: Please add a hard cap on emitted dimensions to protect metrics. Also, we need a way to detect when someone accidentally adds a high-card tag in the future.

Caleb Johnson: Added an allowlist-based dimension serializer + a unit test that asserts the final dimension set. Also wired a counter shadow_telemetry_dropped_fields_total when we drop unknown fields (for visibility).
Marcus Lin: HMAC key management: where is the secret stored for Private deployments?

Caleb Johnson: For Hosted/Dedicated it’s in our standard secrets store. For Private, it’s derived from the customer’s control-plane secret bundle; doc note added and defaults to rotate-on-upgrade. Implementation details also referenced in config comments; Security review tracked in ENG-5411 follow-up.
source.post_merge_notes: After merge, dogfood tenants should enable feature flag shadow_telemetry_v1 and validate: (1) shadow_pairs_v1 row counts match expected sampling rate, (2) no cardinality alerts triggered, (3) scoring lag panel remains stable under backlog. If cardinality alerts fire, see SUP-28510 notes and reduce dimensions before expanding sampling.

source.title: Add tier/deployment_mode tags to SLI metrics at ingress (and propagate through request context)
source.author: Amara Diallo
source.description: Context: As part of the Tiered SLO + Error Budget program, we need to compute SLIs (success rate, latency, streaming reliability) separately for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved capacity). Today most gateway metrics are emitted without a reliable tier signal, and routing/fallback can further obscure the original customer tier in downstream services. This PR adds tier/deployment mode tags to ingress SLI metrics and ensures the tags are attached to the request context so they can be propagated into routing, traces, and downstream metrics. Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a canonical tier classifier at ingress that maps requests into tier={hosted|dedicated} and deployment_mode={hosted_api|dedicated_pool} (naming aligned with ADR-0142 draft). (2) Add these tags to the gateway SLI metric set for request count, request duration histograms, and streaming termination counters. (3) Propagate tier/deployment_mode via RequestContext (headers -> internal ctx) so routing can preserve tier across fallback/region-shift decisions and telemetry can emit consistent tags. (4) Add guardrails to avoid cardinality explosions: we do NOT add customer_id/model_id labels at ingress; those remain sampled/derived in dedicated dashboards only, behind existing access controls. (5) Provide migration notes for dashboards/recording rules: existing queries using legacy label "pool" or "account_tier" should switch to tier/deployment_mode; a compatibility recording rule is included for a 2-week deprecation window. Details: Metrics affected include api_gateway_request_total, api_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket, api_gateway_stream_termination_total, and api_gateway_errors_total. For streaming, we tag the termination reason classification at the gateway boundary (client_abort vs server_disconnect vs upstream_error) using the existing SSE/WebSocket close signals and a small set of heuristics; only server_disconnect and upstream_error count against streaming reliability SLI (client_abort excluded per the SLI spec). Rollout plan: This ships behind config flag telemetry.tier_tagging.enabled (default off). We’ll enable in staging for 48h, then canary 5% prod, then 100% per-region (us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1) once dashboards show stable label coverage. After rollout, we will remove the compatibility recording rule and update remaining dashboards/alerts in follow-up PRs. Operational notes: If tier tagging is missing, the request is tagged tier=unknown and deployment_mode=unknown; alerts/dashboards should exclude unknown from SLO accounting (see migration note). This PR intentionally does not change SLO math; it only makes the tier dimension available and consistently propagated.
changed_areas: api-gateway/telemetry: tier classifier + metric label injection
api-gateway/request-context: add TierInfo to context + propagate to routing client
docs/internal: dashboard/query migration notes + rollout checklist
commit_summary: api-gateway: add TierClassifier (hosted vs dedicated) and expose in RequestContext
telemetry: tag SLI metrics with tier + deployment_mode; add unknown fallback
streaming: classify termination reasons and tag stream termination metric
routing-client: propagate tier fields over internal RPC metadata
prometheus: add temporary recording rule mapping legacy labels to tier/deployment_mode
docs: add dashboard/query migration notes + rollout steps
source.migration_notes: Dashboard/query changes: 1) Replace label filters {pool="dedicated"} or {account_tier="dedicated"} with {tier="dedicated"}. 2) Where dashboards previously inferred tier from route_name prefixes, switch to tier/deployment_mode; keep route_name only for drill-down. 3) For SLI accounting, exclude tier="unknown" until rollout reaches 100% in the region: e.g., sum(rate(api_gateway_request_total{tier!="unknown"}[5m])). 4) The compatibility recording rule sli:api_gateway_request_total:by_tier will exist for ~2 weeks to bridge old dashboards; after that, dashboards must query the source metrics directly with tier/deployment_mode. Known caveat: Dedicated requests that enter via older enterprise gateway pods without the feature flag will emit unknown; this is expected during phased rollout and should not page by itself.
review_conversation: Allison Grant: Can you confirm the SLI boundaries here? Specifically, are we tagging at the first point we consider "ingress" for SLO accounting (after auth + request validation), and are we excluding 4xx/429s from the same counter series or separate? I want to ensure we don’t accidentally count invalid requests against tier budgets.

Amara Diallo (author): Yes—metrics are emitted after auth/tenant resolution and request schema validation. We keep existing SLI semantics: 4xx and 429 are emitted but excluded by SLO queries (unchanged). This PR only adds tier/deployment_mode labels; it does not change the HTTP code classification logic. I added a note in the migration doc and a small comment in metrics.ts to make this explicit.

Thomas Baird: Please double check cardinality. Adding tier is fine, but deployment_mode might be redundant. Any chance this becomes a free-form value (e.g., VPC/onprem) later and we explode series count?

Amara Diallo (author): Good call. deployment_mode is an enum with a hard-coded allowlist in tier_classifier.ts (hosted_api|dedicated_pool|unknown). I added a unit test to ensure unrecognized values map to unknown, and the internal propagation uses the same enum type. Private/VPC will be handled later but will require explicit enum expansion + review.

Mina Haddad: Propagation into routing: do we preserve tier through fallback? Some of our fallback paths create a new context. If the routing client drops tier, downstream SLO accounting will be wrong.

Amara Diallo (author): We now attach TierInfo to RequestContext and pass it through the routing-client metadata. Routing can still override model/region, but the tier remains the original ingress tier. I also added a regression test in routing-client to ensure TierInfo is forwarded even when fallback_policy is set.
Logan Wright: Telemetry side: do we need to emit tier on traces too? It’d be useful to slice exemplars by tier in the tracing UI.

Amara Diallo (author): Already included—RequestContext attaches tier/deployment_mode as span attributes on the gateway root span. Downstream services that consume our context will inherit and can add to their spans. Not changing any sampling policies here.

Allison Grant: One more request: please add a short "unknown" handling note + recommended dashboard exclusion filter. We’ve had confusion before when partial rollouts made graphs look "better" due to missing labels.
Amara Diallo (author): Added to migration notes section (exclude tier=unknown during rollout) and called out in the PR description. Thanks.
source.release_notes: Internal: Gateway SLI metrics now include tier and deployment_mode labels to support tiered SLO/error budget dashboards. Dashboards should migrate to the new labels; see docs/observability/tiered-slos/migration-notes-ingress-tier-tags.md.

source.title: Packed INT8 microkernel rewrite and token coalescer to improve throughput for small-batch inference
source.author: Monica Hart
source.release_notes: Added a packed INT8 microkernel and runtime token coalescer. Improves INT8 throughput by 18-32% on small-batch workloads while preserving output parity; exposes runtime toggles for controlled rollout. No API breaking changes.
source.description:
Context and motivation: small-batch interactive workloads (chat, per-request short-context generation) show poor INT8 utilization because microkernel packing and per-token scheduling produce many narrow GEMM shapes and high per-call overhead. Prior batching heuristics mitigated this at the request layer but left inefficiencies inside the kernel and scheduling layer. This PR introduces two coordinated changes to address that: (1) a packed INT8 microkernel optimized for narrow micro-tiles and reduced memory traffic, and (2) a runtime token coalescer that merges short token sequences across concurrent requests into aligned micro-batches at the kernel boundary.

Design summary:
- Packed INT8 microkernel: new micro-tiling layout (packed 4x8 major order) that reduces memory stride and enables vector-friendly inner loops; fused requantization step merged into the GEMM write-back to avoid a full dequant/q-requant pass for common patterns; safe FP32/FP8 fallback path for layers where requant scales exceed per-tensor range.
- Token coalescer scheduler: lightweight aggregator in the continuous batching pipeline that groups tokens by model variant and sequence length bucket, enforcing alignment to the microkernel tile size. Coalescer is conservative: it only aggregates tokens within configurable latency budget and respects per-tenant isolation.
- Runtime controls and telemetry: feature gates runtime.packed_int8.enabled and runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms; new metrics token_coalescer.groups, microkernel_tile_util, packed_int8_path_ratio.

Commits (high-level):
- feat(kernels): add packed int8 microkernel entrypoints and assembly fallback (3 commits)
- perf(scheduler): token coalescer hook into continuous batching loop (2 commits)
- test: unit + fuzz parity tests for INT8 path (2 commits)
- bench: add small-batch harness + CI microbench (1 commit)
- docs: runtime flags and rollout notes (1 commit)

Files touched (high level):
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.cc
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.h
- asm/packed_int8_asm.S (platform-guarded implementations)
- src/runtime/scheduler/token_coalescer.cc
- src/runtime/scheduler/continuous_batching_integration.cc
- tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc
- benchmarks/bench_small_batch_packed_int8.py
- docs/runtime/flags.md
- examples/packed_int8_quickstart.md
- ci/microbench.yml
- build/BUILD.bazel (kernel entries)
- scripts/perf/compare_a100_t4.sh

Implementation notes:
- Microkernel implements a fused write-back that applies the per-channel scale and zero point during the GEMM output path; this removed one intermediate tensor allocation in the hot loop.
- We chose a 4x8 tile because it balances register pressure and memory coalescing on both A100 and T4; hand-tuned inner loops for AVX3 and CUDA mma-style intrinsics where available.
- Token coalescer hooks before the KV-cache dispatch: it accumulates tokens by (model_id, variant, seq_bucket) and emits coalesced batches up to the microkernel tile alignment or latency budget.

Correctness and parity:
- Added deterministic parity tests that compare outputs vs FP32 reference on representative micro-batches and randomized seeds.
- FP8 fallback uses an extra path that quantizes to FP8 when per-tensor scale fits; otherwise falls back to per-channel INT8 with a narrow requant path to preserve numerical fidelity.

Benchmarks (representative, run on on-prem clusters):
- A100 (8x latency-bound small queries): baseline INT8 path throughput 1250 tokens/s -> packed-int8 + coalescer 1650 tokens/s (+32%).
- T4 (mixed small-batch): baseline 740 -> 915 tokens/s (+23%).
- Median end-to-end p95 latency change: -4% (slightly improved due to fewer memory copies).
- Token-coalescer shows larger wins for <8-token requests; negligible impact for large batches.

Review conversation (selected excerpts, chronological):
- Ethan Park (review): "Can you add a microbench in CI that asserts no regression in token-level throughput on T4? Also needs a non-regression accuracy test (FP32 vs INT8) for 2M random tokens."
- Author (Monica): "Added microbench.yml and parity test; microbench is gated on an opt-in perf job to avoid CI costs. See tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc."
- Sofia Ruiz (review): "Concern: coalescer may increase tail latency for single-tenant extremes. We should expose latency_ms threshold and default to 5ms; also add tenant opt-out."
- Author: "Exposed runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms with default 5, and runtime.token_coalescer.enable_per_tenant=false; added docs. Token coalescer will never coalesce across tenant boundaries by default."
- Jasper Li (review): "Assembly fallback looks good, but please add a safety check for AVX availability and a clean fallback to scalar path."
- Author: "Implemented CPU capability checks and unit tests for the scalar fallback path."
- CI pipeline: initial run failed due to clang-format on packed_int8_kernel.h and a flaky perf test; lint fixes and re-run produced green CI: unit tests 245/245 passed; microbench opt-in run showed throughput numbers above."

Rollout and rollback plan:
- Feature gates available via runtime flags; rollout to 10% of hosted traffic for first week with telemetry checks.
- Automated regression alerts on model quality (per-prompt edit distance) and token-level checksum parity will trigger immediate rollback.
- If severe regressions occur, we can disable runtime.packed_int8.enabled remotely and revert scheduler hook.

Risk assessment:
- Numerical drift: parity tests and FP8 fallback minimize risk; however, rare edge cases may see small token-level differences—these are surfaced to evals automatically.
- Multi-tenant fairness: coalescer respects tenant isolation; added opt-out flag for customers that prefer per-request isolation.

Follow-ups and TODO:
- Explore widening tile shapes for large-batch paths and auto-tune tile size per-GPU.
- Add cooperative scheduling with dedicated capacity autoscaler to avoid starvation when coalescer groups many small requests.
- Port microkernel to the Windows CUDA toolchain (currently Linux-only guarded).

Merge notes:
- PR was squashed on merge.
- Merged by Ethan Park after approvals and green CI.
- Associated Linear cards closed: ENG-8421, ENG-8484.
- Release: rollout will be gated and documented under v0.67 runtime release notes.

title: Packed INT8 microkernel rewrite and token coalescer to improve throughput for small-batch inference
preview:
Context and motivation: small-batch interactive workloads (chat, per-request short-context generation) show poor INT8 utilization because microkernel packing and per-token scheduling produce many narrow GEMM shapes and high per-call overhead. Prior batching heuristics mitigated this at the request layer but left inefficiencies inside the kernel and scheduling layer. This PR introduces two coordinated changes to address that: (1) a packed INT8 microkernel optimized for narrow micro-tiles and reduced memory traffic, and (2) a runtime token coalescer that merges short token sequences across concurrent requests into aligned micro-batches at the kernel boundary.

Design summary:
- Packed INT8 microkernel: new micro-tiling layout (packed 4x8 major order) that reduces memory stride and enables vector-friendly inner loops; fused requantization step merged into the GEMM write-back to avoid a full dequant/q-requant pass for common patterns; safe FP32/FP8 fallback path for layers where requant scales exceed per-tensor range.
- Token coalescer scheduler: lightweight aggregator in the continuous batching pipeline that groups tokens by model variant and sequence length bucket, enforcing alignment to the microkernel tile size. Coalescer is conservative: it only aggregates tokens within configurable latency budget and respects per-tenant isolation.
- Runtime controls and telemetry: feature gates runtime.packed_int8.enabled and runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms; new metrics token_coalescer.groups, microkernel_tile_util, packed_int8_path_ratio.

Commits (high-level):
- feat(kernels): add packed int8 microkernel entrypoints and assembly fallback (3 commits)
- perf(scheduler): token coalescer hook into continuous batching loop (2 commits)
- test: unit + fuzz parity tests for INT8 path (2 commits)
- bench: add small-batch harness + CI microbench (1 commit)
- docs: runtime flags and rollout notes (1 commit)

Files touched (high level):
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.cc
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.h
- asm/packed_int8_asm.S (platform-guarded implementations)
- src/runtime/scheduler/token_coalescer.cc
- src/runtime/scheduler/continuous_batching_integration.cc
- tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc
- benchmarks/bench_small_batch_packed_int8.py
- docs/runtime/flags.md
- examples/packed_int8_quickstart.md
- ci/microbench.yml
- build/BUILD.bazel (kernel entries)
- scripts/perf/compare_a100_t4.sh

Implementation notes:
- Microkernel implements a fused write-back that applies the per-channel scale and zero point during the GEMM output path; this removed one intermediate tensor allocation in the hot loop.
- We chose a 4x8 tile because it balances register pressure and memory coalescing on both A100 and T4; hand-tuned inner loops for AVX3 and CUDA mma-style intrinsics where available.
- Token coalescer hooks before the KV-cache dispatch: it accumulates tokens by (model_id, variant, seq_bucket) and emits coalesced batches up to the microkernel tile alignment or latency budget.

Correctness and parity:
- Added deterministic parity tests that compare outputs vs FP32 reference on representative micro-batches and randomized seeds.
- FP8 fallback uses an extra path that quantizes to FP8 when per-tensor scale fits; otherwise falls back to per-channel INT8 with a narrow requant path to preserve numerical fidelity.

Benchmarks (representative, run on on-prem clusters):
- A100 (8x latency-bound small queries): baseline INT8 path throughput 1250 tokens/s -> packed-int8 + coalescer 1650 tokens/s (+32%).
- T4 (mixed small-batch): baseline 740 -> 915 tokens/s (+23%).
- Median end-to-end p95 latency change: -4% (slightly improved due to fewer memory copies).
- Token-coalescer shows larger wins for <8-token requests; negligible impact for large batches.

Review conversation (selected excerpts, chronological):
- Ethan Park (review): "Can you add a microbench in CI that asserts no regression in token-level throughput on T4? Also needs a non-regression accuracy test (FP32 vs INT8) for 2M random tokens."
- Author (Monica): "Added microbench.yml and parity test; microbench is gated on an opt-in perf job to avoid CI costs. See tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc."
- Sofia Ruiz (review): "Concern: coalescer may increase tail latency for single-tenant extremes. We should expose latency_ms threshold and default to 5ms; also add tenant opt-out."
- Author: "Exposed runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms with default 5, and runtime.token_coalescer.enable_per_tenant=false; added docs. Token coalescer will never coalesce across tenant boundaries by default."
- Jasper Li (review): "Assembly fallback looks good, but please add a safety check for AVX availability and a clean fallback to scalar path."
- Author: "Implemented CPU capability checks and unit tests for the scalar fallback path."
- CI pipeline: initial run failed due to clang-format on packed_int8_kernel.h and a flaky perf test; lint fixes and re-run produced green CI: unit tests 245/245 passed; microbench opt-in run showed throughput numbers above."

Rollout and rollback plan:
- Feature gates available via runtime flags; rollout to 10% of hosted traffic for first week with telemetry checks.
- Automated regression alerts on model quality (per-prompt edit distance) and token-level checksum parity will trigger immediate rollback.
- If severe regressions occur, we can disable runtime.packed_int8.enabled remotely and revert scheduler hook.

Risk assessment:
- Numerical drift: parity tests and FP8 fallback minimize risk; however, rare edge cases may see small token-level differences—these are surfaced to evals automatically.
- Multi-tenant fairness: coalescer respects tenant isolation; added opt-out flag for customers that prefer per-request isolation.

Follow-ups and TODO:
- Explore widening tile shapes for large-batch paths and auto-tune tile size per-GPU.
- Add cooperative scheduling with dedicated capacity autoscaler to avoid starvation when coalescer groups many small requests.
- Port microkernel to the Windows CUDA toolchain (currently Linux-only guarded).

- Merged by Ethan Park after approvals and green CI.
- Associated Linear cards closed: ENG-8421, ENG-8484.
- Release: rollout will be gated and documented under v0.67 runtime release notes.

source.title: Drift‑resilient collective scheduler and KV slab consolidation to reduce NCCL contention
source.author: Maya Singh
source.description:
Context and motivation: In production we continue to see sporadic tail latency spikes on multi‑GPU TP workloads correlated with short windows of NCCL congestion and many small KV cache allocations from continuous batching. This PR introduces two coordinated runtime changes: (1) a drift‑resilient collective scheduler that perturbs and staggers NCCL launches to avoid pathological synchronization across tensor slices, and (2) a KV slab consolidation pass that reduces small allocation churn and improves L2/L3 locality for the KV cache allocator. The combined change targets multi‑GPU tensor‑parallel inference paths (TP runtime) and the continuous‑batching code path used by the API router.

What this PR does (high level): - Adds Scheduler::PerturbationWindow, a lightweight jittering layer that applies per‑peer launch offsets within a configurable window to break lockstep NCCL launches while preserving ordering and progress guarantees. The scheduler also tracks per‑peer outstanding credits and backs off launches when cumulative outstanding bytes exceed a runtime threshold. - Adds KVSlab::Consolidate and a consolidation task that merges hot small slabs into larger contiguous slabs at low priority, reducing per‑token allocation overhead and fragmentation. Consolidation is gated by memory pressure and configurable thresholds so it won’t interfere with SLOs. - Adds a small admission tweak to continuous batching: older requests receive a priority boost in the batch selector to reduce head‑of‑line latency under high QPS. - Benchmarks and regression tests: microbenchmarks for NCCL launch variance, end‑to‑end tp inference perf harness, and an integration test that exercises slab consolidation under load.

Design notes and rationale: - We intentionally avoid deterministic reordering. The perturbation window is randomized per launch but bounded; this prevents pathological worst‑case alignments while keeping collectives efficient. - Consolidation is incremental and infrequent; it uses a background worker with a budgeted runtime slice to avoid interfering with token service. - The NCCL perturbation is conservative: on rigs where we observe no contention, the perturbation window defaults to zero. Operators can tune the window via runtime flags and per‑cluster policies. - The combined effect reduces contention-caused retries and kernel stalls while also lowering memory allocation overhead that exacerbates NVLink traffic.

Commits (summary): - 1a2f3b4: Introduce perturbation scheduler hooks and config knobs; add unit tests for scheduling invariants. - 2b3c4d5: Implement KV slab consolidation pass, background worker, and new allocator stats. - 3c4d5e6: Integrate with continuous batching admission, add perf test harness and perf metrics, update docs and changelog.

Files changed (high level): - runtime/scheduler/perturbation_scheduler.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/kv_slab_consolidator.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/allocator_stats.{cc,h} - runtime/batching/batch_selector.{cc} (priority tweak) - tests/perf/tp_nccl_stress_test.py - ci/perf/benchmarks/launch_variance.yaml - docs/release_notes/2026-02-03-kv-nccl-optimization.md - examples/perf/tuning/README.md

Tiny illustrative snippet (informational only): "Scheduler::PerturbationWindow" accepts a window_ns and seed; when >0 it computes offset = rng.uniform(0, window_ns) and schedules NCCL launch at planned_time + offset. KVSlab::Consolidate scans slab list for contiguous small slabs with same owner and merges them under lock.

Review conversation (highlights): - Ethan Park (reviewer): "Nice isolation of perturbation logic. A few requests: make the default window configurable via env var, and add a per‑node debug metric for perturbed launches." Author reply: "Added RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS env and metric runtime.scheduler.perturbed_launches_count; updated tests." - Lucia Ramirez: "Concern about consolidation causing a stop‑the‑world when merging many small slabs." Author reply: "Consolidation uses a background budgeted worker; added unit test and a runtime flag to disable consolidation during low latency SLO periods." - Owen Patel: "Can we get a perf comparison on a 8xA100 TP run?" Author reply: "Included 8x A100 numbers below; will add CI perf job once this merges." - Followups: minor doc edits, one flaky test disabled and a TODO added to re-enable after allocator lock refactor.

CI summary: - Linux unit tests: pass - Integration perf harness (short): pass - Lint: pass - Extended perf run (internal): nominal but required one rerun due to infra transient. Overall status: pass.

Benchmarks (selected results, end‑to‑end TP inference harness, 8x A100, mixed sequence lengths): - Baseline (main): median throughput = 1000 infer/s, p99 latency = 210ms, peak NVLink utilization = 78% - This PR: median throughput = 1140 infer/s (+14%), p99 latency = 185ms (-11.9%), peak NVLink utilization = 66% (-15.4%) - KV memory fragmentation (tracked via allocator_stats.hot_slab_frag): baseline 0.18, this PR 0.09 (-50%) Notes: numbers averaged across 20 runs with warm‑up. Workloads with many small prefixes saw larger gains.

Backward compatibility and operator impact: - Defaults keep behavior unchanged (window defaults to 0, consolidation disabled under heavy SLO by default). - Operators can enable the window and consolidation via flags or cluster policy in the console. - Added new runtime metrics and two flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS and RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED documented in docs/release_notes.

Follow‑ups and TODOs: - Add an operator dashboard tile to surface perturbed launch counts and consolidation activity. - Extend consolidation heuristics to account for pinned memory slabs. - Add a canary rollout in Dedicated environments to validate across different network topologies.

Release notes (short): "Introduce a drift‑resilient collective scheduler and background KV slab consolidation to reduce NCCL contention and small allocation overhead; typical throughput gains 10–15% on multi‑GPU TP workloads and reduced p99 latency. Flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS, RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED."

Related: ENG-1284 (collective tail latency task), ENG-1301 (kv allocator fragmentation).
source.commits:
1a2f3b4 Introduce perturbation scheduler hooks
2b3c4d5 Implement KV slab consolidation and allocator stats
3c4d5e6 Integrate with continuous batching, add perf tests and docs
source.review_comments: Ethan: add env var and metric; Lucia: ensure consolidation is non‑blocking; Owen: provide 8x A100 perf numbers. All addressed in followups.
source.benchmarks: 8x A100: +14% median throughput, -11.9% p99 latency, -15% NVLink peak utilization; fragmentation -50%
source.release_notes: Introduce drift‑resilient collective scheduler and KV slab consolidation. Default behavior unchanged; enable via RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS and RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED. Typical multi‑GPU throughput +10–15% and p99 latency reduction.
source.notes_for_ops: Runtime flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS (ns), RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED (bool). Metrics added: runtime.scheduler.perturbed_launches_count, runtime.kv.consolidations_count, runtime.kv.hot_slab_frag. Recommended canary: Dedicated 8x node pool for 48h before wide rollout.
