Commits (summary): - 1a2f3b4: Introduce perturbation scheduler hooks and config knobs; add unit tests for scheduling invariants. - 2b3c4d5: Implement KV slab consolidation pass, background worker, and new allocator stats. - 3c4d5e6: Integrate with continuous batching admission, add perf test harness and perf metrics, update docs and changelog.

Files changed (high level): - runtime/scheduler/perturbation_scheduler.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/kv_slab_consolidator.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/allocator_stats.{cc,h} - runtime/batching/batch_selector.{cc} (priority tweak) - tests/perf/tp_nccl_stress_test.py - ci/perf/benchmarks/launch_variance.yaml - docs/release_notes/2026-02-03-kv-nccl-optimization.md - examples/perf/tuning/README.md

Tiny illustrative snippet (informational only): "Scheduler::PerturbationWindow" accepts a window_ns and seed; when >0 it computes offset = rng.uniform(0, window_ns) and schedules NCCL launch at planned_time + offset. KVSlab::Consolidate scans slab list for contiguous small slabs with same owner and merges them under lock.

Review conversation (highlights): - Ethan Park (reviewer): "Nice isolation of perturbation logic. A few requests: make the default window configurable via env var, and add a per‑node debug metric for perturbed launches." Author reply: "Added RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS env and metric runtime.scheduler.perturbed_launches_count; updated tests." - Lucia Ramirez: "Concern about consolidation causing a stop‑the‑world when merging many small slabs." Author reply: "Consolidation uses a background budgeted worker; added unit test and a runtime flag to disable consolidation during low latency SLO periods." - Owen Patel: "Can we get a perf comparison on a 8xA100 TP run?" Author reply: "Included 8x A100 numbers below; will add CI perf job once this merges." - Followups: minor doc edits, one flaky test disabled and a TODO added to re-enable after allocator lock refactor.

CI summary: - Linux unit tests: pass - Integration perf harness (short): pass - Lint: pass - Extended perf run (internal): nominal but required one rerun due to infra transient. Overall status: pass.

Benchmarks (selected results, end‑to‑end TP inference harness, 8x A100, mixed sequence lengths): - Baseline (main): median throughput = 1000 infer/s, p99 latency = 210ms, peak NVLink utilization = 78% - This PR: median throughput = 1140 infer/s (+14%), p99 latency = 185ms (-11.9%), peak NVLink utilization = 66% (-15.4%) - KV memory fragmentation (tracked via allocator_stats.hot_slab_frag): baseline 0.18, this PR 0.09 (-50%) Notes: numbers averaged across 20 runs with warm‑up. Workloads with many small prefixes saw larger gains.

Backward compatibility and operator impact: - Defaults keep behavior unchanged (window defaults to 0, consolidation disabled under heavy SLO by default). - Operators can enable the window and consolidation via flags or cluster policy in the console. - Added new runtime metrics and two flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS and RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED documented in docs/release_notes.

Follow‑ups and TODOs: - Add an operator dashboard tile to surface perturbed launch counts and consolidation activity. - Extend consolidation heuristics to account for pinned memory slabs. - Add a canary rollout in Dedicated environments to validate across different network topologies.

Release notes (short): "Introduce a drift‑resilient collective scheduler and background KV slab consolidation to reduce NCCL contention and small allocation overhead; typical throughput gains 10–15% on multi‑GPU TP workloads and reduced p99 latency. Flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS, RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED."

Related: ENG-1284 (collective tail latency task), ENG-1301 (kv allocator fragmentation).

source.title: Deprecate legacy stream shims; add credential lookup facade and unify SDK iterables
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Introduce Credential Lookup Facade to unify auth across Python/TS/Go SDKs; deprecate legacy stream shims. Non-breaking ergonomics improvements; shims remain for one release. Bump SDKs: py 3.9.14 -> 3.10.0, ts 1.27.0 -> 1.28.0, go v1.8.0 -> v1.9.0.
source.description: Context: Multiple SDK consumers reported subtle differences in auth lookup timing and streaming iterator shapes across languages. The legacy 'stream shims' layer introduced in earlier rollouts has become a maintenance burden and makes cross-language parity work harder. Motivation: Reduce duplicated auth lookup code, surface a single lightweight facade for credential resolution that all SDKs call into, and simplify streaming APIs to a single async iterator/pull-generator mapping with clearer backpressure hints. What this PR does: 1) Adds a Credential Lookup Facade (sdk/common/cred_lookup) that centralizes token resolution, TTL jitter logic, and prefetch hints used by the Python, TypeScript, and Go SDKs. 2) Reworks streaming surface so each SDK exports a single ergonomic async/pull iterator that maps to the runtime streaming protocol; legacy shims (StreamShimV1) are deprecated and left as compatibility wrappers that call the new facade. 3) Adds a small migration compatibility layer for applications that relied on StreamShimV1 side effects; the layer emits deprecation warnings and forwards calls. 4) Harmonizes default retry profiles for auth failures when streaming (fast-fail then background prefetch) and documents new default presets. 5) Bumps SDK versions and updates changelogs and examples in the quickstarts repo. Testing and validation: unit tests across all three SDKs, end-to-end smoke tests using the live test matrix, and cross-language parity test harness; fixed two flaky tests related to heartbeat timing. Rollout plan: merged to main and will be published behind feature flags for hosted customers for two weeks, then SDK releases will flip the default. Migration notes: existing apps continue to work with the provided shim; consumers should switch to the new iterator surface and call cred_lookup.lookup(tokenHints) for advanced prefetch semantics. Backwards compatibility window: one release cycle for the shim; plan to remove StreamShimV1 in three months with a dedicated deprecation announcement.
commits: c1f3d2e: introduce common credential lookup facade and interface
b7a4a90: refactor python async iterator to call cred_lookup and emit deprecation warning for StreamShimV1
d4e1b11: typescript: consolidate stream adapters and export single async generator; update docs
e832a55: go: add cred lookup package, refactor pullstream to simplified iterator interface
f1c9b22: tests: add crosslang parity harness and fix heartbeat timing flakes
a0b2c3d: docs and changelogs; bump SDK versions and examples
review_comments: Javier Morales: Overall this looks good, thanks for consolidating. Two asks: (1) can we expose a public TypeScript type for the prefetch hints? (2) Add a short example showing swapping prefetch strategy at runtime.
Maya Chen (author): Added PrefetchHints type in ts SDK and included a small runtime example under sdk-examples/streaming/ts/example_prefetch.ts. Responded to both asks in the final commit.
Priya Natarajan: The go lookup interface needs clearer error handling when KMS is unavailable. Suggest returning typed sentinel errors and a retryable flag.
Maya Chen: Updated go/cred/lookup.go to return (Token, LookupErr) with LookupErr.Reason and .Retryable. Added unit tests for KMS unavailable scenario.
Elena Rossi: Docs: expand migration.md to include sample curl commands and clear deprecation timeline. Also, please add a short blurb in the console release notes.
Maya Chen: Expanded quickstarts/streaming/migration.md and added release note blurb in docs/sdk/release_notes.md.
source.merge: squash and merge by Priya Natarajan
source.notes_on_migration: Developers should: 1) switch to the new iterator exports (Python: StreamingIterator, TS: createAsyncStream, Go: StreamIterator) and call cred_lookup.lookup when they need advanced prefetching; 2) remove StreamShimV1 usage after one release; 3) opt into the new retry profile via SDK config: { authRetry: 'prefetchThenFail' } to get the new behavior. Backward shim emits deprecation warnings logged once per process.
discussion_threads: Design thread: rationale for facade vs direct SDK duplication - decision notes and benchmarks attached to ENG-8245.
Performance thread: measured 7-12% latency reduction on cold-auth paths in synthetic benchmarks when prefetch is enabled (results in tests/e2e/results/cred_prefetch.json).
Release ops: coordinated feature-flag rollout and console note to avoid surprise for Dedicated customers.

source.title: Coordinated credential handoff, compact retry presets, and generator stream ergonomics across SDKs
source.author: Maya Lopez
source.description:
Motivation: We keep getting support tickets and cross-SDK bugs around: (1) credential handoff semantics when a streaming connection needs to rotate/refresh tokens mid-session, (2) divergent ergonomic surfaces for generator/async-iterator based streaming in each SDK, and (3) inconsistent retry presets that lead to surprising latency/cost tradeoffs in low-latency apps. This PR centralizes the contract in the monorepo and ships coordinated changes for the TypeScript/Python/Go SDKs and runtime float so customers can opt into a single predictable behaviour.

What this PR does (high-level): Add a CredentialHandoff contract (lightweight interface + reference implementation), introduce a new compact retry preset set (compact-1/2/3) tuned for short-sequence, latency-sensitive workloads, and unify the public generator/async-iterator surface across SDKs so streaming ergonomics are consistent and migration paths are obvious. This branch contains three smaller commits for the mono-repo: (A) contract + docs + tests, (B) SDK adapters and examples in ts/py/go packages, (C) version bumps and release notes.

Compatibility story: The default behaviour is backward compatible. SDKs expose opt-in flags: `credentialHandoff: 'auto' | 'explicit'` and `retryPreset: 'standard' | 'compact-2'`. Existing consumers continue to operate without change. The generator surface adds new helpers (e.g. `streamToAsyncGenerator`) that are additive wrappers; we also include a short migration note in the changelog.

Testing & verification: unit tests added for the handoff state machine, e2e integration test that simulates token expiry during a 90s long-stream request (CI runs a mocked auth server), and cross-SDK interop smoke tests that run TS -> runtime -> Go client scenarios. Local perf bench indicates compact-3 reduces median per-token latency by ~8% on short sequences due to reduced retry delays and more aggressive early-fails for known 4xx cases.

Follow-ups: coordinate with infra to roll kernel backoff tuning (ENG-2110), and schedule a docs rollout with the Developer Experience team to publish migration examples and a short cookbook (ENG-2199).
source.commits_summary: 3 commits: 1) introduce CredentialHandoff interface + reference impl + unit tests; 2) adapters: ts/py/go SDK wrappers, migration examples, and cross-sdk smoke test; 3) bump SDK versions, changelogs, and release notes. Commits are small and focused to make cherry-picking to stable branches straightforward.
diff_snippets: sdk/typescript/src/credential/handOff.ts: export interface CredentialHandoff { requestNewToken(): Promise<string> }
sdk/python/redwood_sdk/credential/hand_off.py: class CredentialHandoff(Protocol): async def request_new_token(self) -> str: ...
sdk/go/credential/hand_off.go: type CredentialHandoff interface { RequestNewToken(ctx context.Context) (string, error) }
review_comments: Ethan Park: Can we avoid a hard dependency on the runtime auth mock in unit tests? Suggest extracting a minimal fake cred provider used by both py/ts/go tests.
Maya Lopez: Good call — moved to a shared test helper in tests/_mocks/cred_provider.* and replaced direct runtime mocks in unit tests. Integration tests still run end-to-end with the mock server.
Priya Menon: The compact retry presets look promising; please add a short note in docs about when not to use compact-3 (e.g. high-throughput batching workloads).
Maya Lopez: Added the note under docs/sdk/retries/compact-presets.md and included perf comparison table.
Lars Schmidt: Small nit: TS generator helper should expose both async-iterator and event-emitter styles for parity with older consumers.
Maya Lopez: Added `eventEmitter()` adapter and unit tests in sdk/typescript/src/stream/generator.ts, left old surface as deprecated alias with console.warn on usage.
Ethan Park: CI flake on the Go integration test (race) — re-run once after fix?
Maya Lopez: Fixed a data-race in generator_wrapper.go (locked ephemeral token cache) and re-ran CI.
source.merge_outcome: merged via squash on 2026-02-02 by Priya Menon
source.release_notes: SDKs: introduce CredentialHandoff contract (opt-in), add compact retry presets (compact-1/2/3) for latency-sensitive flows, unify generator/async-iterator streaming helpers across TypeScript/Python/Go. Version bumps: ts v1.29.0, py v1.13.2, go v1.8.0. See docs/sdk/auth/credential-handoff.md for migration steps.
source.post_merge_actions: Created follow-up tickets ENG-2110 (runtime backoff tuning), ENG-2199 (docs cookbook). Coordinated changelog entries, prepared release branches for SDKs.
source.notes_to_releaser: When cutting SDK releases: publish TS release first (npm), then PyPI, then Go modules. Each release should include the changelog snippet and a short migration note in the SDK docs site. Notify Developer Experience and Support about the opt-in flags so they can update support playbooks.

source.title: Holdpoint-carousel phased promote with rehearsal-shadow replay and rollback playbook
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Adds holdpoint-carousel promotion strategy with rehearsal shadow replay and a configurable rollback playbook. Backwards compatible with existing rollout policies; new profiles opt-in.
source.description:
Summary: Introduces a holdpoint-driven 'carousel' promote strategy aimed at safe staged promotes across session-sensitive workloads. The feature combines (1) holdpoint anchors — deterministic traffic cut points keyed by session cohorts, (2) a rehearsal shadow runner that replays representative traffic for each holdpoint prior to live promotion, and (3) a rollback playbook that encodes automatic and manual abort semantics with quarantine lanes.

Motivation: Multiple incidents where incremental canaries passed aggregate metrics but failed for sticky-session cohorts motivated a promotion strategy that ensures session-coherent rehearsals before traffic cutover. This PR implements the orchestration primitives and a default rollback playbook that operators can tune.

Design:
- Holdpoint controller: computes holdpoints from cohort selectors and session affinity metadata, maintains per-holdpoint state and progress checkpoints.
- Rehearsal shadow runner: lightweight traffic replayer that runs sampled requests against candidate model variants with KV-cache warmup and smoke checks.
- Rollback playbook: policy-as-data YAML-driven playbook mapping failure classes (latency spike, token-error regression, model drift) to actions (quarantine, immediate revert, partial drain, human-latch).
- Integration with Console: exposes per-holdpoint timelines, rehearsal badges, and playbook-executed actions.

Checklist:
- [x] Implement controller state machine and progress checkpoints
- [x] Add rehearsal runner with deterministic replay seed
- [x] Provide default rollback playbook and validation harness
- [x] Unit tests for controller transitions
- [x] Integration tests (simulated sessions)
- [x] Console telemetry and holdpoint UI hooks
- [x] Docs and quickstart snippet

Backwards compatibility: The new promote strategy is opt-in via rollout_profile: "holdpoint-carousel". Existing rollout profiles are unchanged. Config changes are additive and safe for clusters that do not opt-in.

Testing:
- Local unit test suites under test/unit or test/orchestrator run with new mock session registry.
- Integration harness has a new scenario that simulates 10k session handoffs across 3 holdpoints and validates session affinity + KV prewarm.
- Ran canary smoke on staging with 2 rehearsal runs; CI flake fixed by increasing test harness timeouts.

Rollout plan: Stage to staging for 48h, enable feature flag for 5% of traffic (non-critical customers), run two rehearsal cycles and collect per-holdpoint smoke metrics, then raise to 20% with automatic rollback monitoring on the playbook thresholds.

Related: builds on ENG-4321 (session-affine routing) and follows the telemetry contract in ENG-4399.

Files changed (high level):
- src/orchestrator/holdpoint_controller.py: new state machine and API hooks
- src/orchestrator/rehearsal_runner.go: sample-driven replay engine with KV warmup
- src/orchestrator/playbooks/rollback_playbook.yaml: policy-as-data examples
- console/ui/holdpoint_timeline.tsx: UI badges and timeline
- docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md: design + quickstart
- test/integration/holdpoint_carousel_test.go: integration scenario
- cfg/rollout_profiles/defaults.yaml: new profile entry

Operational concerns & mitigation:
- Cost: rehearsal runs increase token spend; playbook includes a rehearsal_budget parameter and sampling controls.
- Latency: KV warmup performed in rehearsal; live cutover waits for warmup checkpoint.
- Observability: added per-holdpoint smoke histograms and replay diffs; console links to sample traces.

Rollback semantics: the playbook exposes three abort modes: fast-revert (squash), partial-drain (graceful rollback of subsequent holdpoints), and quarantine (isolate traffic, escalate to human-latch). See rollback_playbook.yaml for mappings and default thresholds.

Documentation: A new page docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md includes conceptual diagrams, playbook examples, and a quickstart.

Migration notes: none required for existing profiles; operators must opt-in to use the new profile.

Open items:
- Add CLI tooling to generate rehearsal seeds from production trace (follow-up PR)
- Add deterministic trace replay for embeddings-heavy workloads (research task)
commits: a3f9d4c: orchestrator: add holdpoint controller state machine and API
b7e2c11: rehearsal: implement replay driver with KV warmup and smoke checks
c4d5ab8: playbooks: add rollback_playbook.yaml and tests
d1e4f07: console: holdpoint timeline badges and telemetry links
e9b3c22: docs: holdpoint-carousel design doc and quickstart
diff_snippets:
+++ src/orchestrator/holdpoint_controller.py
+def progress_holdpoint(hp_id, checkpoint):
+    # advance state machine when rehearsal passes smoke checks
+    state = store.get(hp_id)
+    if checkpoint.passed:
+        state.phase = 'promoted'
+    store.save(hp_id, state)

+++ src/orchestrator/playbooks/rollback_playbook.yaml
-failure_classes:
-  latency_spike:
-    action: partial-drain
-    threshold_ms: 200
-  token_error:
-    action: fast-revert
-    threshold_rate: 0.02
review_comments: Marco Ruiz: Please add a TTL on holdpoint state to avoid stale anchors in long-lived sessions. Also add an operator-visible metric for rehearsal success rate.
Ava Chen: Added holdpoint_ttl config and a per-holdpoint_rehearsal_success metric in the latest commit. See cfg/rollout_profiles/defaults.yaml for defaults.
Priya Anand: The integration test was flaky on CI; increased harness timeout and added deterministic seeds. approving with nit on docs wording.
Ethan Brooks: Left an inline comment about race conditions in progress_holdpoint; please add a Compare-And-Swap when updating the store.
Ava Chen: CAS implemented in progress_holdpoint and a unit test added to test/unit/holdpoint_state_test.py
source.notes: Merged after addressing state TTL and CAS concerns; integration flakiness resolved by deterministic seeds. Feature gated and behind rollout_profile opt-in.
source.changelog_entry: Add: holdpoint-carousel rollout strategy (opt-in) with rehearsal shadow and rollback playbook.

Rollback semantics: the playbook exposes three abort modes: fast-revert (squash), partial-drain (graceful rollback of subsequent holdpoints), and quarantine (isolate traffic, escalate to human-latch). See rollback_playbook.yaml for mappings and default thresholds.

Documentation: A new page docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md includes conceptual diagrams, playbook examples, and a quickstart.

Migration notes: none required for existing profiles; operators must opt-in to use the new profile.

Open items:
- Add CLI tooling to generate rehearsal seeds from production trace (follow-up PR)
- Add deterministic trace replay for embeddings-heavy workloads (research task)
text:
Summary: Introduces a holdpoint-driven 'carousel' promote strategy aimed at safe staged promotes across session-sensitive workloads. The feature combines (1) holdpoint anchors — deterministic traffic cut points keyed by session cohorts, (2) a rehearsal shadow runner that replays representative traffic for each holdpoint prior to live promotion, and (3) a rollback playbook that encodes automatic and manual abort semantics with quarantine lanes.

Motivation: Multiple incidents where incremental canaries passed aggregate metrics but failed for sticky-session cohorts motivated a promotion strategy that ensures session-coherent rehearsals before traffic cutover. This PR implements the orchestration primitives and a default rollback playbook that operators can tune.

Design:
- Holdpoint controller: computes holdpoints from cohort selectors and session affinity metadata, maintains per-holdpoint state and progress checkpoints.
- Rehearsal shadow runner: lightweight traffic replayer that runs sampled requests against candidate model variants with KV-cache warmup and smoke checks.
- Rollback playbook: policy-as-data YAML-driven playbook mapping failure classes (latency spike, token-error regression, model drift) to actions (quarantine, immediate revert, partial drain, human-latch).
- Integration with Console: exposes per-holdpoint timelines, rehearsal badges, and playbook-executed actions.

Checklist:
- [x] Implement controller state machine and progress checkpoints
- [x] Add rehearsal runner with deterministic replay seed
- [x] Provide default rollback playbook and validation harness
- [x] Unit tests for controller transitions
- [x] Integration tests (simulated sessions)
- [x] Console telemetry and holdpoint UI hooks
- [x] Docs and quickstart snippet

Backwards compatibility: The new promote strategy is opt-in via rollout_profile: "holdpoint-carousel". Existing rollout profiles are unchanged. Config changes are additive and safe for clusters that do not opt-in.

Testing:
- Local unit test suites under test/unit or test/orchestrator run with new mock session registry.
- Integration harness has a new scenario that simulates 10k session handoffs across 3 holdpoints and validates session affinity + KV prewarm.
- Ran canary smoke on staging with 2 rehearsal runs; CI flake fixed by increasing test harness timeouts.

Rollout plan: Stage to staging for 48h, enable feature flag for 5% of traffic (non-critical customers), run two rehearsal cycles and collect per-holdpoint smoke metrics, then raise to 20% with automatic rollback monitoring on the playbook thresholds.

Related: builds on ENG-4321 (session-affine routing) and follows the telemetry contract in ENG-4399.

Files changed (high level):
- src/orchestrator/holdpoint_controller.py: new state machine and API hooks
- src/orchestrator/rehearsal_runner.go: sample-driven replay engine with KV warmup
- src/orchestrator/playbooks/rollback_playbook.yaml: policy-as-data examples
- console/ui/holdpoint_timeline.tsx: UI badges and timeline
- docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md: design + quickstart
- test/integration/holdpoint_carousel_test.go: integration scenario
- cfg/rollout_profiles/defaults.yaml: new profile entry

Operational concerns & mitigation:
- Cost: rehearsal runs increase token spend; playbook includes a rehearsal_budget parameter and sampling controls.
- Latency: KV warmup performed in rehearsal; live cutover waits for warmup checkpoint.
- Observability: added per-holdpoint smoke histograms and replay diffs; console links to sample traces.

Rollback semantics: the playbook exposes three abort modes: fast-revert (squash), partial-drain (graceful rollback of subsequent holdpoints), and quarantine (isolate traffic, escalate to human-latch). See rollback_playbook.yaml for mappings and default thresholds.

Documentation: A new page docs/rollouts/holdpoint-carousel.md includes conceptual diagrams, playbook examples, and a quickstart.

Migration notes: none required for existing profiles; operators must opt-in to use the new profile.

Open items:
- Add CLI tooling to generate rehearsal seeds from production trace (follow-up PR)
- Add deterministic trace replay for embeddings-heavy workloads (research task)

source.title: introduce-stream-sanitizer-window-and-functioncall-flush-to-preserve-json-span-contiguity
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: Intermittent production incidents and fuzz tests revealed cases where SSE chunk boundaries could split JSON object spans or function-call sentinel tokens in ways that make downstream parsers emit truncated or mis-ordered events. This caused structured-output consumers and OpenAI-compatibility wrappers to drop function-call arguments or mis-associate tokens when partial frames were replayed. This PR introduces a lightweight streaming sanitizer layer and a small reflow window that: 1) keeps a minimal lookback buffer for boundary repair, 2) normalizes delimiter emission order for function/tool-call sentinels, and 3) applies a safe escape-rewrite for split UTF-8/JSON token fragments. The description below summarizes changes, risks, testing, and rollout plan. Changes made: - runtime/sse/stream_window.go: new StreamSanitizer that maintains a sliding reflow window (configurable max bytes, default 4KB) and detects partial JSON spans using a fast heuristic (brace/quote counters + UTF-8 continuation checks). When a span crosses the window boundary the sanitizer defers emit and attempts repair by joining adjacent chunks or emitting a rescue-marker event. - runtime/sse/protocol.go: added deterministic ordering guarantees for sentinel emissions (heartbeat -> preamble -> function_call_start -> payload chunks -> function_call_end). This avoids reordered sentinel interleaving under high-concurrency flush. - openai/compat/adapter.py: adapter updated to understand the new rescue-marker event and to tolerate delayed function_call_end tokens; compatibility shim will buffer until a matching end or rescue marker. - runtime/kv/cache_adapter.rs: minor change to avoid flushing cached prefixes in the middle of an active function call stream. - tests/integration/sse_partial_json_test.go: new deterministic and randomized tests that simulate chunk-split UTF-8 and nested JSON objects across many micro-batches. - docs/compat/notes_sse_chunking.md: notes for integrators about rescue-marker semantics and upgraded adapter behavior. Implementation notes: - The sanitizer is intentionally conservative: by default it only defers emission for spans <4KB and recent activity; longer objects are passed through to avoid unbounded buffering. - UTF-8 sequences and escape sequences are validated with a fast-path routine; malformed runs trigger a rescue-marker plus the raw bytes (so higher-level telemetry can decide). - The functioncall sentinel ordering change is idempotent for well-formed streams; adapters that already respect function_call_start/end are unchanged. Rollout and migration: - Feature gated behind runtime flag "sse.stream_sanitizer.enabled" (default: off in canary). - Canary rollout to 5% of hosts for 48 hours; telemetry checks for increased latency, increased memory use, and rescued-frame rate. - If rescue rate >0.1% or p95 latency regression >10% we will roll back the gate. Testing and validation: - CI: added unit tests for boundary heuristics and integration test harness that replays recorded truncated-chunk incidents. - Staging: ran long-run fuzz and compatibility suite against openai-compat adapter; observed 0 parity regressions and eliminated a previously reproducible tool-call-argument-drop case. - Load test: negligible CPU increase on average, tail latencies increased by ~3ms in worst-case (within SLO). Review summary and discussion: - Jordan: "Core heuristic looks reasonable; ensure the window size is configurable per-customer since some private deployments may accept higher buffering." Ava: "Added config path and documented VPC override in installer notes." - Maya: "What about cascade effects with autoscaler when many hosts buffer?" Ava: "Sanitizer uses soft memory caps per-worker and will spill to disk after soft cap; we added a metric sse.sanitizer.spill_count and autoscaler alerts to the rollouts." - Ethan: "Adapter changes look safe but add a migration note for older clients." Ava: "Added compatibility shim to adapter.py that keeps old behavior if client declares legacy-mode." CI and merge: - CI initially caught a flaky test in the randomized harness; fixed by tightening the timing window and re-running. - All checks passed on the final run (unit, integration, lint, format, OSS compatibility). - Merge method: squash and merge. Related: ENG-4523 (streaming reliability), SUP-1290 (customer incident report). Release notes: "Fixes where SSE chunking could split JSON or function-call sentinels, causing truncated structured output for some OpenAI-compat consumers. Introduces a stream sanitizer and rescue-marker semantics; disabled by default during canary rollout." Risks and mitigations: "Increased memory under pathological inputs (mitigated via soft cap and spill-to-disk), and potential small p95 latency increase (observed <=3ms in load tests). Rollback plan included. Follow-ups: add server-side telemetry to track rescued-span types and a developer CLI to reassemble rescued frames for debugging.
commits: 9f3b2c4: add StreamSanitizer and initial heuristics
a7d1e6b: normalize sentinel emission ordering and update protocol docs
c2b5f91: openai adapter: handle rescue-marker and delayed function_call_end
de8a441: tests: add sse_partial_json deterministic + fuzz harness
f5b2a10: docs: compat notes and rollout instructions
review_comments: Jordan Patel: Core heuristic looks reasonable; ensure window size is configurable per-customer.
Ava Chen: Added a per-deployment config flag and documented override in the private-installer notes.
Maya Liu: Concerned about autoscaler churn if many hosts buffer simultaneously.
Ava Chen: Sanitizer enforces soft-memory caps and spills to disk; added sse.sanitizer.spill_count metric and alert.
Ethan Brooks: Adapter compatibility with older clients?
Ava Chen: Included legacy-mode shim and documented migration steps in the compat notes.
source.release_notes: Fixes to SSE chunking that preserve JSON/tool-call boundaries by adding a stream sanitizer and rescue markers; canary-gated rollout.
source.notes_for_oncall: If a canary report shows rescue_rate spike or p95 regression, disable sse.stream_sanitizer.enabled via flags service. Metric names: sse.sanitizer.rescue_rate, sse.sanitizer.spill_count, sse.sanitizer.window_size_bytes.

source.title: Policy engine: add priority classes and per-route budgets (deterministic decision logging + validation)
source.author: Jordan Lee
source.body: Summary: This PR extends the policy engine to support (a) explicit priority classes (enterprise/standard/best-effort/internal) and (b) per-route budgets that can be consumed deterministically during admission control / overload protection. It also adds deterministic decision logging (stable reason codes + structured fields) and a schema validation layer to keep policies auditable for enterprise escalations. Motivation: Hot-route capacity protection requires a single, explainable place to answer: "given tenant, route group, and current capacity signals, should we admit / throttle / shed / fallback". We need to do this with minimal per-request overhead and without introducing high-cardinality telemetry. This PR focuses on correctness + determinism; performance tuning (memoization/batched lookups) will follow. What’s in scope: 1) Policy schema additions: - priority_class: enum {INTERNAL, ENTERPRISE, STANDARD, BEST_EFFORT} (default STANDARD for backward compatibility) - route_group: canonical name for grouping endpoints (e.g., chat_completions, embeddings, rerank) - budgets: optional per route_group budgets keyed by priority class (requests/sec + burst + optional concurrency cap) - actions: allow | throttle | shed | fallback (fallback gated; final enforcement happens in gateway/routing) 2) Policy evaluation contract: - input includes: tenant_id, tier, route, route_group, region, and coarse capacity signals (queue_depth_bucket, gpu_saturation_bucket, scheduler_lag_bucket) - output includes: decision, decision_reason, retry_after_ms (optional), chosen_priority_class, matched_rule_id, and a stable decision_hash for auditability 3) Deterministic decision logging: - new "policy_decision" log event emitted by the policy engine with stable, low-cardinality fields - reason codes normalized to align with 429 semantics work in gateway (QUOTA_EXCEEDED vs OVERLOAD_SHED vs ADMISSION_THROTTLE vs FALLBACK_APPLIED) 4) Validation + guardrails: - schema validation on policy load (reject unknown enums/invalid windows) - explicit checks preventing per-tenant/per-route unconstrained overrides unless allowlisted (prevents accidental fleet-wide noisy-neighbor exceptions) - budget sanity checks (burst >= rate, non-negative, max caps) 5) Tests: - golden tests for deterministic output (given the same input policy + request context, decision_hash and reason fields must match) - validation tests covering invalid policies and non-backwards-compatible changes - basic evaluation tests covering priority ordering and budget exhaustion behavior Notes on backwards compatibility: - Existing policies without priority_class/route_group continue to evaluate as STANDARD with route_group derived from route mapping if available; if not available, route_group="unknown" and budgets are not applied. - Decision logging is added but does not change existing endpoints; log event is separate from request logs. Implementation details: - Added a policy schema version bump (v7 -> v8) with a translator that fills defaults on load. - Added a small canonical route->route_group mapping table co-located with schema; enforcement services should treat route_group as authoritative once provided in request context. - Budget accounting is modeled as an input signal ("budget_remaining_bucket") for now; actual counters are managed by gateway/routing. This PR sets the language/types and ensures evaluation is deterministic. Security/Access: - Deterministic decision logging includes tenant_id but is restricted to internal logs; no customer PII. - No new external endpoints. Follow-ups (tracked separately): - Performance: caching / batch lookups for high-QPS (ENG-4924/ENG-4925 dependent, plus upcoming perf PR). - Unify reason-code taxonomy and headers with gateway implementation once landed (paired with Amara/Cole). - Add admin-only debug endpoint to inspect decisions (INT-3302) once access controls are in place. References: - Linear: ENG-4923 - Docs/Design: Hot-route capacity protection design + ADR (see Confluence links in ENG-4923) - Related PRs: gateway 429 reason codes + routing admission control will consume decision_reason + priority_class.
commits: policy/schema: add priority_class, route_group, budgets (v8) + translator defaults
policy/eval: deterministic decision output incl. reason codes + decision_hash
policy/validation: strict enum/window validation + guardrails for overrides
logging: add policy_decision structured log event (low-cardinality fields)
tests: add golden determinism tests + validation failure cases
docs: update internal README for new schema + evaluation contract
source.review_conversation: Ravi Menon (review): The schema additions look right, but I want to make sure we don’t accidentally create a high-cardinality dimension in metrics/logs. Can we guarantee route_group is from a small fixed set? Also, what ensures decision_hash is stable across deploys? Jordan Lee (author): Yep — route_group is an enum-like canonical string list in shared/contracts/route_groups.ts. Validation rejects values not in that allowlist. decision_hash is SHA-256 over a stable, ordered subset of fields: schema_version, matched_rule_id, decision, decision_reason, chosen_priority_class, retry_after_ms_bucket, and the capacity buckets (not raw queue depth). I added golden tests and a comment noting not to include raw timestamps/ids. Mina Haddad (review): In eval, we currently derive route_group from route if the caller didn’t provide one. That’s convenient, but I worry about drift between services. Can we make caller-provided route_group preferred and log when it’s missing? Jordan Lee (author): Agreed. Updated evaluator to prefer explicit route_group and emit a debug-level "route_group_missing" field in the decision log (boolean), no extra tags. Also added a validation warning counter (not tagged by route) that increments when policy load sees unknown route mappings. Ethan Park (review): Backwards compatibility: policies without priority_class default to STANDARD, but enterprise traffic should get ENTERPRISE. Where does that mapping happen? Jordan Lee (author): Mapping from tier->priority_class is intentionally not inside policy engine. Callers pass tier and policy uses rules to map; however, to reduce footguns, I added an optional default mapping in the translator: if a rule has tier==enterprise and no priority_class specified, we set ENTERPRISE during translation. This preserves legacy tier rules. Kaitlyn Nguyen (review): Please double-check we’re not logging customer identifiers that are not already in internal logs. Also ensure structured fields are redacted consistently. Jordan Lee (author): Confirmed: tenant_id is already present in internal request logs, and we do not log API keys, org names, emails, or IPs. The decision log uses tenant_id and a hashed request_id only. I added a unit test to ensure prohibited fields aren’t in the event payload. Logan Wright (review): Reason codes: I like the normalization, but we should align exact strings with gateway 429 work to avoid translation layers. Can you coordinate with Amara/Cole and lock strings? Jordan Lee (author): Done — updated reason code strings to match the gateway draft: QUOTA_EXCEEDED, OVERLOAD_SHED, ADMISSION_THROTTLE, FALLBACK_APPLIED, POLICY_DENY. Added a comment that changes require cross-team review. Benji Okafor (review): Guardrails: the override allowlist lives in config; please make sure failures are actionable and don’t hard-fail prod on a bad config unless explicitly enabled. Jordan Lee (author): Adjusted behavior: validation hard-fails on schema invalidity, but override allowlist violations default to "reject rule and continue" with an error log unless STRICT_VALIDATION=true (only enabled in CI/staging currently). Added a metric counter policy_invalid_rules_total (no high-card tags) and surfaced it in the load summary log. CI: First run failed due to proto compile ordering; fixed in follow-up commit and re-ran. Approvals: Ravi ✅, Mina ✅, Ethan ✅, Kaitlyn ✅. Merged via squash after green CI.
source.release_notes: Internal-only: Policy engine now supports priority classes and per-route budgets with deterministic decision logging and schema validation to enable hot-route capacity protection. No customer-facing behavior changes until gateway/routing enforcement is enabled behind flags.

text: Summary: This PR extends the policy engine to support (a) explicit priority classes (enterprise/standard/best-effort/internal) and (b) per-route budgets that can be consumed deterministically during admission control / overload protection. It also adds deterministic decision logging (stable reason codes + structured fields) and a schema validation layer to keep policies auditable for enterprise escalations. Motivation: Hot-route capacity protection requires a single, explainable place to answer: "given tenant, route group, and current capacity signals, should we admit / throttle / shed / fallback". We need to do this with minimal per-request overhead and without introducing high-cardinality telemetry. This PR focuses on correctness + determinism; performance tuning (memoization/batched lookups) will follow. What’s in scope: 1) Policy schema additions: - priority_class: enum {INTERNAL, ENTERPRISE, STANDARD, BEST_EFFORT} (default STANDARD for backward compatibility) - route_group: canonical name for grouping endpoints (e.g., chat_completions, embeddings, rerank) - budgets: optional per route_group budgets keyed by priority class (requests/sec + burst + optional concurrency cap) - actions: allow | throttle | shed | fallback (fallback gated; final enforcement happens in gateway/routing) 2) Policy evaluation contract: - input includes: tenant_id, tier, route, route_group, region, and coarse capacity signals (queue_depth_bucket, gpu_saturation_bucket, scheduler_lag_bucket) - output includes: decision, decision_reason, retry_after_ms (optional), chosen_priority_class, matched_rule_id, and a stable decision_hash for auditability 3) Deterministic decision logging: - new "policy_decision" log event emitted by the policy engine with stable, low-cardinality fields - reason codes normalized to align with 429 semantics work in gateway (QUOTA_EXCEEDED vs OVERLOAD_SHED vs ADMISSION_THROTTLE vs FALLBACK_APPLIED) 4) Validation + guardrails: - schema validation on policy load (reject unknown enums/invalid windows) - explicit checks preventing per-tenant/per-route unconstrained overrides unless allowlisted (prevents accidental fleet-wide noisy-neighbor exceptions) - budget sanity checks (burst >= rate, non-negative, max caps) 5) Tests: - golden tests for deterministic output (given the same input policy + request context, decision_hash and reason fields must match) - validation tests covering invalid policies and non-backwards-compatible changes - basic evaluation tests covering priority ordering and budget exhaustion behavior Notes on backwards compatibility: - Existing policies without priority_class/route_group continue to evaluate as STANDARD with route_group derived from route mapping if available; if not available, route_group="unknown" and budgets are not applied. - Decision logging is added but does not change existing endpoints; log event is separate from request logs. Implementation details: - Added a policy schema version bump (v7 -> v8) with a translator that fills defaults on load. - Added a small canonical route->route_group mapping table co-located with schema; enforcement services should treat route_group as authoritative once provided in request context. - Budget accounting is modeled as an input signal ("budget_remaining_bucket") for now; actual counters are managed by gateway/routing. This PR sets the language/types and ensures evaluation is deterministic. Security/Access: - Deterministic decision logging includes tenant_id but is restricted to internal logs; no customer PII. - No new external endpoints. Follow-ups (tracked separately): - Performance: caching / batch lookups for high-QPS (ENG-4924/ENG-4925 dependent, plus upcoming perf PR). - Unify reason-code taxonomy and headers with gateway implementation once landed (paired with Amara/Cole). - Add admin-only debug endpoint to inspect decisions (INT-3302) once access controls are in place. References: - Linear: ENG-4923 - Docs/Design: Hot-route capacity protection design + ADR (see Confluence links in ENG-4923) - Related PRs: gateway 429 reason codes + routing admission control will consume decision_reason + priority_class.

source.title: Slab concatenation + FlashMixer kernel with async KV coalescer and continuous-batching horizon
source.author: Maya Chen
source.body: Context: Operator-level profiling in the last quarter showed sustained stalls on small-to-medium sequence lengths due to scatter/gather overheads and suboptimal KV warmup for bursty traffic. This PR introduces a new attention strategy (SlabConcatenate + FlashMixer microkernel) that reduces per-token overhead for heterogeneous sequence distributions and a lightweight async-KV coalescer that amortizes KV cache population across micro-batches. Motivation: 1) Reduce small-batch latency and increase throughput at mid-sequence lengths (256-2048) where customer workloads spend most of their tokens. 2) Retain deterministic rotary/rope semantics while enabling a fused concatenate path that avoids extra memcpy for tiled KV layouts. 3) Improve continuous batching horizon so runtime can opportunistically stretch batching without violating latency SLOs. What this change does: - Introduces FlashMixer microkernel: a variant of Flash-attention style tiling that mixes slab-concatenated Q/K/V regions to minimize memory fragmentation and enable a single fused rotary application pass. Optimized for 8-16 head widths on A100/RTX7x. - Adds SlabConcatenate path to the kernel selector with heuristics based on sequence length distribution and current batch skew metric. This path fuses concatenation, rotary application, and QK matmul into one kernel for contiguous slabs. - Implements Async KV Coalescer: a runtime stage that coalesces concurrent KV writes from overlapping micro-batches into a single GPU write and exposes a KV warmup metric. Coalescer has backpressure safety to avoid overpopulation for very long sequences. - Extends continuous batching horizon: runtime now computes an elastic horizon that trades a small p99 latency tail against higher throughput; this is governed by a new config: continuous_batching.max_horizon_ms and continuous_batching.tail_target_pct. - Preservation of model semantics: fused rotary/rope ops are designed to be numerically identical to the existing path (FP16 accumulation path validated). Testing & benchmarks: - Microbenchmarks (internal harness) show 9-14% throughput uplift and 6-10% median latency reduction at seq=512/1024 for T5-like models and ~12% on LLaMA-variant runs with head dim 64. - KV-heavy chat workload (continuous small messages) showed 18% cost improvement due to reduced redundant KV writes. - Extensive regression tests validate logits and sampling for baseline prompts and prompt sets in eval harness. Rollout plan: gated feature behind runtime flag slab_concat.enabled; rollout to canary clusters for 2 weeks; if no regressions, flip on in dedicated pools with auto-fallback to legacy path. Related: ENG-4821 (kv warmup telemetry), ENG-4899 (batching SLOs), SUP-3210 (customer-reported chat latency spike). Notes: this PR intentionally avoids global allocator changes and keeps the fused kernel opt-in. The body below includes a short appendix with file-level highlights and a summary of reviewer discussion and CI details.
commits: Initial implementation of SlabConcatenate/FlashMixer microkernel (maya/ops)
Add kernel selector heuristic and continuous batching horizon config (maya/runtime)
Async KV coalescer and warmup telemetry (maya/kv)
Unit tests and eval-harness integration (maya/tests)
Fix numeric edge case for rotary application on odd-head-dims (maya/ops)
review_comments: Ethan Park (2025-11-20): Can we add a safety net where the coalescer refuses to merge KV writes if the estimated cold-miss rate exceeds X? Please add a metric for coalescer_rejects.
Maya Chen (2025-11-20): Added coalescer_rejects metric and a tunable threshold; left default conservative (0.05).
Sofia Ramos (2025-11-24): The fused rotary pass looks good, but please add a unit test for odd head sizes and a numeric comparison at fp16 with huge batch sizes.
Maya Chen (2025-11-25): Added test and adjusted accumulation order to match baseline for odd head dims. Benchmark noise decreased.
Luca Bianchi (2025-11-28): CI flaked on int8 quant tests; please verify kernel compiles with quantized execution path.
Maya Chen (2025-11-29): Fixed linker issue in ops/Makefile and added a guard for quantized path to fallback to standard kernel (optional). CI green afterwards.
source.ci_summary: All CI checks passed after fix. Unit tests: 512/512. Integration tests: 98/98. Bench harness ran locally and in CI with smoke runs. Lint: pass. Built artifacts: linux-cuda12 (A100), linux-cuda11 (RTX series).
source.release_notes: Introduces SlabConcatenate + FlashMixer fused attention kernel (opt-in). Adds Async KV Coalescer and continuous_batching horizon config. Expected throughput uplift 8-14% for mid-tail sequences and improved KV write efficiency for chat workloads. Rollout gated behind slab_concat.enabled.
post_merge_tasks: Monitor kv_warmup.* metrics on canary clusters
Enable slab_concat on dedicated pools after 2-week canary
Add dashboard tile for coalescer_rejects and per-route slab_hit_rate

source.title: unified tool-caller API + delayed-commit retry semantics to stabilize streaming and multi-tool results
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.release_notes: Adds a unified Tool Caller API that offers deterministic semantics for multi-tool and parallel tool invocations, streaming stability fixes, and a delayed-commit idempotent retry strategy to reduce duplicate side effects.
commits: Initial unified-tool-caller skeleton and interface definitions
Implement delayed-commit retry coordinator and idempotency token plumbing
Integrate deterministic multi-call ordering and deterministic response merge
Stabilize streaming hooks and add per-tool streaming checkpoints
Add Python and TypeScript SDK shims and compatibility layer tests
Docs and migration guide for existing consumers
Fix: e2e flaky scenario in tool composition test
source.description: Context and motivation: Customers frequently surface two pain points when composing LLM responses with external tools: 1) streaming instability when tool results and model tokens interleave mid-stream, and 2) duplicate side-effects from naive retry semantics for parallel tool calls. This PR introduces a unified 'Tool Caller' API and a delayed-commit retry strategy to address both issues in a developer-friendly, OpenAI-compatible surface. Design summary: The Tool Caller provides a single entrypoint for: a) orchestrating ordered or parallel tool invocations with deterministic merge semantics, b) registering streaming checkpoints so the model can safely stream partial token output without observing incomplete tool side-effects, and c) delayed-commit retries that only expose tool results to the model after idempotency checks pass. Streaming stability is handled via per-tool stream checkpoints plus a small bounded rebuffer window to preserve low-latency streaming while avoiding mid-stream observable state changes. The retry model uses idempotency tokens and a delayed commit phase: tool runs return provisional results that are not yet injected into the model stream; on success and idempotency confirmation we atomically commit the result into the model's context. Implementation highlights: server/api/tool_caller.py defines the ToolCaller class and public HTTP/SDK surface. server/streaming/stream_manager.py implements streaming checkpoints, rebuffer window, and backpressure integration. server/runtime/tool_dispatcher.cpp contains the low-level executor changes for parallel task orchestration and idempotency hooks. SDK shims (sdk/python/redwood/tools.py and sdk/typescript/src/tool-client.ts) expose the new call signature while preserving the old openai-compatible helpers via a compatibility layer. Observability: introduced tool_caller_latency and tool_commit_success metrics and added a small dashboard tile. Tests: new integration test (tests/integration/tool_composition_test.py) covers ordered, parallel, failure+retry, and streaming-snapshot scenarios. CI: added a targeted e2e workflow ci/workflows/tool-e2e.yml. Migration notes: The compatibility layer keeps old helper names; consumers that depended on streaming-mid-tool injections should migrate to the new two-phase commit injection model. See docs/migration/tool-caller-migration.md for a step-by-step guide and sample code. Rollout plan: feature-gate the new API for hosted and dedicated deployments for four weeks, shadow traffic for two days, then enable for 10% of main traffic for a week before full ramp. Monitoring will focus on commit latency, duplicate-effect rate, and streaming rebuffer tail latency. Backout plan: revert feature flag and disable delayed commits; hotfix branch created if we see increased error budgets. Benchmarks: internal perf runs show median end-to-end latency increase of ~6ms for single-tool calls due to idempotency checks, but multi-tool parallel calls saw a 22% reduction in observed duplicate side-effects and a 12% improvement in 99th percentile streaming stability in our multiplexing demo. Checklist: - [x] API spec and SDK shims implemented - [x] Observability added - [x] Integration tests added - [x] Migration guide written - [x] Benchmarks and rollout plan documented QA and verification steps: run example-apps/multiplexing-demo against local stack, validate no duplicate side-effects for simulated retries, run tool-composition integration test under stress, verify CI e2e pass. Security review: idempotency tokens are scoped per-customer and rotated via KMS; we added a short section in docs on token lifecycle. Known limitations: long-running tools that stream their own state must adopt the provisional-result pattern; we added examples in the migration doc. Relationship to other work: builds on ENG-2191 (tool orchestration primitives) and complements ENG-2248 (streaming stability improvements).
review_comments: Evan Mills: Overall approach looks solid. Question: can the rebuffer window be dynamically tuned per-model? Please add a config hook.
Priya Natarajan (author): Added dynamic rebuffer config and documented defaults in docs; small follow-up in commit 5.
Rita Patel: Concern about storage of provisional results for large binary blobs — can we stream-to-temp-s3 and store pointer? Suggest adding garbage-collection TTL.
Priya Natarajan (author): Implemented pointer-based provisional storage and GC TTL; updated tests to simulate object pointers.
Jonas Berg: CI flaked initially on the e2e step due to clock skew in the idempotency token test. After forcing NTP in the runner it passed. Approved.
source.notes: This PR intentionally keeps the external helper names used by OpenAI-compatible clients but changes semantics for injection timing; teams should plan a short migration window to validate workflows that relied on mid-stream observable side effects.
egress_examples: Python usage: client.tool_call([{'name':'search','args':{'q':'top APIs for caching'}}], ordered=True, commit_mode='delayed')
source.changelog_entry: 2025-12-02: Unified Tool Caller API and delayed-commit retries stabilized and released behind feature flag. Added SDK shims and migration docs.

source.title: Add KMS throttle budget + request backpressure to prevent KMS saturation under spikes
source.author: Diego Santos
source.release_notes: KMS calls now participate in a per-process throttle budget and apply backpressure during KMS throttling/latency spikes to avoid request storms and cascading failures.
source.body:
Context: We’ve seen intermittent elevated 5xx correlated with upstream KMS throttling (SUP-19377) and a near-miss retry amplification event that required a hotfix (pr-18563). While the wrapper now has sane retry/jitter defaults and circuit breaking, we still had a failure mode where a large burst of decrypt/envelope ops (e.g. cache cold start + traffic spike) could saturate KMS and extend the incident blast radius.

This PR introduces an explicit "KMS throttle budget" and integrates KMS calls with the existing request admission/backpressure path so we shed/queue work earlier rather than stampeding KMS.

What’s included (high level): (1) Per-process token-bucket budget for KMS operations: A lightweight budget that is decremented on KMS requests and refilled at a configured rate. When depleted, KMS calls are delayed (bounded) or rejected with a typed error depending on caller policy. This keeps concurrency and request rate within a predictable envelope even if upstream is degraded. (2) Adaptive refill + penalty on throttling: When KMS returns throttling (e.g., AWS ThrottlingException / 429 equivalents), we apply an immediate penalty to the budget refill rate for a short window. This quickly reduces pressure and avoids repeated throttles. (3) Backpressure integration: The KMS client wrapper now plugs into `admission.Controller` so callers can either: (a) block up to `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` and then fail fast, or (b) fail immediately (for latency-sensitive paths) with a clear error category (`KmsBudgetExhausted`). (4) Metrics + tracing: Exposes budget state and decisions, and tags spans with the backpressure outcome.

Config knobs (all have safe defaults; prod guardrails still enforced by pr-18502): - `kms.budget.refill_rps` (default: 200 ops/sec) - `kms.budget.burst` (default: 400 ops) - `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` (default: 50ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_window_ms` (default: 30000ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_factor` (default: 0.5)

Behavioral notes: - This budget is per-process (not global) by design. It limits local amplification and works well with our horizontal scaling/admission control. If we need global limits later, we can layer it via control plane config + per-pool budgets. - We do NOT silently drop decrypts. If we can’t meet the wait bound, we return a typed error and let the caller’s policy decide whether to fail the request, fall back, or rely on cached material (where allowed). - When combined with the circuit breaker, the system now transitions more cleanly: budget starts slowing requests first; if KMS remains degraded, the breaker opens and callers follow degrade-mode policy.

Implementation details: - `kmsbudget.Bucket`: token-bucket with penalty hooks; uses monotonic clock; jittered sleeps to avoid herd effects at refill boundaries. - `kmsclient.Client`: wraps `Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey` calls with `Acquire(ctx)`; all outcomes emit structured logs and metrics. - `admission.Controller`: added a dependency-specific limiter hook (KMS only for now) so we can reuse the backpressure path without leaking KMS specifics into product code.

Metrics added: - `redwood_kms_budget_tokens{env,region,service}` (gauge) - `redwood_kms_budget_acquire_total{outcome="immediate|waited|timeout|rejected"}` (counter) - `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms` (histogram) - `redwood_kms_throttle_penalty_active` (gauge) - Existing `redwood_kms_errors_total{category=...}` is now incremented for `BudgetExhausted` and `BackpressureTimeout` (maps to the canonical taxonomy).

Example log fields (new/standardized): `kms_op`, `kms_budget_outcome`, `kms_budget_wait_ms`, `kms_budget_tokens_after`, `kms_error_category`, `trace_id`.

Testing: - Unit tests for token bucket correctness and penalty behavior (including time skews). - Integration harness updated to simulate 429/throttling and verify that request rate to the KMS stub decreases within 2s and that tail latency stays bounded (ties into ENG-4825). - Added a regression test ensuring we don’t exceed retry+budget wait combined latency cap.

Rollout plan: - Behind `kms.backpressure.enabled` (default ON in staging, OFF in prod until we flip per environment). - Canary in us-east Dedicated + Hosted data-plane: monitor `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms p95`, KMS 429 rate, and request 5xx. - Flip prod flag gradually per region; rollback is a single flag change.

admission: add dependency limiter hook for external services

review_conversation: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Can we guarantee backpressure wait is bounded even when ctx has long deadline? We should not add unbounded latency. Also please add a metric that clearly distinguishes waited vs rejected outcomes.

Diego Santos (author): Updated `Acquire()` to cap waits at `min(ctx_deadline, max_wait_ms)` and emit `kms_budget_acquire_total{outcome=...}` + wait histogram. Added a test that combined retry+budget wait never exceeds configured latency cap.
Allison Grant (Observability): Metric namespacing: prefer `redwood_kms_budget_*` and avoid mixing into generic admission metrics. Also add `service` tag for multi-service dashboards.
Diego Santos (author): Renamed metrics to `redwood_kms_budget_*` and added `service` tag (consistent with other dependency health panels).

Diego Santos (author): Agreed; hook remains dependency-specific with minimal surface area.

title: Add KMS throttle budget + request backpressure to prevent KMS saturation under spikes
preview:
Context: We’ve seen intermittent elevated 5xx correlated with upstream KMS throttling (SUP-19377) and a near-miss retry amplification event that required a hotfix (pr-18563). While the wrapper now has sane retry/jitter defaults and circuit breaking, we still had a failure mode where a large burst of decrypt/envelope ops (e.g. cache cold start + traffic spike) could saturate KMS and extend the incident blast radius.

This PR introduces an explicit "KMS throttle budget" and integrates KMS calls with the existing request admission/backpressure path so we shed/queue work earlier rather than stampeding KMS.

What’s included (high level): (1) Per-process token-bucket budget for KMS operations: A lightweight budget that is decremented on KMS requests and refilled at a configured rate. When depleted, KMS calls are delayed (bounded) or rejected with a typed error depending on caller policy. This keeps concurrency and request rate within a predictable envelope even if upstream is degraded. (2) Adaptive refill + penalty on throttling: When KMS returns throttling (e.g., AWS ThrottlingException / 429 equivalents), we apply an immediate penalty to the budget refill rate for a short window. This quickly reduces pressure and avoids repeated throttles. (3) Backpressure integration: The KMS client wrapper now plugs into `admission.Controller` so callers can either: (a) block up to `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` and then fail fast, or (b) fail immediately (for latency-sensitive paths) with a clear error category (`KmsBudgetExhausted`). (4) Metrics + tracing: Exposes budget state and decisions, and tags spans with the backpressure outcome.

Config knobs (all have safe defaults; prod guardrails still enforced by pr-18502): - `kms.budget.refill_rps` (default: 200 ops/sec) - `kms.budget.burst` (default: 400 ops) - `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` (default: 50ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_window_ms` (default: 30000ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_factor` (default: 0.5)

Behavioral notes: - This budget is per-process (not global) by design. It limits local amplification and works well with our horizontal scaling/admission control. If we need global limits later, we can layer it via control plane config + per-pool budgets. - We do NOT silently drop decrypts. If we can’t meet the wait bound, we return a typed error and let the caller’s policy decide whether to fail the request, fall back, or rely on cached material (where allowed). - When combined with the circuit breaker, the system now transitions more cleanly: budget starts slowing requests first; if KMS remains degraded, the breaker opens and callers follow degrade-mode policy.

Implementation details: - `kmsbudget.Bucket`: token-bucket with penalty hooks; uses monotonic clock; jittered sleeps to avoid herd effects at refill boundaries. - `kmsclient.Client`: wraps `Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey` calls with `Acquire(ctx)`; all outcomes emit structured logs and metrics. - `admission.Controller`: added a dependency-specific limiter hook (KMS only for now) so we can reuse the backpressure path without leaking KMS specifics into product code.

Metrics added: - `redwood_kms_budget_tokens{env,region,service}` (gauge) - `redwood_kms_budget_acquire_total{outcome="immediate|waited|timeout|rejected"}` (counter) - `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms` (histogram) - `redwood_kms_throttle_penalty_active` (gauge) - Existing `redwood_kms_errors_total{category=...}` is now incremented for `BudgetExhausted` and `BackpressureTimeout` (maps to the canonical taxonomy).

Example log fields (new/standardized): `kms_op`, `kms_budget_outcome`, `kms_budget_wait_ms`, `kms_budget_tokens_after`, `kms_error_category`, `trace_id`.

Testing: - Unit tests for token bucket correctness and penalty behavior (including time skews). - Integration harness updated to simulate 429/throttling and verify that request rate to the KMS stub decreases within 2s and that tail latency stays bounded (ties into ENG-4825). - Added a regression test ensuring we don’t exceed retry+budget wait combined latency cap.

Rollout plan: - Behind `kms.backpressure.enabled` (default ON in staging, OFF in prod until we flip per environment). - Canary in us-east Dedicated + Hosted data-plane: monitor `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms p95`, KMS 429 rate, and request 5xx. - Flip prod flag gradually per region; rollback is a single flag change.

Checklist: - [x] SRE reviewed paging/threshold implications (no new pages by default; dashboards use derived panels) - [x] Security reviewed failure semantics (no weakening of envelope decrypt correctness) - [x] Added metrics + trace tags per observability conventions - [x] Added tests for throttle + backpressure scenarios

Follow-ups: - Consider exposing recommended budget defaults in the Private operator guide once we validate prod behavior across 2+ regions. - Evaluate whether control plane should push pool-specific budgets for Dedicated customers with known KMS quotas.

Related: ENG-4821, ENG-4825, ENG-4828; incident context SUP-19377; complements pr-18563 (retry storm hotfix), pr-18449 (circuit breaker), pr-18502 (config guardrails).

source.title: adaptive qk-kv fusion with prefetching and rotary interleave for long-context throughput
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: Long-context workloads (>=16k tokens) dominate several customer workloads. Existing FlashAttention and separate QK/KV kernels yield suboptimal memory traffic when serving long sequences with continuous batching and active KV caching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel strategy that fuses QK compute and KV accumulation paths where beneficial, plus a lightweight KV prefetcher and a rotary-interleave layout to reduce memory bandwidth and kernel launch overhead. Design: 1) New fused kernels (CUDA) that compute QK scores and simultaneously write/aggregate KV fragments for future decode steps. These kernels are selected when sequence-length, batch composition, and kv cache hit heuristics indicate net win. 2) Adaptive kernel dispatcher: runtime heuristics (histogram of seq lengths, batch fill factor, GPU occupancy) choose between FlashAttention variants, fused QK-KV path, or the legacy split kernels. 3) KV prefetcher: background thread schedules small prefetches of cache pages for the most likely next-tokens based on route-level bandit policy, reducing tail latency on cache-miss heavy workloads. 4) Rotary interleave: reorganizes rotary embeddings in-memory to make fused kernels use contiguous loads for q/k/v, reducing strided accesses on long context. Implementation notes: - CUDA kernels implemented in runtime/attention with careful register/block tuning for Ampere/Blackwell GPUs. - Dispatcher lives in runtime/attention/kernel_dispatcher.cc and exposes a policy hook for testing via env vars. - Continuous batcher updated to expose batch shape histograms and to annotate requests with expected next-token probabilities (used by prefetcher). - Small pinned-memory prefetch buffer added to runtime/kv_cache, with heuristics to avoid polluting hot-cache entries. Tests & validation: microbenchmarks (tests/benchmarks/attn_long_context.py) show median throughput gain of 18% on 32k-context benchmarks vs previous flash-attn-only baseline, p50 decode latency reduced 12% for batched streaming, and token-cost reduction of ~9% under production-like mixes due to fewer kernel launches and lower GPU memory traffic. We ran full GPU integration tests, perf canary, and private deployment smoke tests. Rollout plan: feature gated behind env REDWOOD_FEATURE_ADAPTIVE_FUSE and opt-in in Dedicated as default off for first 2 weeks. Safety: we added deterministic fallbacks and correctness unit tests comparing fused outputs to reference split-kernel outputs for a variety of dtype/seq-length/model-size combinations. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. See release_notes for user-visible improvements.
commits: a3f4c9e: add fused qk-kv CUDA kernels and tests
b7d11f2: kernel dispatcher and policy heuristics
c05e2a8: add kv prefetcher and pinned buffer management
d1b9f3e: rotary interleave layout transform and loader
e66a4b0: expose env flags and add docs/perf writeup
f90c2e1: minor lint and CI fixes
source.discussion: Ethan Park: Can we get deterministic unit tests that cover rotary offsets and half-precision edge cases? Maya Chen: Added tests in tests/unit/test_fused_vs_split.py that compare fp16/fp32 outputs and several rotary offset patterns across seq lengths up to 32k. Also added a CI perf gate that runs a reduced microbenchmark on each PR. Sofia Ruiz: Concerned about polluting the KV cache with speculative prefetches for low-probability tokens. How do we avoid regressions for small batch sizes? Maya Chen: Prefetcher uses a conservative bandit with decay and an LRU guard; for small batches (<4) prefetching is disabled. We also added telemetry to monitor prefetch hit/miss ratios and an env flag REDWOOD_PREFETCH_ENABLE to turn this off. Liam O'Connor: Any impact on memory footprint for multi-tenant Dedicated? Maya Chen: Pinned buffer defaults to 32MB per GPU and is configurable by deployment; we added a guard to cap total pinned usage to 1% of GPU memory by default. QA: Found a race in the dispatcher when batch histogram updates happen concurrently — fixed in commit f90c2e1 and added a mutex around histogram writes. Post-merge: ops will run a canary for two days on a small fleet before enabling the feature by default for internal staging.
source.release_notes: Improves long-context inference throughput and latency by introducing an adaptive fused QK-KV kernel path, KV prefetching, and memory-layout optimizations for rotary embeddings. Gains: ~18% throughput on 32k-context benchmarks, ~12% p50 decode latency reduction, ~9% token-cost reduction in mixed workloads. Feature is behind REDWOOD_FEATURE_ADAPTIVE_FUSE and off by default for Dedicated; safe rollout planned.

source.title: decouple-serializer-from-affinity-and-introduce-cooperative-serialize-queues
source.author: Avery Chen
source.description: Motivation: our telemetry and recent incident traces show a non-trivial CPU-side bottleneck in the request hotpath caused by blocking serialization work that holds thread-affinity (OS-level pinning) and prevents efficient cooperative scheduling between IO, serializer, and runtime workers. This PR separates the concerns: (1) introduce a lightweight cooperative serialization queue that runs on worker threads without requiring permanent thread pinning, (2) decouple the serializer state machine from thread-affinity so short critical sections can opportunistically run on any available worker, and (3) add backpressure signals to the queue which feed into the scheduler to dynamically throttle batch assembly when CPU saturation is detected. Design summary: add runtime/serialize_queue.{h,cpp} implementing a fixed-size ring queue with lock-free handover for small payloads and a CAS-protected overflow path for large messages. Add a SerializerActor that owns transient per-request buffers but is not bound to a specific CPU core; a short move-to-stack and memcpy micro-optimization keeps copies minimal. Thread pool changes: make ThreadPool::pinning optional at task-granularity, introduce ThreadPool::submit_cooperative(task) which participates in the cooperative scheduling loop. KV cache interaction: when a request hits KV cache for a prefix, the cache lookup now signals the serialize queue to short-circuit full marshalling when cached prefix covers >70% of tokens. Rollout strategy: feature-flag guarded, defaults off for Dedicated regions until we run a gated canary. Benchmarks: integration perf runs on our internal perf-lab show median tail (P95) endpoint latency improvement of 9.2% on mixed small/medium requests, median CPU sys-time for request workers fell from 42% to 29% under the same load profile (16 concurrent clients), and end-to-end tokens/sec improved by 12% for continuous-batching workloads due to reduced serialization stalls. Safety and testing: unit tests for the queue and overflow path, stress tests with randomized task yields, and end-to-end perf harness runs included. Compatibility: no API change to external clients. Rollback plan: flip feature flag and revert the small patch in main that wires up the queue. Notes: this is intentionally conservative on the overflow path to avoid introducing GC/alloc churn in the hotpath.
commits: a9e3f12: introduce serialize queue and cooperative submit API
b4c29d7: wire serializer actor into request hotpath and add short-copy micro-optimizations
cfe4b5a: kv cache short-circuit hook and backpressure signal
d1a7f88: tests and perf harness updates
e55b021: docs and feature-flag gating
review_comments: Marco Ruiz: Nice separation. Two requests: add a brief comment explaining why overflow path uses a small heap allocation and ensure metrics expose overflow count.
Avery Chen: Added overflow_count metric and comment, kept allocation path for large messages per perf guidance (see tests).
Priya Natarajan: The cooperative submit API looks good; can we add a configuration knob to disable coop scheduling per pool?
Avery Chen: Added ThreadPool config 'enable_coop_submit' and defaulted to true for runtime pools and false for legacy pinned pools.
Zoe Patel: LGTM after addressing thread-safety review comments and adding a regression perf test for continuous batching.
Daniel Kim: Verified no increase in memory alloc churn in a 24h canary. Approving for merge.
source.release_notes: Improve CPU-side request throughput by introducing cooperative serialization queues and decoupling serializer state from strict thread-affinity. Expect ~10% tail latency reduction and ~12% token throughput increase for continuous-batching workloads. Rollout gated behind feature flag.

source.title: Introduce lightweight tracepoint sampler and batch-pressure microbench for scheduler visibility
source.author: Aisha Kapoor
source.release_notes: Adds a lightweight tracepoint sampler and a small microbenchmark that measures batch-pressure and scheduler stall contributions. Exposed a perf dashboard template for quick triage. No user-facing API changes.
source.body:
Motivation: We need a low-overhead way to correlate scheduler decisions, KV-cache hot prefixes, and continuous-batching pressure in production-like workloads. Existing NVTX and perfetto exports are useful but either too heavy or too coarse for iterative scheduler tuning. This PR introduces a lightweight tracepoint sampler plus a compact microbench harness that reproduces batch-pressure scenarios seen in customer workloads.

What this PR does: 1) Introduces a TracepointSampler module (C++): periodic, low-payload tracepoints emitted around scheduler windowing and key KV-cache touchpoints. Sampling is rate-limited and context-tagged so it is safe to enable in Dedicated/Private pockets for short experiments. 2) Adds a BatchPressureMicrobench: a small harness that simulates mixed arrival patterns (burst+poisson), tracks batch fill/evict cycles, and reports per-step metrics (queue depth, avg-batch-size, scheduler-latency). 3) Adds a lightweight exporter template that converts sampler output to a compact CSV/JSON line format and a dashboard YAML for the perf observability stack. 4) Provides unit and integration tests for correctness and a CI bench job to run the microbench in controlled runners.

Design notes: - TracepointSampler emits an inline 64-bit tag plus epoch counter; it avoids heavy NVTX payloads to keep overhead < 0.2% in our microbench. - The sampler can be configured by percent-sampling or deterministic-epoch windows; configuration lives under runtime.conf under [tracepoint_sampler]. - The bench harness focuses on reproducing scheduler backpressure (when batches are delayed waiting for KV-cache or kernel queues). It is intentionally small so it can be run in CI and locally.

Benchmarks: Local runs on an r5-like simulated env show: - 0.12% median runtime overhead with sampler enabled at 1% sampling. - Microbench reproduces a clear inflection when batch-timeouts exceed 6ms and shows scheduler queue growth matching production traces. Included CSV outputs for two representative runs in bench/outputs/*.csv.

Testing & CI: - Added unit tests for sampler tagging and epoch wrap-around. - Added a bench CI job (ci/bench/bench-runner.yml) that executes microbench in a small container; results are archived as artifacts. CI initially failed due to a missing clock dependency in the test harness; fixed in second commit.

Migration / Rollout: This is a safe, opt-in instrumentation path. Default sampling is disabled in production configs. We recommend: 1) enable 0.1% sampling on a canary Dedicated node for 24 hours, 2) collect artifacts and load into the dashboard template, 3) iterate sampling/window params and run microbench locally to validate hypotheses.

Follow-ups: - Wire sampler output to the existing perf-trace ingestion pipeline (ENG-1185). - Add a lightweight JS/TS helper to download and visualise CSVs in the console (ENG-1190).

Related: builds on prior instrumentation work but intentionally avoids heavy NVTX payloads and Perfetto dependency so we can run in constrained CI nodes. See related PRs: ENG-93547-style batching probes and ENG-93421 (kv-cache sim) for context.
commits: a1b2c3d: add TracepointSampler (core implementation, config)
d4e5f6g: add batch-pressure microbench harness and example workloads
h7i8j9k: exporter template and perf dashboard YAML
l0m1n2o: add unit tests and CI bench job; fix clock dependency
p3q4r5s: update docs and usage examples
review_comments: Ben Ortiz (review): Can the sampler be further optimized to avoid atomic ops on the hot path? Suggested use of relaxed-atomics or sharded counters.
Aisha (reply): Switched to thread-local counters for the hot path and only use atomics on flush. Bench shows negligible change in overhead after update.
Sofia Müller (review): Dashboard YAML looks good but we should add a panel for 'avg-batch-wait-ms' and a simple alert for sustained queue growth.
Aisha (reply): Added panel and an example alert rule in the template; alert thresholds are conservative and documented.
Ethan Park (suggestion): Tests flaked on CI when container clock skewed. Consider using steady_clock mocks in tests.
Aisha (reply): Replaced system_clock usage with steady_clock for tests and added a clock mock helper in runtime/tests/utils/clock_mock.h
