source.title: Adaptive kernel routing and KV-scheduler tweaks for Mistral/Qwen (GQA/MQA tuning)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: production workloads showed inconsistent latency/throughput tradeoffs across model families (Mistral, Qwen, Llama variants) when using a single kernel selection and KV cache routing policy. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel routing layer and a lightweight KV-scheduler that chooses sharding and prefetch priorities per model and request workload, plus targeted GQA/MQA tuning knobs. The goals are: reduce p50/p95 latency variance, increase sustained throughput for mixed-sequence traffic, and avoid tail regressions when switching model variants. Summary of changes (single-line condensed): implement model profiles for kernel selection and quantization-aware algorithms; add KV-scheduler with per-route priority and prefetch window; continuous-batching changes to respect model affinity and early-flush heuristics; small Llama-specific attention correction for 8-bit MQA flows; Qwen memory layout workaround to avoid kernel thrash on long-context streams; add microbench harness + regression tests + docs updates. Checklist: 1) Perf harness and baseline numbers included 2) Unit tests for scheduler logic 3) Integration canary on perf-canary passed 4) Added release notes and console docs 5) Backwards-compatible config defaults. Commits summary: 1) "model-profiles: add adaptive kernel routing and profile schema" 2) "runtime: KV-scheduler implementation and tests" 3) "batcher: affinity and early-flush integration" 4) "mistral: 8-bit attention edge-case fix" 5) "qwen: continuous-kernel fallback for long context" 6) "docs: runtime tuning guide and release notes" Changed areas (high-level file list): runtime/kernel_router.cpp; runtime/kernel_selector.h; runtime/kv_scheduler.{cc,h}; runtime/batching/continuous_batcher.cpp; models/mistral/attention_fixes.cpp; models/qwen/kernel_fallback.cpp; perf/bench/kv_sched_harness.py; docs/runtime/optimizations.md; tests/runtime/test_kv_scheduler.py. Small illustrative snippets (kept minimal): added selection score calculation in kernel_router.cpp to prefer quantization-aware kernels for MQA flows; KV-scheduler uses LRU-backed prefetch window with a per-model weight. Tests and CI: initial CI run hit an intermittent GPU smoke failure in integration/gpu-smoke (nvidia-alloc-timeout), retried and passed; full perf-canary run showed 6-12% median latency reduction for Mistral-7B workloads (p50 down 8% median), and 18-24% increase in sustained tokens/sec for mixed short+long sequences on Qwen-14B when MQA profile enabled. Llama 2-13B 8-bit flows showed a 4% p95 improvement after the attention edge-case fix. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout for 2 weeks on dedicated cluster subset, followed by staged enablement per customer for private deployments; follow-up ENG task to instrument model-profile telemetry and auto-tune heuristics. Backwards compatibility: default kernel_router policy remains legacy until feature flag is enabled; config knobs documented. Release notes: performance: adaptive kernel routing and KV-scheduler reduce latency variance and improve throughput across Mistral/Qwen/LLM families; fixes: Mistral 8-bit attention correctness; notes: may improve or change token cost estimates due to different kernel selection. Related tickets: ENG-7684 (runtime perf initiative), ENG-7721 (KV scheduling design).
commits: model-profiles: add adaptive kernel routing and profile schema
runtime: KV-scheduler implementation and tests
batcher: affinity and early-flush integration
mistral: 8-bit attention edge-case fix
qwen: continuous-kernel fallback for long context
docs: runtime tuning guide and release notes
review_comments: Diego Morales: Please add a short section in the docs about the cost accounting impact of kernel switches — looks good otherwise.
Ava Chen: Added docs paragraph and note in release notes about token-cost variance when adaptive routing is enabled.
Sarah Patel: Can you add a unit test covering the prefetch window eviction path? Current tests don't hit the edge-case.
Ava Chen: Added deterministic eviction unit test and updated test harness to simulate long-tail request patterns.
Ethan Brooks: Observed a small memory spike on Qwen long-context runs during canary; did you confirm the fallback path prevents thrash?
Ava Chen: Yes, added max-fallback limit and instrumented metric kv_scheduler.fallback_count; canary shows fallback_count < 1% in steady state.
release_notes: Adaptive kernel routing: per-model profiles choose quantization-aware kernels to improve MQA/GQA throughput.
KV-scheduler: reduces tail latency by prefetching hot KV shards and applying per-route priority.
Mistral fix: correctness edge-case for 8-bit attention flows.
Qwen: kernel fallback for long-context to avoid repeated kernel re-compilation thrash.
Console: runtime tuning guide added to docs.

source.title: scheduler: jittered slot reassignment and KV-warmup throttling
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.release_notes: Introduces jittered slot reassignment to reduce synchronized preemption, adds KV-warmup throttling to avoid cache stampedes, and exposes two new tuning knobs for batcher schedulers. Improves p95 latency under mixed workloads and prevents starvation observed in multi-tenant bursts.
source.description: Context: In high-concurrency mixed workloads we observed correlated slot reclamations and KV cache warmups causing transient starvation and p95 latency spikes. This PR implements two complementary runtime controls: (1) jittered slot reassignment to spread slot release/reassignment events across short randomized windows (configurable), and (2) a KV-warmup throttler that staggers cold-KV prefetches and enforces a soft admission budget per-slot to avoid simultaneous heavy prefetches. Changes: Implements a new scheduler helper (jitter_reassign) which inserts small randomized offsets before slot handoff and coordinates with the slot allocator to avoid churn; implements kv_warmup_throttle which tracks per-model warmup tokens and decays budgets based on successful cache hits; exposes scheduler.tuning.jitter_ms and kv.warmup.budget_rate config knobs; updates continuous_batcher admission path to consult warmup budget before immediate prefetch; adds lightweight metrics (jitter.delay_ms distribution, kv.warmup.throttle_rejections) and dashboard panels. Commits: "add jittered slot reassignment helper"; "implement kv warmup token bucket and decay"; "plumb knobs through config and expose metrics"; "perf tests and bench harness for mixed workloads". Changed files (high-level): runtime/scheduler/jitter_reassign.go; runtime/scheduler/slot_allocator.go (small API); runtime/kv/warmup_throttle.go; runtime/batcher/continuous_batcher.go (admission path); metrics/registry.go (new metrics); docs/benchmarks/jitter_warmup_readme.md; tests/perf/test_jittered_reassign.py; tests/unit/test_kv_warmup_throttle.go; ci/workflows/perf.yml. Motivation: Previous approaches (hard timeouts and global cooldowns) either caused head-of-line blocking or were too coarse-grained for multi-tenant bursts. The jittered reassignment spreads out reassignments to reduce synchronized pressure on the slot allocator and downstream kernel selection; warmup throttling prevents cache stampedes and reduces wasted kernel invocations. Performance: On internal perf harness using a 64-GPU cluster and mixed 75% short, 25% long requests we saw median latency change ~+2ms, p95 reduced ~18%, p99 reduced ~10%, and KV cache miss storms reduced by ~42% in burst scenarios. CPU/GPU utilization improved: fewer small kernel startups and more sustained larger kernels resulting in 6% lower cost/throughput token. Testing: unit tests for token decay and edge cases; perf tests covering staggered bursts, tenant fairness, and long-tail KV loads; load test in staging with warmup budget default of 10 tokens/sec. CI: initial perf run showed a flaky bench; reran perf pipeline with increased harness stabilization and all checks passed. Rollout plan: feature-flagged behind runtime.scheduler.jittered_reassign (default enabled for dedicated clusters, opt-in for hosted at 10% rollout), staged ramp: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with automated rollback on regression signals (p95 increase >5% or error rates >0.1%). Backout: config toggle and immediate revert PR prepared if needed. Related: references ENG-4219 for prioritized scheduling work, ENG-4291 for KV cache prefetch follow-up. Checklist: design doc attached in Linear; unit tests added; perf benchmark included; docs updated.
commits: add jittered slot reassignment helper (jitter_reassign)
implement kv warmup token bucket with decay
plumb config knobs and metrics
integrate warmup throttle into continuous_batcher admission
perf harness tests and docs
review_comments: Ethan Clarke: Can you explain why a randomized jitter is better than exponentially backoffed retries for reassignment?

Priya Natarajan (reply): Jitter reduces synchronized events across slots; exponential backoff pushes responsibility to requesters and can still cause correlated retries across tenants. We benchmarked both and jitter had lower p95 in mixed bursts.
Maya Rios: Please add a test for the warmup token decay edge case where decay interval is extended by scheduler stall.
Priya Natarajan (reply): Added test_kv_warmup_throttle.go covering stall+decay scenario and updated comments to document expected behavior.
Liam Zhang: Suggest exposing metrics with rate-normalized panels to make alerting simpler.
Priya Natarajan (reply): Updated metrics/registry.go to publish per-minute normalized counters and added dashboard panels.
source.merge_outcome: merged via squash on 2025-11-16 by Maya Rios
source.notes: Configuration defaults chosen conservatively after staging runs; knobs available for customers on Dedicated. No customer-visible API changes. Monitoring and alerts added to Console.

Rollout plan: feature-flagged behind runtime.scheduler.jittered_reassign (default enabled for dedicated clusters, opt-in for hosted at 10% rollout), staged ramp: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% with automated rollback on regression signals (p95 increase >5% or error rates >0.1%). Backout: config toggle and immediate revert PR prepared if needed. Related: references ENG-4219 for prioritized scheduling work, ENG-4291 for KV cache prefetch follow-up. Checklist: design doc attached in Linear; unit tests added; perf benchmark included; docs updated.

source.title: Reduce CPU syscalls and thread handoff on request path
source.author: Aisha Kumar
source.description: Motivation: CPU-side bottlenecks on the request hot path were dominated by frequent syscalls and expensive thread handoffs when requests crossed the network/IO boundary. This PR reduces syscall frequency and simplifies handoff between acceptor/dispatcher threads and worker pools to lower tail latency and overall CPU usage. Summary of approach: 1) Replace a large fraction of blocking read()/write()/select() syscalls on the hot path with an edge-triggered epoll-based poll loop + per-core submission queues to amortize kernel transitions; 2) Introduce a light-weight per-core MPMC ring buffer (lock-free where supported) used to hand off ready requests to CPU-local worker shards instead of parking/unparking threads; 3) Move request serialization (marshalling) off the acceptor path onto a short-lived serialization worker that batches small requests together (continuous batching window = 1.5ms configurable) to reduce per-request syscall and kernel scheduling pressure; 4) Make KV cache lookups prefetch-friendly and avoid cacheline thrash by introducing per-shard micro-ttl caches and partitioned LRU lists; 5) Reduce futex/wake cycles by aggregating wake events using a shared event-coalescer and using eventfd on Linux where available. Results: On our internal bench (conv1k latency-heavy mix) we see a 28-35% reduction in 99th percentile CPU time spent in syscall/wakeup handling and a 12-18% improvement in overall token throughput on c5d-large simulation (worker-limited). Tail latency (p99.9) decreased by ~9% for small requests (<128 tokens). Backwards compatibility and safety: All changes are behind a feature flag (runtime.enable_syscall_reduction) and fall back to the original path when disabled. New primitives are exercised via unit + integration tests, and we added a runtime smoke test that toggles the feature flag under load. Details of changes: commits, files touched, and notes below.
commits: a3f8b28: introduce per-core submission queues and ring buffer (initial)
d4c9e1a: add continuous batching worker and batching window config
b7d41c9: move serialization into dedicated worker with batch marshaller
f2e7b55: partition KV cache into per-shard micro-ttls
c9a8d34: replace blocking poll with epoll edge-triggered loop + eventfd coalescer
e11b2a1: add tests and perf harness, update docs and feature-flagging
small_snippets: +++ runtime/threading/submission_ring.h: added lock-free MPMC ring API (try_push/try_pop)
- blocking acceptor path: used to call notify_worker() on every socket event
+ new path: push descriptor into per-core ring; worker polls ring and performs a single epoll_wait for batched sockets
review_comments: Marcus Lee: Please add a comment explaining why the ring is bounded and how capacity is chosen; also add metrics for ring-full events.
Aisha Kumar: Added ring capacity metric and docs; default chosen as 4096 entries after load tuning.
Sofia Ramos: Concern about priority inversion when heavy serialization batches starve small low-latency requests. Please add a max-batch-bytes and an override path for urgent requests.
Aisha Kumar: Implemented max-batch-bytes (configurable) and urgent flag that bypasses batching; added tests covering urgent path.
benchmarks: baseline: throughput 14.3k tokens/s, p99 handler latency 68ms
this PR: throughput 16.2k tokens/s (+13.3%), p99 handler latency 62ms (-8.8%), syscall CPU cost -31%
notes_on_porting: Feature flag runtime.enable_syscall_reduction defaults to false for now; enable via console or env var REDWOOD_SYSFLAG_SYSCALL_REDUCTION=1
Windows: eventfd path is no-op; the fallback uses WaitForMultipleObjects with coalescing logic. See runtime/net/poller_win.cpp
Observability: added metrics submission_ring_push_failures, batcher_avg_size, ring_fill_percent per-core
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: ring full during sudden traffic spikes -> drop to fallback path (original acceptor) and emit metric. Mitigation: metric-driven autoscale and conservative default ring size.
Risk: batching could increase latency for single-request workloads -> mitigated via small default batching window (1.5ms) and urgent bypass flag.
Risk: lock-free primitives subtle on older kernels/arch -> guarded by runtime.use_lockfree_rings; we fall back to mutex-backed queue on unsupported archs.
source.release_notes: Performance: Reduced kernel-transition overhead on the request path by introducing per-core submission queues, coalesced event wakeups, and configurable continuous batching. Enabled behind runtime.enable_syscall_reduction.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash by Marcus Lee after approvals. All CI green and perf guardrails met.
source.backcompat: No API changes. Feature gated and defaults preserved. Config docs updated in docs/feature_flags.md.
source.testing: Unit tests for ring buffer and batch marshaller added; integration test toggles feature flag under load; perf harness run nightly for 2 weeks pre-merge.
source.changelog_entry: perf: reduce CPU syscalls/wakeups on hot path via per-core rings, epoll coalescing, and small continuous batching (feature-flagged)

source.title: Introduce jurisdiction-aware assertion proxy, KMS consent router, and audit enrichment shim
source.author: Aisha Gupta
source.release_notes: Added an assertion proxy and KMS routing middleware to enable jurisdictional residency decisions and enriched audit headers; feature gated behind `jurisdiction_assertion` and rolled out with staged canary.
source.description: Motivation: customers with mixed-residency fleets asked for a deterministic place to evaluate operator/service jurisdiction assertions so KMS usage and audit provenance can be routed safely without sprinkling logic across services. This PR introduces an assertion proxy (service: residency-assertor) that normalizes incoming claims, evaluates configured jurisdiction policies, and emits a compact proof token used by downstream KMS routing and audit enrichment. It also adds a KMS Consent Router to pick an appropriate CMK/region and a lightweight audit-enrichment shim that attaches pseudonymous residency and proof metadata to audit events. Implementation summary: 1) residency-assertor service (new binary) with a small policy DSL converted into OPA-compatible JSON; 2) kms-consent-router middleware integrated into the existing kms-proxy client path with a decision cache and TTL; 3) audit-enricher sidecar that subscribes to commit log and augments events with residency_proof and audit_cohort tags; 4) feature flags and rollout controls (config path: /deploy/feature-flags/jurisdiction_assertion.json) and a canary rollout plan. Security and compliance notes: decisions compute a minimal proof (HMAC-signed) containing issuer, asserted_region, policy_id and exp; the HMAC key is stored in KMS and rotated via existing rotation pipeline; audit enrichment strips any direct tenant-identifiers and emits cohort hashes to maintain investigatability without exposing PII. Backwards compatibility: all services fall back to existing routing when the feature flag is off or residency-assertor returns no-decision. Performance: decision cache and async enrichment keep tail latency impact <5ms p95 on request path in our local benchmarks. Checklist: - [x] Policy DSL parser and unit tests - [x] kms-consent-router integrated and e2e tests - [x] audit-enricher implemented with audit schema migration + migration test harness - [x] Feature flag gating and canary rollout manifest - [x] Documentation: runbook + threat model note and operator guide - [x] Added telemetry: rbac_decisionlatencies, kms_routing_hits, audit_enrich_failures Rollout plan: staged canary to 5% tenants in region-us then 20% then global over two weeks; automated rollback triggers on >1% enrichment failures or >10% request-latency degradation. Related work: builds on ENG-991 (decision-cache) and SEC-88 (audit-envelope revamp). Links: linear/ENG-2345, internal-docs/ops/jurisdiction-rules. Testing: unit coverage 94% for new modules; integration pipeline added: unit -> lint -> e2e/kms-stub -> canary-simulation; CI transient: one flaky integration rerun but green on retry. Merge strategy and outcome: squash merge; post-merge migration job scheduled to backfill audit cohorts for 7 days. Notes for reviewers: pay attention to policy DSL escaping rules and HMAC key rotation path; reviewers asked to validate migration safety and to run the canary simulation tool locally.

source.title: token-span-attribution-and-adaptive-alert-smoothing
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description:
Motivation:
We currently surface token-level cost and latency aggregations but lack a clear way to attribute SLO impact to specific token spans (e.g. model prompt prefix vs generated tail) and we get frequent alert flapping on percentile SLOs when low-volume, high-cost tails show up. This PR introduces two complementary capabilities: 1) span-level token attribution traces and rollups keyed by token position buckets (prefix/mid/suffix) and cache-affinity (kv-hit vs kv-miss); and 2) an adaptive alert-smoothing layer that reduces noise for route-level token-cost SLOs using a decayed-reservoir quantile estimator that weights tokens by cost and recency.

What this changes (high level):
- Runtime telemetry: emit token-level spans with attributes: token_index, span_bucket (prefix|mid|suffix), kv_affinity (hit|miss), token_cost_units. These are synthesized as aggregated metrics and lightweight trace annotations to avoid high-cardinality explosion.
- Metrics rollups: new Prometheus/OpenMetrics series and Cortex rollup pipeline config to produce: rwd_token_span_cost_sum, rwd_token_span_cost_count, rwd_token_span_latency_p75/p95/p99 per route and per span_bucket.
- Sampling strategy: a token-aware reservoir sampler in the tracing pipeline that biases retention probability proportionally to token_cost_units and recent latency anomalies to preserve informative tails.
- Alerting: SLO rules updated to reference the smoothed quantiles produced by the adaptive estimator; alerting notifications include the top contributing span_bucket breakdown and a compact token-cost histogram snapshot.
- Console: observability UI adds a per-route Token Span panel with heatmap, selectable span_bucket filters, and a toggle to show raw vs smoothed SLO curves for a chosen time window.
- Docs/Quickstart: example notebook showing how to interpret span attribution and how to tune smoothing window for a given workload.

Implementation notes:
- The runtime emits a compact token-span protobuf (rwd.trace.TokenSpan) and a new lightweight counter for kv-affinity to keep cardinality low; the code path in serving/runtime/telemetry is hardened to avoid sync disk writes.
- Reservoir estimator implemented as a fixed-memory sketch using a weighted priority queue and exponential decay; state is materialized at the rollup tier and also emitted as a derivative metric so alert rules can reference it directly without additional query-time compute overhead.
- Backwards compatibility: when normalized flags are off the system falls back to previous token aggregation metrics (no name changes to legacy meters).

Checklist:
- [x] Instrument runtime token-span emission (serving/runtime/telemetry)
- [x] Rollup pipeline config and transformation (observability/rollups)
- [x] Add Prometheus export and Cortex rules (observability/metrics)
- [x] Tracing sampler implementation and unit tests (observability/tracing)
- [x] Console UI panels and integration tests (console/frontend)
- [x] Docs and quickstart (docs/observability)
- [x] Load test (benchmark-lab token-heavy scenario)

Commits summary:
- feat(telemetry): token-span proto + runtime emitter
- feat(rollups): transform and rollup keys for span_bucket and kv_affinity
- feat(tracing): weighted reservoir token sampler with decay
- feat(metrics): exporter and prometheus rules expose span-level aggregates
- feat(console): token-span heatmap and smoothed SLO toggle
- docs: observability token-span guide and tuning examples

Files changed (high level):
- redwood/serving/runtime/telemetry/token_span.proto
- redwood/serving/runtime/telemetry/emitter.go
- redwood/observability/rollups/span_rollup_transform.yaml
- redwood/observability/tracing/reservoir_sampler.go
- redwood/observability/metrics/prom_rules/token_span_rules.yaml
- redwood/console/frontend/src/components/TokenSpanPanel.tsx
- redwood/docs/observability/token-span.md
- benchmark-lab/scenarios/token-heavy/20k_requests.yaml
- redwood/quickstarts/observability/token-span-quickstart.ipynb

Testing and CI:
- Unit tests added for reservoir sampler edge cases (zero-weight, overflow) and rollup transformation invariants.
- End-to-end test in benchmark-lab simulating 10k requests with varying prompt lengths to validate sampler retention and alert smoothing behavior.
- CI: all automated checks passed on first run; load test executed in a post-merge pipeline.

Review conversation (high level):
- Diego Alvarez: "This addresses noise we've seen on /chat during the November spike. Two asks: add a small p50 metric for each span_bucket and ensure the sampler is configurable per-tenant."
- Author reply: "Added p50 rollup and moved sampler config to tenant-visible config with safe defaults. See emitter.go and config docs."
- Priya Natarajan: "Concern about metric cardinality -- how are we preventing explosion for many routes?"
- Author reply: "We only emit span_bucket and kv_affinity dimensions at the rollup layer and sample traces at a rate bounded by the reservoir size. The raw token spans are not indexed in the long-term TSDB."
- Liam O'Sullivan: "Minor: please include a console screenshot or gif in the docs for reviewers."
- Author reply: "Added a small animated gif and an annotated screenshot to docs/observability/token-span.md."

Merge notes:
- PR merged via squash after approvals from all reviewers. Post-merge: rolled out metric transforms to staging then production during a low-traffic window; no alerts fired during initial ramp. Load tests showed a 3.1% CPU increase on the telemetry emitter and a 0.6% increase in Cortex ingestion cost; both within acceptable bounds per PM-421.

Release notes:
- "Observability: Added token-span attribution metrics and adaptive alert-smoothing to reduce SLO noise and surface per-route span-level cost breakdowns. Console UI includes a Token Span heatmap and smoothed SLO visualization. Tunable sampler and tenant-level knobs included."

Rollback plan:
- Disable sampler flag (runtime config) and revert rollup transform; legacy metrics remain available.

Followups (open):
- ENG-8010: Add tenant-level retention budgeting for sampled token spans (scheduled Q1 2026)
- ENG-8041: Expose a per-route cost forecast using span attribution in Optimize recommendations

Notes for oncall:
- If SLO alerts go into a flapping pattern after deployment, toggle the alert-smoothing feature off via observability/feature_flags and open ENG-8010.
commits: feat(telemetry): token-span proto + emitter
feat(rollups): span_bucket rollup transform
feat(tracing): weighted reservoir sampler
feat(metrics): prometheus rules for span aggregates
feat(console): token-span UI and tests
docs: token-span guide and gif
review_comments: Diego Alvarez: Please add p50 and tenant-level sampler config.
Maya Chen: Added p50 and made sampler tenant-configurable.
Priya Natarajan: How are we protecting against cardinality growth?
Maya Chen: Rollup-only dimensions and bounded reservoir sampler. See the rollup_transform for detail.
Liam O'Sullivan: Add UI screenshot/gif to docs.
Maya Chen: Added gif and screenshot to docs/token-span.md.
CI: All checks passed (unit, lint, integration, e2e).

- ENG-8010: Add tenant-level retention budgeting for sampled token spans (scheduled Q1 2026)
- ENG-8041: Expose a per-route cost forecast using span attribution in Optimize recommendations

Notes for oncall:
- If SLO alerts go into a flapping pattern after deployment, toggle the alert-smoothing feature off via observability/feature_flags and open ENG-8010.
text:
Motivation:
We currently surface token-level cost and latency aggregations but lack a clear way to attribute SLO impact to specific token spans (e.g. model prompt prefix vs generated tail) and we get frequent alert flapping on percentile SLOs when low-volume, high-cost tails show up. This PR introduces two complementary capabilities: 1) span-level token attribution traces and rollups keyed by token position buckets (prefix/mid/suffix) and cache-affinity (kv-hit vs kv-miss); and 2) an adaptive alert-smoothing layer that reduces noise for route-level token-cost SLOs using a decayed-reservoir quantile estimator that weights tokens by cost and recency.

What this changes (high level):
- Runtime telemetry: emit token-level spans with attributes: token_index, span_bucket (prefix|mid|suffix), kv_affinity (hit|miss), token_cost_units. These are synthesized as aggregated metrics and lightweight trace annotations to avoid high-cardinality explosion.
- Metrics rollups: new Prometheus/OpenMetrics series and Cortex rollup pipeline config to produce: rwd_token_span_cost_sum, rwd_token_span_cost_count, rwd_token_span_latency_p75/p95/p99 per route and per span_bucket.
- Sampling strategy: a token-aware reservoir sampler in the tracing pipeline that biases retention probability proportionally to token_cost_units and recent latency anomalies to preserve informative tails.
- Alerting: SLO rules updated to reference the smoothed quantiles produced by the adaptive estimator; alerting notifications include the top contributing span_bucket breakdown and a compact token-cost histogram snapshot.
- Console: observability UI adds a per-route Token Span panel with heatmap, selectable span_bucket filters, and a toggle to show raw vs smoothed SLO curves for a chosen time window.
- Docs/Quickstart: example notebook showing how to interpret span attribution and how to tune smoothing window for a given workload.

Implementation notes:
- The runtime emits a compact token-span protobuf (rwd.trace.TokenSpan) and a new lightweight counter for kv-affinity to keep cardinality low; the code path in serving/runtime/telemetry is hardened to avoid sync disk writes.
- Reservoir estimator implemented as a fixed-memory sketch using a weighted priority queue and exponential decay; state is materialized at the rollup tier and also emitted as a derivative metric so alert rules can reference it directly without additional query-time compute overhead.
- Backwards compatibility: when normalized flags are off the system falls back to previous token aggregation metrics (no name changes to legacy meters).

Checklist:
- [x] Instrument runtime token-span emission (serving/runtime/telemetry)
- [x] Rollup pipeline config and transformation (observability/rollups)
- [x] Add Prometheus export and Cortex rules (observability/metrics)
- [x] Tracing sampler implementation and unit tests (observability/tracing)
- [x] Console UI panels and integration tests (console/frontend)
- [x] Docs and quickstart (docs/observability)
- [x] Load test (benchmark-lab token-heavy scenario)

Commits summary:
- feat(telemetry): token-span proto + runtime emitter
- feat(rollups): transform and rollup keys for span_bucket and kv_affinity
- feat(tracing): weighted reservoir token sampler with decay
- feat(metrics): exporter and prometheus rules expose span-level aggregates
- feat(console): token-span heatmap and smoothed SLO toggle
- docs: observability token-span guide and tuning examples

Files changed (high level):
- redwood/serving/runtime/telemetry/token_span.proto
- redwood/serving/runtime/telemetry/emitter.go
- redwood/observability/rollups/span_rollup_transform.yaml
- redwood/observability/tracing/reservoir_sampler.go
- redwood/observability/metrics/prom_rules/token_span_rules.yaml
- redwood/console/frontend/src/components/TokenSpanPanel.tsx
- redwood/docs/observability/token-span.md
- benchmark-lab/scenarios/token-heavy/20k_requests.yaml
- redwood/quickstarts/observability/token-span-quickstart.ipynb

Testing and CI:
- Unit tests added for reservoir sampler edge cases (zero-weight, overflow) and rollup transformation invariants.
- End-to-end test in benchmark-lab simulating 10k requests with varying prompt lengths to validate sampler retention and alert smoothing behavior.
- CI: all automated checks passed on first run; load test executed in a post-merge pipeline.

Review conversation (high level):
- Diego Alvarez: "This addresses noise we've seen on /chat during the November spike. Two asks: add a small p50 metric for each span_bucket and ensure the sampler is configurable per-tenant."
- Author reply: "Added p50 rollup and moved sampler config to tenant-visible config with safe defaults. See emitter.go and config docs."
- Priya Natarajan: "Concern about metric cardinality -- how are we preventing explosion for many routes?"
- Author reply: "We only emit span_bucket and kv_affinity dimensions at the rollup layer and sample traces at a rate bounded by the reservoir size. The raw token spans are not indexed in the long-term TSDB."
- Liam O'Sullivan: "Minor: please include a console screenshot or gif in the docs for reviewers."
- Author reply: "Added a small animated gif and an annotated screenshot to docs/observability/token-span.md."

Merge notes:
- PR merged via squash after approvals from all reviewers. Post-merge: rolled out metric transforms to staging then production during a low-traffic window; no alerts fired during initial ramp. Load tests showed a 3.1% CPU increase on the telemetry emitter and a 0.6% increase in Cortex ingestion cost; both within acceptable bounds per PM-421.

Release notes:
- "Observability: Added token-span attribution metrics and adaptive alert-smoothing to reduce SLO noise and surface per-route span-level cost breakdowns. Console UI includes a Token Span heatmap and smoothed SLO visualization. Tunable sampler and tenant-level knobs included."

Rollback plan:
- Disable sampler flag (runtime config) and revert rollup transform; legacy metrics remain available.

Followups (open):
- ENG-8010: Add tenant-level retention budgeting for sampled token spans (scheduled Q1 2026)
- ENG-8041: Expose a per-route cost forecast using span attribution in Optimize recommendations

Notes for oncall:
- If SLO alerts go into a flapping pattern after deployment, toggle the alert-smoothing feature off via observability/feature_flags and open ENG-8010.

source.title: Introduce policy simulator with KMS proofing and audit-diff tooling
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.release_notes: Adds a dry-run policy simulator for RBAC v2 policies with optional signed KMS proofs for simulated decisions, plus an audit-diff report generator for compliance reviews.
source.description: Title: Introduce policy simulator with KMS proofing and audit-diff tooling. Motivation: Customers and internal auditors frequently ask for a way to validate RBAC v2 policy changes before rollout and to produce cryptographic proof that a proposed change does not widen region/KMS residency exposure. This PR adds a developer-facing simulator (rbac-sim) that evaluates a set of example requests against a candidate policy delta, emits a human- and machine-readable audit-diff, and (optionally) attaches a short-lived KMS-signed proof bundle that attests to the simulator inputs and outputs for downstream compliance workflows. This is intended to reduce post-deploy surprises, speed review cycles for Dedicated/Private deployments, and plug into our audit retention processes. Summary of changes (high level commits): 1) feat(simulator): introduce rbac-sim CLI and library with JSON policy delta apply and evaluation harness — high-level API, test suite and example fixtures (commit 1/6). 2) feat(audit-diff): add audit-diff generator that produces compact ndjson with decision-level deltas and counts for each service/operation combination (commit 2/6). 3) feat(kms-proof): add optional KMS proof signer that wraps the simulator run in a signed envelope (supporting both hosted KMS and customer CMKs via delegation tokens) (commit 3/6). 4) chore(ci): add unit and integration tests to workflow and container build step (commit 4/6). 5) docs: add quickstart and compliance playbook describing how to run sims against production policy snapshots and how to consume proof bundles (commit 5/6). 6) perf: small perf improvement to policy compile cache to keep simulator runs under 300ms for typical policy sets (commit 6/6). Changed areas/files (high level): - services/rbac/simulator/{main.go,simulator.go,policy_parser.go,fixtures/} - tools/rbac-sim/dockerfile and Makefile - libs/kms/proof_signer.go with integrations for hosted KMS and delegated CMK tokens - infra/ci/rbac-sim.yml (unit + policy integration tests) - docs/compliance/rbac-simulator.md and examples/compliance/playbook.md - testdata/policy-deltas/* (example deltas used in CI) Review notes and design decisions: - Simulator semantics intentionally mirror production PDP evaluation order; we reuse policy compile and the PDP cache. Edge cases for resource wildcards were clarified and documented. - KMS proofing is opt-in; the envelope contains a compact SHA256 of the input fixtures and the ndjson audit-diff, a timestamp, and a signer id. Proofs use existing hosted KMS signing key by default; for Dedicated/Private customers we accept a delegated CMK token and include the key fingerprint in the envelope metadata. - We modeled the audit-diff format around existing ndjson conventions used by the observability team so the output can be ingested into our log-retention and compliance pipelines without extra transformation. - Security review: ephemeral tokens for delegated CMK usage are validated against operator-scoped grants; we added additional guardrails to prevent simulator-initiated KMS requests from being used as an escalation vector. Testing and CI: - Unit tests added for policy parser edge cases, comparator and ndjson generation. - Integration test runs in CI spin up a minimal policy store and runs three representative deltas; artifacts are validated (ndjson schema + optional proof envelope signature verification). - CI checks passed on final run: build (pass), unit-tests (pass), integration-tests (pass), lint (pass). Review conversation (condensed into single timeline): - Ethan Chu (reviewer): "Nice to see a simulator — two asks: 1) make sure the simulator cannot call production KMS by default; 2) add a gated flag for proofing so that automated CI jobs don't accidentally sign artifacts with hosted keys." - Author (Priya): "Both addressed — default is dry-run-only and proofing requires explicit --enable-proof + env var pointing to KMS key alias. Added tests to assert the default behavior." - Marisol Vega (reviewer): "We should include a short retention policy recommendation for proof bundles — added a doc comment." - Samir Patel (security reviewer): "Verified token validation logic and put a small nit on log noise; asked for an additional audit check that the simulator writes a non-replayable nonce into the envelope." - Priya: "Added nonce and updated docs; also added an integration test that verifies nondeterminism across runs with the same inputs when nonce is present." - Merge readiness: reviewers approved after two rounds of fixes. CI green on approval. Observability and ops notes: - Simulator runs emit metrics under rbac.simulator.run.{success,failure,duration_ms} and proofing emits rbac.simulator.proof.sign.{success,failure}. - Console: added a minimal dashboard tile for recent simulator runs (docs include Grafana snippet). - Rollout plan: feature behind a feature flag rbac.simulator.enabled; default disabled in Hosted. Admins can enable per-tenant for Dedicated and Private installs. Compliance playbook: - How to run: ./tools/rbac-sim --policy-snapshot /path/to/snapshot --delta /path/to/delta.json --out audit-diff.ndjson --enable-proof --kms-key=alias/custom-cmk - How to review: the audit-diff groups changed allow/deny decisions by service and includes sample example requests that would change decision. Proof bundle contains base64(signed_envelope)+ndjson and a verification helper script for auditors. Migration and operational impact: - No runtime changes to PDP; simulator is a standalone tool and library. - Policy changes should still go through standard Linear flow; engineers are encouraged to attach audit-diff + proof to policy change PRs. - For Dedicated/Private customers, operator docs include steps to provide CMK delegation tokens and to configure short-term key usage. Security mitigations implemented: - Simulator KMS operations are rate-limited and annotated; we added a sandbox KMS path for Hosted to avoid accidental use of production keys in automated workflows. - Proof envelopes include signer id, key fingerprint, timestamp, nonce and a tight TTL (default 24h). - Added RBAC rule to prevent non-admin roles from creating proof-enabled simulator runs through the API wrapper. Known limitations and future work: - Current simulator validates policy deltas only; future work will support side-by-side multi-tenant scenarios where composite policy resolution is necessary. - We do not yet automatically attach simulator artifacts to Linear tickets; that will be a small follow-up (ENG-4369). Merge outcome: merged via squash/merge after approvals; release note drafted and added to changelog. Additional metadata: example command lines, proof envelope format and verification helper included in docs. Contact: priya@redwood.example for follow-ups.

This is intended to reduce post-deploy surprises, speed review cycles for Dedicated/Private deployments, and plug into our audit retention processes. Changed areas/files (high level): - services/rbac/simulator/{main.go,simulator.go,policy_parser.go,fixtures/} - tools/rbac-sim/dockerfile and Makefile - libs/kms/proof_signer.go with integrations for hosted KMS and delegated CMK tokens - infra/ci/rbac-sim.yml (unit + policy integration tests) - docs/compliance/rbac-simulator.md and examples/compliance/playbook.md - testdata/policy-deltas/* (example deltas used in CI) Review notes and design decisions: - Simulator semantics intentionally mirror production PDP evaluation order; we reuse policy compile and the PDP cache. Edge cases for resource wildcards were clarified and documented. - KMS proofing is opt-in; the envelope contains a compact SHA256 of the input fixtures and the ndjson audit-diff, a timestamp, and a signer id. Proofs use existing hosted KMS signing key by default; for Dedicated/Private customers we accept a delegated CMK token and include the key fingerprint in the envelope metadata. - We modeled the audit-diff format around existing ndjson conventions used by the observability team so the output can be ingested into our log-retention and compliance pipelines without extra transformation. - Security review: ephemeral tokens for delegated CMK usage are validated against operator-scoped grants; we added additional guardrails to prevent simulator-initiated KMS requests from being used as an escalation vector. Testing and CI: - Unit tests added for policy parser edge cases, comparator and ndjson generation. - Integration test runs in CI spin up a minimal policy store and runs three representative deltas; artifacts are validated (ndjson schema + optional proof envelope signature verification). - CI checks passed on final run: build (pass), unit-tests (pass), integration-tests (pass), lint (pass). Review conversation (condensed into single timeline): - Ethan Chu (reviewer): "Nice to see a simulator — two asks: 1) make sure the simulator cannot call production KMS by default; 2) add a gated flag for proofing so that automated CI jobs don't accidentally sign artifacts with hosted keys." - Author (Priya): "Both addressed — default is dry-run-only and proofing requires explicit --enable-proof + env var pointing to KMS key alias. Added tests to assert the default behavior." - Marisol Vega (reviewer): "We should include a short retention policy recommendation for proof bundles — added a doc comment." - Samir Patel (security reviewer): "Verified token validation logic and put a small nit on log noise; asked for an additional audit check that the simulator writes a non-replayable nonce into the envelope." - Priya: "Added nonce and updated docs; also added an integration test that verifies nondeterminism across runs with the same inputs when nonce is present." - Merge readiness: reviewers approved after two rounds of fixes. CI green on approval. Observability and ops notes: - Simulator runs emit metrics under rbac.simulator.run.{success,failure,duration_ms} and proofing emits rbac.simulator.proof.sign.{success,failure}. - Console: added a minimal dashboard tile for recent simulator runs (docs include Grafana snippet). - Rollout plan: feature behind a feature flag rbac.simulator.enabled; default disabled in Hosted. Admins can enable per-tenant for Dedicated and Private installs. Compliance playbook: - How to run: ./tools/rbac-sim --policy-snapshot /path/to/snapshot --delta /path/to/delta.json --out audit-diff.ndjson --enable-proof --kms-key=alias/custom-cmk - How to review: the audit-diff groups changed allow/deny decisions by service and includes sample example requests that would change decision. Proof bundle contains base64(signed_envelope)+ndjson and a verification helper script for auditors. Migration and operational impact: - No runtime changes to PDP; simulator is a standalone tool and library. - Policy changes should still go through standard Linear flow; engineers are encouraged to attach audit-diff + proof to policy change PRs. - For Dedicated/Private customers, operator docs include steps to provide CMK delegation tokens and to configure short-term key usage. Security mitigations implemented: - Simulator KMS operations are rate-limited and annotated; we added a sandbox KMS path for Hosted to avoid accidental use of production keys in automated workflows. - Proof envelopes include signer id, key fingerprint, timestamp, nonce and a tight TTL (default 24h). - Added RBAC rule to prevent non-admin roles from creating proof-enabled simulator runs through the API wrapper. Known limitations and future work: - Current simulator validates policy deltas only; future work will support side-by-side multi-tenant scenarios where composite policy resolution is necessary. - We do not yet automatically attach simulator artifacts to Linear tickets; that will be a small follow-up (ENG-4369). Merge outcome: merged via squash/merge after approvals; release note drafted and added to changelog. Additional metadata: example command lines, proof envelope format and verification helper included in docs. Contact: priya@redwood.example for follow-ups.

source.title: heterogeneous-attention-fusion-with-staged-scheduler-and-continuous-batching-support
source.author: Maya Singh
source.description: Motivation: reduce p99 latency regression for mixed-sequence workloads and improve throughput for production chat and retrieval runtimes by selecting optimal attention kernels at runtime and enabling continuous batching without sacrificing KV cache hit stability. Changes: Introduces a heterogeneous attention selection layer (HeteroAttn) that picks between a low-latency microkernel and a high-throughput Flash-like kernel based on sequence length heuristics and current queueing pressure; adds a staged scheduler that enforces a short-term reservation window for latency-sensitive requests and a batch-aggregation window for throughput requests; implements a cooperative KV cache eviction policy (staged LRU) that works with continuous batching to avoid repeated cache thrash during rollups; adds fused rotary/rope offset precomputation paths that avoid memcpy for common rotary patterns; integrates fp16 accumulation path with optional fp32 fallback for edge-case stability; exposes runtime knobs: ATTEN_SELECT_WINDOW_MS, KV_STAGED_WINDOW, CBATCH_MAX_WAIT_MS for tuned rollouts. Testing and validation: microbenchmarked on c6i-gpu clusters and internal perf-lab; added synthetic mixed-sequence workload harness and end-to-end chat replay traces. Results: median latency improved 6-9% on mid-sized seqs, p99 reduced up to 22% for mixed workloads, throughput increased 12% on long-sequence tails while KV miss rate decreased 18% under continuous batching. Rollout plan: staged canary on dedicated pool alpha -> 50% traffic after 72h, automatic rollback on p99 regressions >10% or error rate increase >0.5%. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. Known limitations: rare fp16 accumulation differences at extreme sequence lengths (documented and guarded by runtime flag), scheduler parameters tuned for our standard clusters; customers on small on-prem may require adjustments. Checklist: - [x] Benchmarks added to perf-lab harness - [x] Unit + integration tests for scheduler and KV policy - [x] Feature-flagged rollout - [x] Docs entry in runtime tuning guide - [x] Telemetry events for selection decisions
commits: 7f2b4d7: introduce hetero-attn selector and microkernel shim
c48a1b2: staged scheduler core loop and windowing primitives
a9c3f11: fused rotary precompute and fp16 accumulation path
d55b9a0: KV staged-LRU eviction and telemetry
e6f0c44: tests, perf harness integration and docs
source.diff_snippet: @@ packages/runtime/atten_selector.cc +75, -22 @@ // heuristic: prefer microkernel for seq <= 128 if queue depth < 4 bool choose_microkernel(int seq_len, int queue_depth) { return (seq_len <= 128 && queue_depth < 4) || (seq_len <= 256 && queue_depth == 0); } @@ packages/runtime/kv_cache/staged_lru.cc +200, -8 @@ // move hot prefixes to protected window to avoid eviction thrash void mark_protected(const PrefixKey &k) { protected_window_.push_front(k); if (protected_window_.size() > config_.protected_size) protected_window_.pop_back(); }
review_comments: Ethan Park: Overall this looks good; two questions: 1) how stable is the fp16 accumulation path on long sequences? 2) we need a clearer metric in telemetry for selection reversions.

Maya Singh (author): FP16 path has guard to fall back to fp32 when accumulation overflow detected; unit test added for 32k token synthetic to assert no NaNs. Added selection_reversion_count metric and exposed in selection_events.
Sofia Ramirez: Could you add a small note in the docs about recommended KV_STAGED_WINDOW for Dedicated vs Hosted? Length of default felt tuned for large pools.
Maya Singh: Added docs entry and example per-cluster recommendation; default remains conservative and can be overridden per deployment.
Liam O'Connor: CI flake on cuda11.8 matrix, reran and passed; good to squash merge once e2e looks green.
source.release_notes: Introduces heterogeneous attention kernel selection with staged scheduler and continuous batching support. Improves p99 and throughput for mixed-sequence workloads. Feature-flagged; not a public API change. See docs/runtime/tuning/hetero_attn.md for tuning guidance.

text: Motivation: reduce p99 latency regression for mixed-sequence workloads and improve throughput for production chat and retrieval runtimes by selecting optimal attention kernels at runtime and enabling continuous batching without sacrificing KV cache hit stability. Changes: Introduces a heterogeneous attention selection layer (HeteroAttn) that picks between a low-latency microkernel and a high-throughput Flash-like kernel based on sequence length heuristics and current queueing pressure; adds a staged scheduler that enforces a short-term reservation window for latency-sensitive requests and a batch-aggregation window for throughput requests; implements a cooperative KV cache eviction policy (staged LRU) that works with continuous batching to avoid repeated cache thrash during rollups; adds fused rotary/rope offset precomputation paths that avoid memcpy for common rotary patterns; integrates fp16 accumulation path with optional fp32 fallback for edge-case stability; exposes runtime knobs: ATTEN_SELECT_WINDOW_MS, KV_STAGED_WINDOW, CBATCH_MAX_WAIT_MS for tuned rollouts. Testing and validation: microbenchmarked on c6i-gpu clusters and internal perf-lab; added synthetic mixed-sequence workload harness and end-to-end chat replay traces. Results: median latency improved 6-9% on mid-sized seqs, p99 reduced up to 22% for mixed workloads, throughput increased 12% on long-sequence tails while KV miss rate decreased 18% under continuous batching. Rollout plan: staged canary on dedicated pool alpha -> 50% traffic after 72h, automatic rollback on p99 regressions >10% or error rate increase >0.5%. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. Known limitations: rare fp16 accumulation differences at extreme sequence lengths (documented and guarded by runtime flag), scheduler parameters tuned for our standard clusters; customers on small on-prem may require adjustments. Checklist: - [x] Benchmarks added to perf-lab harness - [x] Unit + integration tests for scheduler and KV policy - [x] Feature-flagged rollout - [x] Docs entry in runtime tuning guide - [x] Telemetry events for selection decisions

source.title: introduce-auth-tactician-ttl-prefetch-and-pullstream-refactor
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: Context: Cross-SDK follow-up to earlier session/lease work — introduces an "auth tactician" component that centralizes token TTL awareness and a small prefetch policy to avoid tail latency for streamed pull/push APIs. Motivation: customers saw intermittent ~200-400ms spikes when long-running streams triggered last-second token refreshes; retry/backoff logic masked this but increased request churn and observable noise. Goal: reduce tail latency by proactively warming credentials within a TTL window, provide a small, well-documented opt-in prefetch policy across Python/TS/Go SDKs, and clean up PullStream ergonomics so the language SDKs expose a common surface for cancellable/asynchronous consumption. What I changed: 1) auth/tactician: new minimal stateful component (Python: redwood_sdk.session.tactician, TS: auth/tactician.ts, Go: session/tactician.go) that tracks token TTLs, schedules a single prefetch per session when TTL < configured window, and exposes hooks for the SDK session factories to opt into warm-up; 2) prefetch policy: defaults to disabled for backwards compatibility, opt-in via SDK session config as prefetch_window_ms and prefetch_enabled; adds telemetry events (tactician.prefetch.scheduled, tactician.prefetch.success, tactician.prefetch.error); 3) PullStream refactor: unified pullstream interface name changes (Python: PullAsync -> PullStreamAsync, TS: PullStream class tidy, Go: PullIterator improvements) and clarified cancellation semantics in all three SDKs; 4) retries interplay: small change to retry predicate so a refresh in-flight counts as a non-retryable transient state for a single attempt to avoid double-refresh races; 5) docs and examples: migration guide, prefetch demo, and updated version notes for Python/TS/Go SDK releases. Compatibility notes: all changes are opt-in and default behavior is preserved; SDK minor bumps included to expose the new options (py 2.7.0, ts 1.18.0, go 1.5.0). Testing: unit tests around tactician scheduling and backoff, integration tests that run a long-lived PullStream while forcing token TTL expiry, and end-to-end scenario against a staging control-plane that simulates delayed token issuance. Rollout plan: feature flagged server-side and SDK flags default off; enable in canary customers after 2 weeks smoke. Checklist: [x] add tactician implementations across three SDKs [x] add telemetry and docs [x] add integration tests [x] add version bumps and changelogs [x] run cross-SDK regression job
source.commits: initial: add tactician interface and python impl; ts: implement tactician + expose prefetch config; go: session tactician + iterator changes; docs: add prefetch guide and examples; tests: integration test for stream w/ forced ttl expiry; fix: reconcile type names and update changelogs
source.review_thread: Aisha: +1 on overall approach; can we keep prefetch disabled by default? Also request clearer metric names for integration with Console. Ethan: Prefetch default is false; added telemetry prefixes tactician.prefetch.* and included mapping in docs. Luca: I think the TS PullStream rename might be breaking for some consumers; would prefer an alias export to maintain ergonomics. Ethan: Added export alias PullAsync -> PullStream in TS package entrypoint to preserve import paths. Riley: go iterator change looks good but please add comment about thread-safety of the tactician scheduling state. Ethan: Added sync.Mutex guard and a unit test simulating concurrent Close/Refresh. Aisha: CI flaked once on the e2e test due to timeouts. Ethan: increased test timeout and added retry logic in the harness; reran CI. Final: approvals from Aisha and Luca; Riley left an approving comment after the mutex test landed.
source.release_notes: Added an opt-in auth 'tactician' that can prefetch credentials shortly before expiry to reduce stream-tail latency. New SDK config: prefetch_enabled (bool), prefetch_window_ms (int). PullStream naming unified across SDKs; TS maintains backward-compatible exports. Minor SDK bumps: py 2.7.0, ts 1.18.0, go 1.5.0.
source.notes_for_release_manager: This change is behind SDK and server-side feature flags. Do not enable globally until smoke tests for canary customers pass. Coordinate coordinated release: publish SDKs (py/ts/go) in same day and update Console mapping for tactician telemetry.
source.post_merge_tasks: publish python wheel, npm publish typescript package, go module tag, update SDK samples repo, enable flag for 5% canary, monitor tactician.prefetch.* metrics for error rates
source.ci_log_summary: CI: lint/format OK; unit tests PASS; integration tests flaky first run (timeout) then PASS after timeout bump; e2e staging puzzle: one run had 502 from staging auth service simulated delay, rerun passed.
source.testing_summary: Unit coverage: tactician scheduling, retry predicate interaction, pullstream cancellation; Integration: simulated TTL expiry while a long-lived stream is open; End-to-end: validated streaming latency tail reduction in staging with prefetch_enabled=true (median tail reduced from 320ms to 60ms in controlled run).
source.priority_notes: Opt-in feature; prioritized for SRE/Platform customers reporting stream tail spikes. Not a breaking change but version bumps required to access new flags.
source.example_migration_snippet: # Python session config example: session = Session(prefetch_enabled=True, prefetch_window_ms=15000)\n# TS example: new Session({ prefetchEnabled: true, prefetchWindowMs: 15000 })\n# Go example: sessioncfg := session.Config{PrefetchEnabled: true, PrefetchWindowMs: 15_000}

source.title: Tokenwise scale-lowering and ring-buffer collation for quantized kernels
source.author: Marina Alvarez
source.release_notes:
- Introduces a tokenwise scale-lowering pass for INT8/FP8 execution paths that reduces transient requant/dequant overheads by ~12-18% on common LLM workloads.
- Adds a ring-buffer collation layer for KV-cache request slices that coalesces contiguous token writes and reduces kernel launch stalls.
- Backward-compatible: no API surface changes; runtime behavior gated behind feature flag runtime.quant.scale_lowering (off by default).
source.description:
Summary: Adds a tokenwise scale-lowering compiler pass and a ring-buffer collation stage in the runtime to reduce per-token quant/dequant churn and to coalesce KV cache writes from continuous-batching. Motivation: some workloads with short prefixes and frequent small continuations were suffering from per-token scale conversions and tight kernel launches that prevented microbatch coalescing on INT8 and FP8 execution paths. This PR targets the hot path by lowering scale operations earlier and grouping adjacent KV writes into a ring-backed collator so the GPU-side kernel sees larger contiguous work items without changing model numerics.

What changed (high-level):
- New compiler pass runtime/quant/scale_lowering.cc that detects consecutive token-scale operations and emits a lowered tokenwise scale applied as a fused kernel input where safe. The pass is careful to preserve per-head scaling semantics and falls back to explicit dequant when quantization boundaries are ambiguous.
- Ring-buffer collation runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.cc which accumulates KV cache writes from the continuous-batching queue and emits coalesced write batches to the kernel backend. Collation is implemented lock-free per-GPU-stream and supports configurable watermark/latency tradeoffs.
- Cache eviction hook runtime/cache/evictor.h to ensure collated segments respect existing per-shard eviction windows and do not hold stale KV pointers across rollouts.
- Microbench harness bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py and a small integration test tests/quant/scale_lowering_test.cc that validate numerical parity against the baseline within tolerated quantization noise.
- Docs: docs/perf/quant-collation.md describing knobs, feature flags, and recommended settings for Dedicated and Private deployments.

Commits (summary):
- feat(quant): introduce tokenwise scale-lowering pass (Marina Alvarez)
- perf(scheduler): add ring buffer collator and watermark policy (Marina Alvarez)
- fix(tests): stabilize scale-lowering unit tests and edge-case fallbacks (Marina Alvarez)
- bench: add token collation microbench and CI benchmark job (Marina Alvarez)
- docs: usage and rollout guide for quant collation (Marina Alvarez)

Files touched (representative):
- runtime/quant/scale_lowering.cc, runtime/quant/scale_lowering.h
- runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.cc, runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.h
- runtime/cache/evictor.h
- bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py
- tests/quant/scale_lowering_test.cc
- docs/perf/quant-collation.md

Design notes and tradeoffs:
- Numeric safety: the scale-lowering pass applies a conservative analysis to ensure per-head and per-token scale semantics are preserved. Where the analysis is inconclusive (eg. custom layer-scale hooks or non-standard quant tables), the pass emits an explicit dequant/requant path to preserve correctness. The feature is gated by runtime.quant.scale_lowering and runtime.quant.enable_collation flags.
- Latency vs throughput: ring collator uses a watermark delay (default 1.5ms) to collect contiguous KV writes; on low-concurrency workloads customers can reduce or disable collation to prioritize single-request latency.
- Memory: ring-buffer allocates an ephemeral per-stream buffer sized by max_batch_tokens which defaults to 8k tokens per GPU. This is configurable and documented.

Benchmarks:
- Microbench on A100 (seq_len 2048, batch 8, generation 128): throughput +15% for INT8 end-to-end token/s compared to baseline quant path; median tail latency (p95) increased <2% with default watermark.
- Real workload sim (chat-like prefixes with many small continuations): median tokens/sec +18% and average GPU kernel launches reduced by ~22%. Detailed bench outputs attached in PR artifacts.

Review conversation (high level):
- Daniel Kim (review): "Nice win. Can you add a small test covering per-head scales and the mixed-precision fallback? Also please verify this doesn't regress KV streaming on Dedicated low-memory instances."
- Author reply: "Added per-head unit test and an explicit Dedicated config run in CI. Evictor hook added to avoid stale pointer windows."
- Sofia Nguyen (review): "Please document the feature flags and safe rollout steps; add a metrics counter for collated_batches and collated_tokens."
- Author reply: "Docs added and two counters (quant.collation.batches, quant.collation.tokens) exposed to console and Prometheus."
- Ethan Park (approval): "Bench numbers look solid; tests are green. Recommend squash-merge after CI smoke passes."

CI and validation:
- Unit tests: pass
- Integration (scale path): pass
- Bench job: artifacts show throughput improvements; bench job marked as informational in CI (not gating).
- Lint/format: pass

Rollout plan:
1) Merge behind feature flag off by default.
2) Canary on small Dedicated pool (internal tag: canary-quant-collation) with metrics for collated_batches and kernel_launch_count.
3) Gradual ramp with health checks for tail latency and model-quality regressions (Linear ENG-9273 monitors).

Related: Linear ENG-9273 (quant perf initiative), ENG-9410 (kv cache coherency), SUP-1891 (customer report of small-continuation latency).

Notes for maintainers:
- If future work expands the pass to span inter-layer fusions, ensure the analysis preserves custom kernel hooks (eg. fused FusedFFN variants).
- The ring collator intentionally avoids reordering across tokens belonging to different request IDs; do not change that invariant without consensus.

Merge outcome: Squash-merged after green CI and two approvals.

If you want to test locally: see docs/perf/quant-collation.md and run bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py with --gpu-streams and --watermark-ms to reproduce microbench numbers.

source.title: adaptive-kernel-selector-and-prefetch-degradation-policy
source.author: Leah Morgan
source.description:
Motivation: recent incidents showed increased p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched kernels aggressively for short sequences and simultaneously attempted speculative KV prefetching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel selection policy plus a controlled prefetch degradation path that trades a small amount of throughput for deterministic correctness and lower tail latency under mixed workloads.

What this does (single-string description): Adds a KernelSelector that scores candidate kernels by estimated latency, numerical-safety score, and cache-friendliness; introduces a fallback budget token that bounds how often the fast path can be taken per model+device pair; implements a PrefetchDegradationPolicy that steps down speculative KV prefetch aggressiveness when p95/p99 latency or error-rate thresholds are exceeded; exposes a runtime flag --safe-mode to force conservative kernel and disable micro-prefetch heuristics for regulated workloads. The change coordinates with the continuous-batching layer to emit per-batch scoring metadata so the scheduler can avoid mixing high-risk fastpath batches with conservative ones.

Commits summary: compact, focused commits: 1) kernel selector core + scoring table (kernel_selector.rs, kernel_profiles.json), 2) fallback budget token implementation and metrics, 3) kv prefetch policy and prewarm adjustments (kv/prefetch.rs), 4) scheduler integration (scheduler/batch_manager.rs), 5) unit + microbench + e2e regression tests, 6) docs and changelog.

Files (high-level): runtime/kernel_selector.rs, runtime/kernel_profiles.json, scheduler/batch_manager.rs, kv/prefetch.rs, kv/cache_metrics.rs, serving/continuous_batching.rs, tests/perf/test_kernel_selector.py, tests/e2e/prefetch_degradation.yaml, docs/runtime/fallbacks.md, .github/workflows/perf.yml. Small illustrative snippets are included in the diff where helpful (see kernel selection scoring in runtime/kernel_selector.rs and the degradation thresholds in kv/prefetch.rs).

Performance notes: microbenchmarks on our internal perf-lab (A100 and T4 pools): adaptive selector reduces bad-kernel mispicks by 62% on mixed-length traces; p99 CPU-side tail reduced 18% on A100 with minimal p50 impact. Prefetch degradation cut memory thrash in KV-heavy workloads and removed a class of correctness-sensitive race where prefetch + rapid cache eviction led to stale KV lookups; we instrumented and validated by adding deterministic KV roundtrip tests.

Safety/correctness: numeric-safety score favours kernels with proven fp16/quantized stability for the model; the fallback budget ensures we do not silently oscillate between kernels (avoids thrash). The --safe-mode flag forces: 1) kernel policy to 'conservative', 2) prefetch disabled, 3) strict KV validation on every cache hit (adds latency but improves determinism). Documentation in docs/runtime/fallbacks.md explains when to use safe-mode (regulated/enterprise customers, incident mitigation).

Testing: added unit tests for kernel scoring edge cases, microbench harness to simulate mixed sequence-length streams, and an end-to-end scenario that reproduced the original incident locally. CI matrix covers x86 A100/T4 runners; a flaky perf test (tests/perf/test_batching_latency.py) initially failed and was rerun once. All checks passed on final run: build, lint, unit, microbench, and e2e regression.

Review thread (condensed into single string):
- Ava Chen (review): "Can we add a telemetric signal for fallback-budget depletion? Want to ensure operators see throttling events."
- Leah (author): "Added metrics: runtime.fallback_budget.used and runtime.fallback_budget.reset_count; documented in metrics.md."
- Miguel Torres (review): "Concerned about memory overhead of prefetch accounting on large-model KV, suggest bounded history window."
- Leah: "Implemented ring-buffer accounting with configurable window; default 128 entries, configurable via env VAR."
- Priya Nair (review): "Please justify safe-mode defaults and add opt-in threshold tuning."
- Leah: "Added configuration docs and conservative defaults that mirror existing SLA targets; included a small utility to translate latency SLOs to prefetch thresholds."
- Final: Approvals from Ava and Priya after addressing comments; Miguel requested a follow-up for telemetry retention, tracked in ENG-4210.

CI summary: build ✓, lint ✓, unit tests ✓, microbench ✓, e2e regression ✓ (one flaky perf test rerun). Post-merge: perf dashboards updated automatically via perf-lab webhook to include runtime.fallback_budget.* metrics.

Release notes: "runtime: add adaptive kernel selection and KV prefetch degradation policy to reduce tail latency and provide a --safe-mode for deterministic deployments. See docs/runtime/fallbacks.md for configuration and operational guidance."

Merge: Squash-merged to main after approvals and CI green. Follow-ups: ENG-4210 (telemetry retention), ENG-4299 (experiment: dynamic fallback budget tuning via RL controller).

title: adaptive-kernel-selector-and-prefetch-degradation-policy
preview:
Motivation: recent incidents showed increased p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched kernels aggressively for short sequences and simultaneously attempted speculative KV prefetching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel selection policy plus a controlled prefetch degradation path that trades a small amount of throughput for deterministic correctness and lower tail latency under mixed workloads.

What this does (single-string description): Adds a KernelSelector that scores candidate kernels by estimated latency, numerical-safety score, and cache-friendliness; introduces a fallback budget token that bounds how often the fast path can be taken per model+device pair; implements a PrefetchDegradationPolicy that steps down speculative KV prefetch aggressiveness when p95/p99 latency or error-rate thresholds are exceeded; exposes a runtime flag --safe-mode to force conservative kernel and disable micro-prefetch heuristics for regulated workloads. The change coordinates with the continuous-batching layer to emit per-batch scoring metadata so the scheduler can avoid mixing high-risk fastpath batches with conservative ones.

source.title: kv-prefetch-aware-batching-with-sparse-scratch-allocator
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Context: In production workloads with long chat histories the KV cache dominates transient GPU workspace allocations and forces arena growth during batching spikes. Motivation: Reduce peak GPU footprint while preserving latency SLOs and steady-state throughput. Summary: Introduces a KV-prefetch hotset buffer and a sparse scratch allocator to avoid inflating the global arena during continuous batching. The hotset prefetcher tracks request-level KV hotness and pre-populates a compact per-device hotset capable of serving the majority of cache hits without expanding the scratch arena. The sparse scratch allocator exposes a lightweight free-list for short-lived activation fragments and returns large contiguous ranges back to the arena quickly, reducing fragmentation. Key changes: 1) runtime/kv: prefetch_hotset component with heuristics for prefix/predictive prefetch and bounded memory cap. 2) runtime/allocator: sparse_scratch_allocator that coexists with existing arena and supports deferred materialization for checkpointed activations. 3) scheduler: integrate prefetch decisions into continuous_batcher so requests in the same batch can share prefetched KV and avoid duplicate allocations. 4) observability: new metrics for kv_hotset_hit_ratio, scratch_frag_pct, and prefetch_evictions, plus a dashboard. Results: in internal LLaMA-2-13B quantized chat trace we saw peak GPU RSS drop ~18% (24.6GB -> 20.1GB), 95th latency unchanged within noise, throughput +3% under steady batching, and per-token cost reduction ~12%. Compatibility: falls back to existing arena+kv behavior when hotset cap is exceeded or prefetch disabled. Rollout: feature-flagged, opt-in per-customer; staged rollout with smoke tests and perf canary on dedicated pools. Checklist: [x] unit tests, [x] integration tests, [x] perf regression run, [x] docs, [x] observability. Related: ENG-863 describes requirements for KV costing and ENG-874 is follow-up for multi-GPU hotset federated eviction.
commits: a1c4b9e Add sparse_scratch_allocator implementation and unit tests
b7f3de2 Introduce kv prefetch hotset data structures and cap logic
d2f1a6e Integrate prefetch into continuous_batcher and add sharing logic
e6a4fab Add metrics and dashboard panels for kv_hotset and scratch fragmentation
f9b2c1d Fix race condition in hotset eviction and multi-GPU sync
0a3d55b Update docs and benchmark harness
review_comments: Ethan Park (2025-11-03): Nice decomposition. Two asks: add a stress test for prefetch backpressure and make hotset cap an explicit config parameter at runtime. Maya: added cap flag and stress test in continuous_batcher_test.cc (see commits).

Luca Moretti (2025-11-05): How does this interact with activation checkpointing and deferred materialization? Suggest adding a note in allocator to avoid double-free of deferred fragments. Maya: added guard and unit test covering checkpointed activations.

Aisha Rahman (2025-11-07): Observability looks good. Can we emit a synthetic prefetch in the perf harness to detect regressions? Maya: added benchmark variant that toggles prefetch and records hit ratio.
ci_checks: integration-tests: flaky on first run -> rerun -> pass
perf-regression-check: pass (no regression on canonical traces)
source.merge_details: Merged via squash on 2025-11-10 by Aisha Rahman. Merge commit message: "kv-prefetch-aware-batching + sparse scratch allocator: reduce peak GPU memory and stabilize arena growth. Closes ENG-863."
source.release_notes: Introduce KV prefetch hotset and sparse scratch allocator. Peak GPU memory reduced ~18% on targeted workloads. Feature is behind runtime feature flag 'kv_prefetch_hotset' and can be toggled per-deployment. No breaking changes expected; falls back to previous behavior when disabled.
metrics_summary: Per-token cost: -12% (measured on quantized LLaMA-2-13B trace)
source.testing_summary: Unit tests added for allocator and hotset eviction. Integration tests exercise prefetch across batch boundaries. Perf harness includes a regression profile comparing baseline vs prefetched runs. Benchmarks executed on dedicated gpu-canary pool; canary passed.
risk_and_mitigation: Risk: prefetch may increase memory if mis-tuned. Mitigation: hard cap config and auto-disable when prefetch_evictions spike.
Risk: interaction with multi-GPU kv sharding may cause duplicate prefetches. Mitigation: guarded sync path and eviction tokens to de-duplicate.
Risk: allocator fragmentation regressions. Mitigation: allocator exposes diagnostic metrics and fast fallback to global arena.
source.rollout_plan: Feature is behind 'kv_prefetch_hotset' flag. Stage 1: internal canary on dedicated pool (5 servers) for 48h. Stage 2: opt-in customer beta with contractual workloads. Stage 3: gradual enable on non-sensitive hosted traffic with traffic steering and perf alarms. Full rollout requires KPI: kv_hotset_hit_ratio>0.6 and no latency SLO violations for 24h.

source.title: introduce-tenant-kms-binding-enforcer-and-audit-linker
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Enforce tenant-scoped KMS bindings at the service operator level and add an audit correlation proxy that ties KMS operations to tenant/namespace claims. Backwards-compatible rollout with policy flag.
source.description: Motivation: We need a stronger guardrail that prevents cross-tenant KMS usage in multi-tenant Dedicated/Private deployments while preserving operator ergonomics and auditability. This PR implements a runtime enforcer that ensures KMS key bindings are validated against the request's tenant/namespace claims before allowing encrypt/decrypt/sign operations. It also introduces an audit linker service that enriches existing audit events with KMS provenance (key id, provider, binding-check-result) and a correlation id that ties application requests to KMS calls for post-incident investigation. Design summary: (1) policy-operator plugin that evaluates declared kms.bindings for a tenant namespace and caches validated bindings in a short-lived policy store; (2) request-time gate in the kms-proxy that checks the caller's tenant claim against the policy store and denies calls that violate bindings; (3) audit-linker sidecar that subscribes to internal audit-stream, joins KMS provenance, and emits correlated audit events to the audit-aggregator with a new field audit.kms_correlation_id; (4) feature-flagged rollout behind operator.feature.flags. This description includes a small checklist and rollout plan. Checklist: - [x] policy schema addition and unit tests - [x] kms-proxy binding checks and integration tests - [x] audit-linker implementation and end-to-end tests - [x] migration to grace period for namespaces with no bindings - [x] docs for admin operators and Linear tickets linked. Testing: unit tests for policy compiler, e2e harness using local kms-mock, chaos run for denial scenarios, and load test to ensure latency added is < 3ms p50 for kms-proxy path. Rollout: staged canary (10% traffic) for 4h, 50% for 12h, 100% for 24h; monitor audit link failures, KMS-deny rates, and latency SLOs. Backout: flip feature flag off to revert enforcement; audit-linker safe-mode preserves original audit stream when disabled. Related: ENG-3421 implements the policy schema; SEC-78 is the security review. Links: linear/ENG-3421, security/SEC-78. Notes: No public API changes. Operators will need to declare kms.bindings for existing namespaces or opt into a grace period to avoid sudden denials.
commits: feat(policy): add tenant kms.binding schema and compiler (Maya Chen)
feat(kms-proxy): enforce binding check before KMS calls and add metrics (Ethan Park)
feat(audit-linker): implement correlation enrichment and output mapping (M. Chen)
test: add e2e tests with kms-mock and policy fixtures (R. Gomez)
docs: admin guide for declaring kms.bindings and migration notes (Anita Singh)
chore: feature flag wiring and staged rollout script
review_comments: Rafael Gomez: Can we avoid a hot-path cache miss causing a KMS call to be delayed? Suggest pre-warming cache on token mint and adding an async refresh. Reply (Maya): Added pre-warm on token mint and async refresh; details in policy/compiler/kms_bindings.go; added prometheus metric kms_policy_cache_miss_count.

Anita Singh: Audit schema change looks good but we should version the audit event to avoid downstream parsers breaking. Reply (Maya): Audit linker emits audit.kms_correlation_v1 and we added migration note in docs; agreed on versioning.

Ethan Park: Load test shows ~2.2ms additional latency p50 and ~4.8ms p95 in kms-proxy under 500rps. Can we add circuit-breaker to fallback to deny safely under overload? Reply (Maya): Added circuit-breaker that defaults to deny with tagged audit reason 'enforcer_overload'. Updated tests and rollout notes.
source.notes_on_rollout: Canary rollout via deploy/scripts/staged-kms-enforcer.sh that updates feature flag for target namespaces. Monitoring: alert on audit.linker_errors > 1% over 15m, kms.enforcement_denies spike, or p95 latency regressions. Operators must add kms.bindings in namespace CRD or enable grace_mode=true for 72h to migrate.
post_merge_actions: Deploy audit-linker as sidecar in audit-aggregator cluster
Coordinate with infra to seed kms bindings for top 50 tenants
Create dashboard for kms_enforcement_metrics and audit.kms_correlation rate
